dimension: Electron threat model (main process, preload contextBridge, webview, CSP, IPC path validation)
summary: The baseline hardening is present and better than most AI-built Electron apps: contextIsolation + sandbox + nodeIntegration:false on the main window, a typed contextBridge surface (no raw ipcRenderer leak), UUID validation on most IPC args, and a webview navigation allowlist. But the threat model has serious holes that matter for shipping to 20 non-technical users. The two worst are structural: (1) the CSP is registered only on session.defaultSession, yet the production build runs the privileged renderer AND the remote-content webview in the 'persist:powerbi-viewer' partition session — so the shipped app enforces NO Content-Security-Policy at all; and (2) the preload exposes auth.getAccessToken(), handing the raw Power BI bearer token to a renderer that also hosts remote app.powerbi.com content via webview, making token theft a one-XSS-away event. The main window has a will-navigate guard but no setWindowOpenHandler, and the export path validator is renderer-trusted with an over-broad allowed root (the entire user profile). Fix the CSP-session mismatch and remove getAccessToken from the bridge before Monday.

findings.title: CSP is applied only to defaultSession; the production partition session has no CSP
findings.description: The Content-Security-Policy is installed via session.defaultSession.webRequest.onHeadersReceived (index.ts:105-114). But the main BrowserWindow uses webPreferences.partition = PARTITION_NAME ('persist:powerbi-viewer') in production (index.ts:61), and the webview inherits that same partition (AppViewer.tsx:193). Responses served to a partitioned session do NOT pass through defaultSession's onHeadersReceived handler. Therefore in the shipped (packaged) build, NO CSP header is injected into the renderer that carries the privileged preload, nor into the webview loading app.powerbi.com. The CSP only works in dev mode, where partition is undefined and defaultSession is used. This is a silent, build-mode-dependent failure: it 'works' when the developer tests with npm run dev and disappears in the artifact users receive.
findings.evidence: src/main/index.ts:61 (partition: isDev ? undefined : PARTITION_NAME), :105 (session.defaultSession.webRequest.onHeadersReceived), :110 (the CSP string); src/renderer/components/viewer/AppViewer.tsx:193 (partition={partitionName||undefined}); src/shared/constants.ts:6 (PARTITION_NAME='persist:powerbi-viewer')
findings.impact: The production app ships with zero CSP on the window that holds the access-token-bearing preload bridge. Any injected script (malicious dependency, supply-chain compromise of the 27 vulnerable npm packages, or remote content that escapes the webview) runs without script-src/connect-src restriction and can call window.electronAPI.auth.getAccessToken() and POST the token anywhere. The defense the author believed was active is inert for all 20 users.
findings.recommendation: Register the onHeadersReceived CSP handler on the SAME session the windows use: session.fromPartition(PARTITION_NAME) in production (and defaultSession in dev), or register on both. Verify by inspecting response headers in the packaged build, not dev. Add object-src 'none', base-uri 'self', and frame-ancestors 'none' to the policy.

verification.reasoning: Opened all cited files and confirmed every claim. index.ts:61 sets partition: isDev ? undefined : PARTITION_NAME, so production binds the main BrowserWindow to the named session persist:powerbi-viewer while dev uses defaultSession. index.ts:105-114 registers the CSP onHeadersReceived handler on session.defaultSession only — a Grep across src/ confirms this is the ONLY CSP registration; there is no session.fromPartition(...).webRequest handler anywhere, and no per-window registration on the partitioned session. AppViewer.tsx:193 sets partition={partitionName||undefined}, and the app:get-partition-name IPC (index.ts:605-609) returns PARTITION_NAME in production, so the webview also runs outside defaultSession. I tried to refute via a <meta> CSP fallback in src/renderer/index.html, but line 6 explicitly comments that CSP is applied via session.webRequest in production and there is no meta CSP tag — so the privileged preload renderer (loaded via loadFile from file://) ships with no CSP at all in packaged builds. Electron webRequest listeners are per-session and a defaultSession listener provably does not intercept responses for a different partition's session, so the handler is inert for both the preload-bearing window and the webview in production. This is a genuine build-mode-dependent failure: CSP is active under npm run dev and silently absent in the shipped artifact.
verification.notes: Downgraded from critical to high. The defect is real and exactly as described, but CSP is a defense-in-depth control: exploitation still requires a prior script-injection foothold (malicious dependency / webview escape). Its absence removes the script-src/connect-src barrier that would otherwise blunt token exfiltration via window.electronAPI.auth.getAccessToken, but it is not by itself a directly exploitable vulnerability. Fix: register onHeadersReceived on session.fromPartition(PARTITION_NAME) (in production) in addition to / instead of defaultSession, or add a <meta http-equiv=Content-Security-Policy> to renderer/index.html as a fallback. Note the webview loading app.powerbi.com would need frame/connect allowances regardless.

findings.title: Raw Power BI bearer access token is handed to the renderer via contextBridge
findings.description: preload exposes auth.getAccessToken() (preload/index.ts:13) which calls the 'auth:get-token' IPC handler (index.ts:220) returning authService.getAccessToken() — the raw OAuth access token. getEmbedToken (powerbi-api.ts:354-382) likewise returns the same raw access token as the 'embed token' (user-owns-data). So the full-scope AAD access token lives in renderer-reachable JavaScript. The same renderer tree renders a <webview> pointed at remote app.powerbi.com (AppViewer.tsx:185-195). With the CSP gap above, there is no connect-src restriction to stop exfiltration. Tokens should stay in the main process; the renderer should never hold a bearer token it can read or transmit.
findings.evidence: src/preload/index.ts:13 (getAccessToken), src/main/index.ts:220 (ipcMain.handle('auth:get-token')), src/main/services/powerbi-api.ts:367-374 (returns tokenResponse.data as embed token), src/renderer/components/viewer/AppViewer.tsx:185
findings.impact: A single XSS or malicious script in the renderer (or a compromised transitive dependency executing at load) can read a live, full-scope Power BI/Graph access token and POST it to an attacker server, granting the attacker the user's Power BI data access for the token lifetime (~1h, silently renewable while the app runs). For 20 non-technical users this is a tenant-wide data-exposure risk from one client compromise.
findings.recommendation: Remove auth.getAccessToken from the contextBridge surface entirely. Keep all token use in the main process (the API service already runs there). If the renderer's powerbi-client embed genuinely needs a token, mint a scoped, short-lived Power BI EMBED token (app-owns-data GenerateToken) rather than handing out the AAD access token, and pass only that. Audit every renderer call site of getAccessToken/getEmbedToken.

verification.reasoning: I opened all four cited files. The mechanical facts are accurate: preload/index.ts:13 exposes auth.getAccessToken(); main/index.ts:220 handles auth:get-token by returning authService.getAccessToken() (raw token); powerbi-api.ts:354-382 (getEmbedToken) explicitly returns the raw access token as data.token for the user-owns-data scenario; and the renderer does hold it in plain JS — ReportViewer.tsx:137 reads tokenResponse.data.token and passes it to powerbi-client setAccessToken() (same pattern in DashboardViewer.tsx:167 and PresentationMode.tsx:451). A <webview> to app.powerbi.com is rendered in the same renderer tree (AppViewer.tsx:185). So the renderer genuinely holds a full-scope AAD bearer token. HOWEVER, the claim's stated CRITICAL impact depends on its premise: "With the CSP gap above, there is no connect-src restriction to stop exfiltration." That premise is FALSE in the cited code. The actual CSP at index.ts:109-111 sets connect-src https://api.powerbi.com https://login.microsoftonline.com (no wildcard) AND script-src 'self'. The connect-src directive specifically blocks the described attack ("POST it to an attacker server") via fetch/XHR/WebSocket, and script-src 'self' substantially mitigates the XSS injection vector that the exfiltration chain requires. The "CSP gap above" is a separate finding not included in this finding's own evidence, so it cannot be assumed here. With contextIsolation:true, sandbox:true, nodeIntegration:false also confirmed (index.ts:63-65), the renderer is hardened. The architectural observation is legitimate (a full-scope user token in the renderer is a real defense-in-depth weakness, and scoped embed tokens would be better), but for a user-owns-data app the powerbi-client embed fundamentally requires the token in the renderer, and the specific tenant-wide-exfiltration-from-one-XSS impact is materially blocked by the connect-src + script-src directives that the claim asserts are absent.
verification.notes: Cited facts about token exposure are all correct. Downgraded from critical because the claimed exfiltration impact rests on a non-existent CSP gap: connect-src is restricted to api.powerbi.com/login.microsoftonline.com and script-src is 'self' (index.ts:109-111), which block the described attack. Remaining real concern is architectural defense-in-depth (raw full-scope token in renderer vs scoped embed token), inherent to the user-owns-data powerbi-client design.

findings.title: Main window has no setWindowOpenHandler; window.open creates an unconstrained child window
findings.description: The web-contents-created handler installs setWindowOpenHandler AND the navigation allowlist ONLY when contents.getType() === 'webview' (index.ts:141-167). The main window gets a will-navigate guard (index.ts:87-91) but NO setWindowOpenHandler. In Electron, window.open() / target=_blank / a renderer-initiated popup is governed by setWindowOpenHandler, not will-navigate. With no handler, the default action ('allow') creates a brand-new BrowserWindow whose webPreferences are NOT inherited from the parent's hardened config and which can load an arbitrary URL.
findings.evidence: src/main/index.ts:139-168 (guard gated on getType()==='webview'), :87-91 (main window will-navigate only). No app-level or main-window setWindowOpenHandler exists.
findings.impact: A script (or injected content) in the main renderer can spawn a new top-level window pointing at attacker-controlled https content. The new window is a fresh browsing context not covered by the will-navigate allowlist, and on older Electron defaults can be a route to a less-sandboxed context or a convincing phishing surface rendered as a native window of the trusted app.
findings.recommendation: Add a global app.on('web-contents-created') that calls setWindowOpenHandler returning { action: 'deny' } (then shell.openExternal for vetted https) for ALL contents, not just webviews, and remove the getType()==='webview' gate so the navigation allowlist also protects the main window's child windows.

verification.reasoning:
I read src/main/index.ts in full. The structural facts in the claim are accurate: setWindowOpenHandler appears exactly once (line 143), inside the block gated on contents.getType()==='webview' (line 141), so it does NOT apply to the main window. The main window gets only a will-navigate guard (lines 87-91), and in Electron will-navigate does not govern window.open/new-window creation — the default windowOpenHandler action is 'allow'. So the missing handler on the main window is a real defense-in-depth gap and worth fixing (Electron's own security checklist recommends returning {action:'deny'} on all webContents).

However, the claimed HIGH severity and escalation impact are overstated by the code: (1) The main window loads only the app's own bundled file:// content (line 83) under a strict CSP set at lines 109-110 (default-src 'self'; script-src 'self'). For the cited attack ('a script or injected content in the main renderer') to call window.open, an attacker must already have script execution in the main renderer, which script-src 'self' is specifically designed to prevent — i.e. the premise presupposes a prior XSS that would itself be the real bug. (2) The parent webPreferences have nodeIntegration:false and sandbox:true (lines 64-65). (3) package.json pins electron ^28.2.0, where child windows opened via window.open are sandboxed by default and do not inherit a Node-enabled context, so the claim's 'route to a less-sandboxed context' escalation does not hold (the claim itself only asserts this 'on older Electron defaults'). The worst realistic outcome is a popup rendering attacker https:// content as a native window — a phishing/UX concern, not RCE or privilege escalation. Legitimate hardening item, but not HIGH.
verification.notes: File: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\main\index.ts. Fix: add a global setWindowOpenHandler returning {action:'deny'} for non-webview contents (or set it on mainWindow.webContents in createWindow). Mitigating factors already present: strict CSP (script-src 'self'), nodeIntegration:false, sandbox:true, Electron 28.

findings.title: Export path validator trusts a renderer-supplied path and allows the entire user profile
findings.description: isValidExportPath (index.ts:27-38) is the only gate on where exportReportToPdf (index.ts:316) and current-view-pdf (index.ts:518) write files. The filePath comes straight from the renderer (preload exportReportToPdf param 5; export.currentViewToPdf options.filePath) — the trusted save dialog (export:choose-pdf-path) is a SEPARATE handler whose result the renderer is free to ignore and pass any path. The allowlist root includes os.homedir() itself, i.e. the ENTIRE user profile, so any path under %USERPROFILE% ending in .pdf passes. The startsWith(root + path.sep) check does not resolve Windows symlinks/junctions/8.3 short names, and Windows path semantics (alternate data streams, trailing dots/spaces, case folding) are not normalized beyond path.resolve + toLowerCase.
findings.evidence: src/main/index.ts:27-38 (validator), :316 (fs.writeFile(filePath, exportResponse.data) with renderer filePath), :518 (fs.writeFile(targetPath, pdfBuffer)); src/preload/index.ts:34-40 (filePath passed from renderer), :78-82 (options.filePath)
findings.impact: A compromised/injected renderer can write attacker-influenced bytes (the export buffer is server-returned content written verbatim) to almost anywhere in the user profile, e.g. %APPDATA%\Microsoft\Windows\Start Menu\Programs\Startup\x.pdf or overwriting existing .pdf files the user relies on. fs.writeFile silently overwrites. While the .pdf-extension constraint blocks dropping a .exe, profile-wide arbitrary .pdf write with no overwrite confirmation is a real integrity/abuse primitive and defeats the apparent 'user picks the path' security model.
findings.recommendation: Do not accept a filePath from the renderer at all. Have the main process own the save flow: show the dialog in the same handler and write only to the dialog-returned path. If a passed path must be supported, narrow allowed roots to Downloads/Desktop/Documents only (drop os.homedir()), resolve real paths (fs.realpath) before the prefix check, reject paths containing ':' beyond the drive and trailing dots/spaces, and write with flag 'wx' or prompt before overwrite.

verification.reasoning: I opened src/main/index.ts and src/preload/index.ts and confirmed the mechanical facts: isValidExportPath (lines 27-38) is the sole write gate, invoked at the report-export handler (validate line 306, write fs.writeFile(filePath, exportResponse.data) line 316) and at current-view-pdf (validate line 430, write fs.writeFile(targetPath, pdfBuffer) line 518). filePath/targetPath do come straight from the renderer via preload (content.exportReportToPdf param 5 line 39-40; export.currentViewToPdf options.filePath lines 78-82). export:choose-pdf-path (lines 321-346) is indeed a separate handler whose dialog result the renderer is free to ignore. The allowlist (line 33) includes os.homedir() itself, so any .pdf path anywhere under %USERPROFILE% passes, and the validator only does path.resolve + endsWith('.pdf').toLowerCase() with no symlink/junction/8.3/ADS normalization (line 28, 35-36). So the description is technically accurate. However the severity is overstated and the impact framing is partly wrong: (1) The entire primitive requires a COMPROMISED/INJECTED renderer — under normal operation the renderer runs trusted app code and only ever passes the dialog-chosen path, so there is no vulnerability absent a separate renderer-compromise bug. This is a defense-in-depth gap, not a standalone exploit. (2) The bytes written are not attacker-arbitrary: line 316 writes the Power BI server's export buffer and line 518 writes a PDF rendered from a screen capture of the app's own window — an attacker does not control these contents unless the upstream API or the app itself is already compromised. (3) The headline persistence example (Startup folder .pdf) is a non-threat: Windows does not execute .pdf files placed in the Startup folder, and the .pdf-extension constraint blocks dropping an .exe/.lnk. The residual real issue is: a compromised renderer could create/overwrite .pdf files anywhere under the user profile with no overwrite confirmation, plus weak Windows path normalization. That is a legitimate hardening item (the root should be narrowed from os.homedir() to downloads/desktop/documents, and overwrite/symlink handling tightened), but it is not a HIGH-severity production bug — it is a low/medium defense-in-depth weakness contingent on a prior renderer compromise.
verification.notes: Reasonable hardening: drop os.homedir() from allowedRoots (line 33) so the gate is downloads/desktop/documents only; consider fs.writeFile with flag 'wx' or an explicit overwrite confirmation, and reject paths whose realpath/lstat indicates a symlink/junction. None of this is urgent given it requires a renderer compromise and the written content is server/app-controlled, not attacker-arbitrary.

findings.title: webview relies on defaults: nodeintegration/webpreferences unset and allowpopups enabled
findings.description: The <webview> (AppViewer.tsx:185-195) loading remote https://app.powerbi.com sets src, partition, and allowpopups={true} but does NOT explicitly set nodeintegration=false, webpreferences (to pin contextIsolation/sandbox), or disablewebsecurity=false. Electron 28 defaults are safe (nodeIntegration off, contextIsolation on, web security on), so this is defense-in-depth rather than an active break — but it is fragile: any future change, or a webPreferences inheritance quirk, removes the only barrier between remote content and the host. allowpopups=true is on; popups are routed through setWindowOpenHandler (good) but only because the webview-typed guard happens to exist.
findings.evidence: src/renderer/components/viewer/AppViewer.tsx:185-195 (no nodeintegration/webpreferences/disablewebsecurity attributes; allowpopups={true})
findings.impact: Remote Power BI content is rendered in a webview whose isolation depends entirely on Electron's implicit defaults rather than explicit hardening. A regression (e.g., adding webpreferences with nodeIntegration) would expose Node to remote content. Low probability today, but it is remote content in a privileged desktop shell with no belt-and-suspenders.
findings.recommendation: Explicitly set on the webview: nodeintegration={false} (string 'false'/omit), webpreferences="contextIsolation=yes,sandbox=yes", and never set disablewebsecurity. Keep allowpopups only if the deny-by-default window-open handler is guaranteed for all contents (see main-window-no-window-open-handler).

findings.title: CSP allows style-src 'unsafe-inline' and omits object-src/base-uri/frame-ancestors
findings.description: Even where the CSP does apply (dev, or after the session fix), the policy at index.ts:110 uses style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' and lacks object-src 'none', base-uri 'self', and frame-ancestors 'none'. 'unsafe-inline' for styles enables CSS-based data exfiltration/UI-redress and is generally required only because of Fluent UI / Tailwind inline styles — it should be scoped or nonce'd. Missing base-uri allows a <base> injection to repoint relative URLs; missing object-src allows plugin/embed vectors.
findings.evidence: src/main/index.ts:110 ("...style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'; img-src 'self' data:" with no object-src/base-uri/frame-ancestors)
findings.impact: Weakens the CSP that is supposed to be the last line of defense for the privileged renderer. Combined with the exposed access token, any inline-style or base-tag injection vector becomes more exploitable. Lower severity only because the larger problem is that this CSP currently is not applied in production at all.
findings.recommendation: After fixing the session binding, tighten to: add object-src 'none'; base-uri 'self'; frame-ancestors 'none'; and replace style-src 'unsafe-inline' with nonces/hashes if the UI framework permits, or at minimum document why it is required. connect-src should also include any telemetry/CDN the app legitimately uses so tightening does not silently break features.

findings.title: OAuth popup window lacks sandbox and the redirect handler does not verify the state parameter
findings.description: openAuthWindow (auth-service.ts:220-228) creates the login BrowserWindow with nodeIntegration:false and contextIsolation:true but does NOT set sandbox:true, and has no preload — minor, but inconsistent with the main window's hardening. More notably, handleRedirect (auth-service.ts:232-243) extracts code and state from the localhost redirect and resolves with them, but the surrounding flow's CSRF protection depends on comparing the returned state to expectedState; openAuthWindow receives expectedState as a parameter yet handleRedirect never compares state === expectedState before resolving. If the comparison is not enforced by the caller, the OAuth state/CSRF check is effectively absent.
findings.evidence: src/main/auth/auth-service.ts:218 (openAuthWindow(authUrl, expectedState)), :224-227 (webPreferences without sandbox), :232-243 (handleRedirect resolves {code,state} without comparing to expectedState)
findings.impact: Auth popup runs with weaker isolation than the rest of the app while displaying a real Microsoft login. If the expectedState is never validated against the returned state, the app is open to OAuth authorization-code/CSRF injection where an attacker-supplied code is accepted. Needs confirmation of where state is compared downstream.
findings.recommendation: Set sandbox:true on the auth window. Enforce state === expectedState inside handleRedirect (reject and resolve(null) on mismatch) rather than relying on a downstream check; verify the code-exchange path compares state. Confirm PKCE is used by msal-node (it is by default for public clients) and that the redirect is matched on full URI not just hostname==='localhost'.

findings.title: getEmbedToken returns the full AAD access token mislabeled as a 1-hour embed token
findings.description: getEmbedToken (powerbi-api.ts:354-382) does not generate a scoped Power BI embed token; it returns authService.getAccessToken().data directly with a hardcoded expiration 'Date.now()+3600000' that is unrelated to the token's real expiry. This is exposed to the renderer through content.getEmbedToken (preload/index.ts:32). So a second renderer-reachable path leaks the same full-scope bearer token, and the renderer is told a wrong expiration it may rely on for refresh logic.
findings.evidence: src/main/services/powerbi-api.ts:367-374, src/preload/index.ts:32-33 (getEmbedToken exposed)
findings.impact: Reinforces the token-exposure finding: even if getAccessToken is removed from the bridge, getEmbedToken still leaks the same credential. The fabricated 1-hour expiry can cause the renderer to keep using or caching a token past/before its true validity, leading to silent auth failures for users mid-session.
findings.recommendation: Either remove getEmbedToken from the renderer bridge and do embedding via main-process-mediated calls, or implement a true app-owns-data GenerateToken returning a scoped embed token with the real expiration from the API response. Never return the raw access token under an 'embed token' name.

findings.title: current-view-pdf loads a large data: URL into a BrowserWindow with no CSP/navigation guard
findings.description: export:current-view-pdf (index.ts:477-518) creates a hidden BrowserWindow (contextIsolation:true, sandbox:true — good) and loads a data:text/html URL built by string-interpolating image dimensions and a base64 PNG (index.ts:488-509). The content is app-generated (a screenshot of the app itself), so injection risk is low, but the window has no CSP, no will-navigate/window-open guard, and the data URL is fully privileged. The numeric inputs (bounds/insets) are sanitized with Math.round/clamp, which is good.
findings.evidence: src/main/index.ts:477-518 (hidden window + data:text/html loadURL with interpolated html)
findings.impact: Minimal today because inputs are app-controlled and numeric. It is a latent pattern risk: if any user/remote-controlled string ever flows into that HTML template, it becomes script injection into a privileged window. Flagging for hygiene before the codebase grows.
findings.recommendation: Prefer loadFile with a static template + image written to a temp file, or set a strict CSP on this transient window and add a deny-all window-open handler. Confirm none of the interpolated values can ever be string-typed/user-controlled.

summary: The MSAL implementation gets the headline security controls right (PKCE S256, CSRF state validation, safeStorage encryption, host-allowlisted auth window, PII logging disabled), but it is riddled with lifecycle and resilience defects that will generate a steady stream of support tickets across a 20-user non-technical rollout. The most damaging patterns: any transient silent-refresh failure or scope re-consent silently wipes the entire token cache and logs the user out (validateToken on every app launch makes this fire constantly), the auth window has no timeout and several dead-end states that leave users stuck on a blank/closed window with no error, and safeStorage being unavailable throws unhandled inside save paths that get swallowed, producing a "logged in but never persists" loop. Multi-account is mishandled (always account[0]), and the access token is handed to the renderer as the Power BI embed token. None of this is covered by a single test. For a Monday ship to non-technical users, the resilience defects are more dangerous than the (real but lower-likelihood) crypto nitpicks.

findings.title: Silent-token failure calls logout() and wipes the entire account cache instead of triggering interactive refresh
findings.description: In getAccessToken() (auth-service.ts:302-311), when acquireTokenSilent throws InteractionRequiredAuthError the handler calls this.logout() — which clears the encrypted cache AND removes every MSAL account — then returns INTERACTION_REQUIRED. The correct MSAL pattern is to fall back to an interactive acquireToken on that account, NOT to destroy persisted state. Worse, validateToken() (lines 79-96) calls getAccessToken() and ALSO calls logout() on INTERACTION_REQUIRED/NO_ACCOUNT, and checkAuth() in auth-store.ts:32 runs validateToken() on EVERY app launch. So a normal expired-refresh-token (which happens routinely after the ~90-day refresh token lifetime, conditional-access re-auth, password change, or any transient network blip that MSAL surfaces as interaction-required) silently logs the user fully out with no interactive retry.
findings.evidence: auth-service.ts:302-311 (catch InteractionRequiredAuthError -> await this.logout()), auth-service.ts:86-89 (validateToken calls logout), auth-store.ts:32 (validateToken on every checkAuth)
findings.impact: Non-technical users get silently kicked back to the login screen on a recurring basis with no explanation. Each occurrence is a 'it logged me out again' support ticket. There is no silent->interactive fallback at all, so the app cannot do the seamless re-auth that is the entire point of offline_access.
findings.recommendation: On InteractionRequiredAuthError, attempt interactive token acquisition for the existing account (acquireTokenInteractive / re-run the auth-code flow) before clearing anything. Never call logout() from a read path like validateToken(); at most clear cache only after interactive re-auth is declined by the user.

verification.reasoning: Opened src\main\auth\auth-service.ts and src\renderer\stores\auth-store.ts. The cited code is accurate verbatim. getAccessToken() (lines 302-311) catches InteractionRequiredAuthError from acquireTokenSilent and calls await this.logout(), then returns INTERACTION_REQUIRED. logout() (322-340) is genuinely destructive: it calls tokenCache.clearCache() (wipes the encrypted persisted cache), sets this.account=null, and loops removeAccount() over every MSAL account. validateToken() (79-96) calls getAccessToken() and again calls logout() on INTERACTION_REQUIRED/NO_ACCOUNT. auth-store.ts checkAuth() (line 32) invokes validateToken() as the app-launch auth gate. A project-wide grep for acquireTokenInteractive returns zero hits, confirming there is NO silent->interactive fallback anywhere; the only re-auth path is the separate login() flow which requires a manual user click. msal-config.ts:46 does request the 'offline_access' scope, so the app is set up for refresh-token-based seamless re-auth, but the catch handler defeats it by destroying the cache on the first silent failure. The correct MSAL pattern on InteractionRequiredAuthError is to fall back to an interactive token acquisition on the same account rather than logging out and discarding all state. The claim's mechanism and impact (routine forced logouts after refresh-token expiry / CA re-auth / password change with no seamless retry) are correct.
verification.notes: Could not refute: the code does exactly what the claim states and no fallback exists elsewhere. Downgraded critical->high because this is a recurring UX/auth-resilience defect, not a security exposure, data loss, or crash, and the user can always recover via manual re-login. Suggested fix: on InteractionRequiredAuthError, call acquireTokenInteractive (or surface a re-auth prompt) for the existing account instead of logout(); and remove the redundant/destructive logout() in validateToken so a transient silent failure does not erase persisted accounts.

findings.title: Auth BrowserWindow has no timeout and several silent dead-end paths that strand the user
findings.description: openAuthWindow() (auth-service.ts:218-270) opens a BrowserWindow and resolves the Promise only on a localhost redirect or window close. There is NO timeout. If the user stalls on MFA, the network hangs, an MSAL/AAD interstitial fails to redirect, or a conditional-access page renders but never reaches localhost, the promise never resolves and login() hangs forever with isLoading=true in the store. Additionally: will-navigate blocks disallowed hosts via event.preventDefault() but does NOT close the window or resolve(null), so a blocked navigation leaves a frozen blank window. And if the localhost redirect arrives with an error param (e.g. error=access_denied / consent declined) instead of code, handleRedirect closes the window and resolve(null) maps to a generic LOGIN_CANCELLED, hiding the real AAD error.
findings.evidence: auth-service.ts:218-270 (no setTimeout, no error-param handling), lines 250-261 (will-navigate preventDefault without resolve), lines 232-244 (handleRedirect only reads code/state, ignores error/error_description)
findings.impact: Users who decline consent, fail MFA, or hit a network stall see a spinner that never ends or a blank window, with the app stuck in isLoading. This is the single most likely 'the app froze on login' ticket source for 20 non-technical users.
findings.recommendation: Add a timeout (e.g. 3-5 min) that closes the window and resolves null with a TIMEOUT error. Parse error/error_description from the redirect URL and surface them. On blocked navigation, close + resolve(null). Distinguish user-cancel from AAD errors in the returned error code.

verification.reasoning:
Opened src\main\auth\auth-service.ts (openAuthWindow lines 218-270) plus the auth store (src\renderer\stores\auth-store.ts) and IPC handler (src\main\index.ts:208) to trace the full isLoading lifecycle.

Factual primitives are correct: (1) there is no setTimeout in openAuthWindow; the promise resolves only via handleRedirect on a localhost URL (lines 234-243) or the 'closed' event (line 264). (2) will-navigate (lines 250-261) calls event.preventDefault() for disallowed hosts and returns without close/resolve, leaving a blank window. (3) handleRedirect (lines 232-244) reads only code/state and ignores error/error_description, so an AAD error=access_denied redirect yields code=null -> resolve(null) -> generic LOGIN_CANCELLED.

But the headline impact ("login hangs forever with isLoading=true," "spinner that never ends," "frozen window strands the user") is materially overstated:

- The window is created with show:true (line 223) and is visible. authWindow.on('closed') => resolve(null) (lines 264-266) is always wired. So whenever the user stalls on MFA, hits a network hang, or lands on a non-redirecting interstitial, they can close the visible window and the promise resolves null -> login() returns LOGIN_CANCELLED -> store sets isLoading:false (auth-store.ts:91-95). This is the normal escape hatch for any interactive auth window; the promise only stays pending while the user keeps the window open, which is expected. It is not an unrecoverable dead-end.
- For the error-param case, authWindow.close() executes at line 237 BEFORE the code/state null check, so the window closes and isLoading clears. That directly contradicts the claim's "endless spinner / frozen window" for that path. The only real defect there is a misleading generic "cancelled" message instead of surfacing the AAD error — a UX/diagnostics issue, not a freeze.
- The will-navigate preventDefault path is the one genuine rough edge (blank window left open), but it is still recoverable by closing the window, and is unlikely in normal flows since ALLOWED_AUTH_HOSTS covers the standard MS login/CDN hosts.

Net: the absence of a timeout is a real, defensible hardening gap (a hung remote page with no user action will sit pending), and the error-param swallowing is a real minor UX bug. Neither produces the claimed unrecoverable "app froze, spinner forever" condition because window-close always resolves and the error path already closes the window. Downgraded from high to low: real but minor, recoverable, and the impact narrative is inaccurate.
verification.notes: Reasonable low-priority improvements: add a setTimeout(...resolve(null)) safety net in openAuthWindow; on will-navigate block, call authWindow.close() instead of just preventDefault; and in handleRedirect, read error/error_description on the localhost redirect and propagate a real error code instead of mapping to LOGIN_CANCELLED. None are production-critical given the always-resolving 'closed' handler on the visible window.

findings.title: safeStorage unavailable throws inside save paths that are swallowed, causing a permanent 'logs in but never stays logged in' loop
findings.description: encrypt() in token-cache.ts:22-28 throws if safeStorage.isEncryptionAvailable() is false. saveCache/saveUserInfo call encrypt() with no try/catch. persistCache() in auth-service.ts:53-60 wraps saveCache in try/catch and only console.warn()s the error, so login() returns success even though NOTHING was persisted. On next launch the cache is empty -> user must log in again -> persist fails again. On Windows safeStorage is normally available, but it fails on accounts with no DPAPI / certain locked-down or roaming-profile / non-interactive-desktop configurations. The user is never told encryption is unavailable; they just can never stay logged in.
findings.evidence: token-cache.ts:22-28 (encrypt throws), auth-service.ts:53-60 (persistCache swallows error and returns), token-cache.ts:46-49 (saveCache no catch)
findings.impact: On any machine where OS encryption is unavailable, the user is trapped in an infinite re-login loop with no error message. Impossible for a non-technical user to diagnose; guaranteed escalation.
findings.recommendation: Check safeStorage.isEncryptionAvailable() once at startup and surface a clear, actionable error to the UI if false (rather than failing silently per-write). Propagate persist failures to the login result so the user is told their session can't be saved, instead of reporting success.

verification.reasoning: I read both cited files plus auth-store.ts and LoginScreen.tsx. The claim's mechanism is wrong. It assumes the only unguarded encrypt() in the save path is via persistCache() (swallowed), so login() returns success with nothing persisted, producing a silent infinite re-login loop. But it overlooks tokenCache.saveUserInfo() at auth-service.ts:191. The login() sequence is: (1) persistCache() at line 184 — internally try/catch, swallows encrypt errors; (2) saveUserInfo(userInfo) at line 191 — NOT wrapped, so encrypt()'s throw propagates; (3) success return at 193-203. Line 191 sits inside login()'s outer try (132-215), so when safeStorage.isEncryptionAvailable() is false, encrypt() throws inside saveUserInfo, the throw is caught at line 210, and login() returns { success: false, error: { code: 'LOGIN_FAILED', message: String(error) } } — i.e. it returns FAILURE, not success. The renderer confirms this surfaces visibly: auth-store.ts login() (lines 81-95) only sets isAuthenticated:true when response.success && response.data.success; otherwise it sets error = response.error.message and leaves the user logged out. LoginScreen.tsx (57-64) renders that error in a 'Sign in failed' MessageBar, and the thrown Error's text ('OS-level encryption unavailable — refusing to store tokens in plaintext') is carried in the message. So the claimed impact — 'login() returns success even though NOTHING was persisted', 'infinite re-login loop with no error message', 'user is never told' — does not occur. The login attempt fails loudly with a displayed, reasonably diagnostic error on machines where OS encryption is unavailable. There is no silent loop. At most there is a minor inconsistency (persistCache swallows while saveUserInfo does not), but it does not produce the claimed CRITICAL/HIGH symptom; it actually causes the safer loud-fail behavior. Relevant files: C:/Users/Brendan Cameron/Desktop/powerbi-viewer/src/main/auth/auth-service.ts (lines 53-60, 182-215), C:/Users/Brendan Cameron/Desktop/powerbi-viewer/src/main/auth/token-cache.ts (lines 22-28, 45-65), C:/Users/Brendan Cameron/Desktop/powerbi-viewer/src/renderer/stores/auth-store.ts (76-102), C:/Users/Brendan Cameron/Desktop/powerbi-viewer/src/renderer/components/auth/LoginScreen.tsx (57-64).
verification.notes: Downgraded from high to low. The unguarded saveUserInfo() call (auth-service.ts:191) inside login()'s try/catch makes login() return LOGIN_FAILED when encryption is unavailable, which the UI displays as a 'Sign in failed' message bar with the encryption-unavailable text. No silent re-login loop, no missing error message. Residual minor concern only: persistCache's swallow-and-continue is inconsistent with saveUserInfo's throw, but it does not produce the claimed impact.

findings.title: Always selects accounts[0] — multi-account and account-switch are broken and non-deterministic
findings.description: Both initializeCache() (auth-service.ts:43-46) and getAccessToken() (lines 276-291) pick accounts[0] with no logic to match the intended user. MSAL returns accounts in no guaranteed order. If a user ever has two accounts in the cache (e.g. logged in with a work account, later a different one, or a guest/B2B account got added), the app may silently acquire tokens for the WRONG account. There is no account-selection UI and login() stores only the most recent account's userInfo, so getCurrentUser() (which reads cached userInfo when this.account is null) can disagree with the account getAccessToken() actually uses.
findings.evidence: auth-service.ts:45 (accounts[0]), auth-service.ts:283-290 (firstAccount = accounts[0]), getCurrentUser auth-service.ts:100-112 reads tokenCache.loadUserInfo independently
findings.impact: Any user who has authenticated more than one identity sees data from the wrong tenant/account, or a name in the UI that doesn't match the data being fetched. Hard-to-reproduce, high-confusion tickets. Also a data-exposure risk if accounts span tenants.
findings.recommendation: Persist and key off homeAccountId. Look up the account by stored homeAccountId via getAccountByHomeId; if absent, force re-login. Provide an explicit account when multiple exist rather than index 0.

verification.reasoning: The cited code is quoted accurately: initializeCache() (auth-service.ts:45) and getAccessToken() (auth-service.ts:283-290) both unconditionally select accounts[0], and getCurrentUser() (auth-service.ts:100-112) reads tokenCache.loadUserInfo() independently when this.account is null. So the structural observation is correct. But the claimed HIGH impact (wrong-tenant data exposure, UI name disagreeing with fetched data) is substantially overstated for THIS app: (1) msal-config.ts:12 pins the authority to a single specific tenant (https://login.microsoftonline.com/${tenantId}), not /common or /organizations, so the cross-tenant / guest / B2B-from-another-home-tenant exposure scenario is largely foreclosed — only this one tenant's accounts and Power BI resource tokens can ever land in the cache. (2) Multi-account accumulation is very hard to reach: logout() (lines 322-331) removeAccount()s ALL accounts and clears userInfo; the InteractionRequiredAuthError path (line 306) also calls logout() before re-login; and the LoginScreen only renders when isAuthenticated is false. There is no add-account / switch-account UI that calls login() while a valid session exists, so a second account essentially never coexists with the first in normal use. (3) Within a session, this.account is cached in memory after the first accounts[0] read, so getCurrentUser() and getAccessToken() read the same account and cannot diverge unless the rare multi-account state exists. saveUserInfo (line 191) and this.account (line 183) are both set from the same tokenResponse.account at login, keeping them in sync across logout/login cycles. Net: the accounts[0] pattern is genuinely fragile / not best practice and worth hardening (match by homeAccountId), but the conditions for the claimed user-facing harm are difficult to reach given single-tenant authority, all-accounts-cleared logout, and no account-switch UI. This is a latent robustness issue, not a HIGH production defect.
verification.notes: Relevant files: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\main\auth\auth-service.ts (lines 36-51, 98-129, 272-291, 322-340), C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\main\auth\msal-config.ts (line 12, single-tenant authority), C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\renderer\stores\auth-store.ts, C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\renderer\components\auth\LoginScreen.tsx. Recommended low-priority hardening: store homeAccountId at login and use getAccountByHomeId() instead of accounts[0] in initializeCache/getAccessToken to make selection deterministic and future-proof if a switch-account feature or multi-tenant authority is ever added.

findings.title: Logout clears local cache only — AAD/browser SSO session is never cleared, so 'switch account' is impossible
findings.description: logout() (auth-service.ts:322-340) deletes the encrypted store and removes MSAL accounts, but never clears the AAD server session or the auth BrowserWindow's session cookies (the auth window uses the default session; the main window uses a persistent PARTITION_NAME partition in prod per index.ts:61). MSAL has no logout redirect here. So after 'logout', the next login() reopens the AAD page which silently SSO's straight back into the SAME account via the still-valid AAD session cookie. A user who logged in with the wrong account cannot switch.
findings.evidence: auth-service.ts:322-340 (no session.clearStorageData, no AAD logout url), index.ts:61 persistent partition, openAuthWindow uses default session (no partition) auth-service.ts:220-228
findings.impact: Users cannot change accounts. For a shared machine or a user who first signs in wrong, logout-then-login is a no-op that re-binds the wrong identity. Classic 'it won't let me sign in as someone else' ticket with no workaround short of clearing app data.
findings.recommendation: On logout, clear the auth window partition's cookies/storage (session.fromPartition(...).clearStorageData) and/or load the AAD logout endpoint, and add prompt=select_account (or prompt=login) to the auth-code URL so users can pick an account.

verification.reasoning: I read auth-service.ts and index.ts and the claim holds. logout() (auth-service.ts:322-340) only calls tokenCache.clearCache(), nulls this.account, and loops removeAccount() over MSAL accounts. There is no session.clearStorageData, no AAD end-session/logout redirect, and no logoutRequest anywhere (grep for clearStorageData|select_account|endSession|logoutRequest returned zero hits in main). The auth flow loads the AAD login URL into a BrowserWindow created at auth-service.ts:220-228 with NO partition, i.e. the default session; AAD's auth cookies (ESTSAUTH/ESTSAUTHPERSISTENT) persist there. login() builds the auth URL at lines 141-149 via getAuthCodeUrl and passes scopes/state/PKCE but NO prompt parameter (loginRequest in msal-config.ts also has no prompt). So on the next login() after logout, openAuthWindow reopens the same default-session window where the still-valid AAD cookie silently SSOs straight back into the same account with no account picker and no credential prompt. Net effect: a user who signed in with the wrong account cannot switch via logout-then-login; the only workaround is clearing app data. The standard remedies (prompt:'select_account' in the auth URL, or session.defaultSession.clearStorageData() on logout) are both absent. The IPC handler (index.ts:212-214) adds nothing. One small correction to the claim's framing: the un-cleared cookies live in the default session used by the auth window, not the PARTITION_NAME partition (which the main window uses, index.ts:61); the claim's own evidence notes the auth window uses the default session, so its reasoning is consistent. This is a real functional/UX defect rather than a data-exposure security hole, but high severity is appropriate for the impact described.
verification.notes: Cited lines accurate. Fix: add prompt:'select_account' to authCodeUrlParams and/or call session.defaultSession.clearStorageData({storages:['cookies']}) in logout(). File: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\main\auth\auth-service.ts

findings.title: Raw AAD access token is returned to the renderer as the Power BI 'embed token' with a fabricated 1-hour expiry
findings.description: getEmbedToken() (powerbi-api.ts:354-382) returns the raw user access token as the embed token and hardcodes expiration = now + 1 hour. The real token lifetime is whatever AAD issued (often <60 min, and the cached one may already be partway through its life). The renderer therefore holds a full Power BI AAD bearer token (exposed to any XSS in the renderer, dev-tools, or a compromised powerbi-client) and trusts a fake expiry. When the real token expires before the fabricated timestamp, embeds fail mid-session with no proactive refresh, because the renderer believes the token is still valid.
findings.evidence: powerbi-api.ts:367-374 (returns tokenResponse.data as token, expiration now+3600000)
findings.impact: Mid-session 'report failed to load' / blank-embed tickets when the real token expires before the fake expiry. Also widens token exposure: a long-lived Graph/Power BI access token sits in renderer memory. The renderer has no way to know the token actually expired.
findings.recommendation: Decode the JWT exp claim and return the real expiration so powerbi-client can refresh on time, or wire powerbi-client's accessToken refresh callback back through IPC to getAccessToken on demand. Long term, prefer app-owns-data embed tokens scoped to the report.

findings.title: No mutex around getAccessToken / cache persistence — concurrent IPC calls race on refresh and cache serialize/write
findings.description: getAllItems() fans out many getReports/getDashboards calls in parallel (powerbi-api.ts:514-528), each of which calls getAccessToken() -> acquireTokenSilent() -> persistCache() concurrently. MSAL's in-memory cache mostly serializes internally, but persistCache() does getTokenCache().serialize() + encrypt + electron-store write with no lock, so concurrent writers can interleave and the last writer wins, and an InteractionRequiredAuthError from ANY one of the parallel calls triggers logout() mid-flight (see interaction-required-nukes-cache), tearing the cache out from under the other in-flight requests. There is no single-flight guard on interactive login either, so two triggers can open two auth windows.
findings.evidence: powerbi-api.ts:514-528 (Promise.all fan-out), auth-service.ts:295-306 (per-call acquireTokenSilent + persistCache + logout), no locking anywhere
findings.impact: Intermittent, hard-to-reproduce failures on the initial 'load everything' screen: partial content, spurious logouts, or two login windows. The exact 'works on my machine, flaky for users' bug class.
findings.recommendation: Serialize token acquisition behind a single in-flight promise (cache the pending acquireTokenSilent), debounce persistCache, and ensure only one interactive login can be open at a time.

findings.title: clientId/tenantId baked into the bundle and redirectUri='http://localhost' — not secret, but operationally fragile
findings.description: scripts/generate-config.js embeds AZURE_CLIENT_ID/TENANT_ID into azure-config.generated.ts at build time, bundled into the asar. For a public client these are NOT secrets (they appear in every auth URL), so this is acceptable — the real risk would be a client secret, and correctly there is none. However: (1) the generated file is gitignored, so a developer who clones and builds without the .env gets a hard build failure (process.exit(1)) — fine, but undocumented for whoever rebuilds. (2) redirectUri is the bare 'http://localhost' with no fixed port/path; this must be registered EXACTLY in the AAD app registration as a 'Mobile and desktop' / public-client redirect, and any mismatch yields AADSTS500113/redirect errors that surface to users as a generic failed login. (3) .env parsing in generate-config.js is naive (no quote stripping, splits on '=' prefix only) so a quoted or CRLF-laden value silently embeds wrong characters.
findings.evidence: generate-config.js:11-37, msal-config.ts:6-12, auth-service.ts:143 redirectUri 'http://localhost'
findings.impact: Low security impact (no secret leaked). Operational: a botched rebuild or AAD redirect-URI mismatch produces unexplained login failures. CRLF/quoted .env values would embed a malformed tenant/client id and break auth for the whole fleet.
findings.recommendation: Document the required AAD redirect-URI registration and the .env build requirement. Trim quotes and trailing \r in generate-config.js. Consider validating clientId/tenantId look like GUIDs at build time. Confirm electron-builder asar packing doesn't ship the .env itself.

findings.title: CSRF state and PKCE verifier handling is single-slot instance state — concurrent or restarted logins corrupt validation
findings.description: pendingAuthState is a single instance field (auth-service.ts:16) and the PKCE verifier is a local closure in login(). If login() is invoked twice (e.g. user double-clicks the login button, or a retry fires while the first window is open), the second call overwrites pendingAuthState, so the first window's redirect will fail CSRF validation (state mismatch) and the user sees 'possible CSRF attack' for a perfectly legitimate double-click. The state comparison itself is a plain !== (not constant-time), which is a minor nit for a self-generated nonce but not best practice.
findings.evidence: auth-service.ts:16, 137-138 (single slot), 163-167 (=== compare and scary error text)
findings.impact: Double-click or rapid-retry on login throws a 'possible CSRF attack' error at a non-technical user, who reads it as the app being hacked. Confusing and alarming support ticket.
findings.recommendation: Guard against concurrent logins (single-flight), key state+verifier together per attempt, and soften the user-facing error text (CSRF detail belongs in logs, not the UI).

findings.title: On startup validation failure the store sets isAuthenticated=false with no error, dropping the user to login with no explanation
findings.description: When validateToken() returns data=false (which now happens whenever the refresh token is stale — see interaction-required-nukes-cache), checkAuth() (auth-store.ts:51-58) sets user=null, isAuthenticated=false, error=null. The user is silently bounced to the login screen on app open with no message about why their session ended. Combined with the cache-nuking bug, this is the visible symptom users will report.
findings.evidence: auth-store.ts:51-58 (error stays null), validateToken auth-service.ts:79-95 returns success:true,data:false
findings.impact: Users perceive random forced logouts with zero feedback. Hard to triage from a support call because the app reports no error.
findings.recommendation: Distinguish 'never logged in' from 'session expired' and show a brief 'your session expired, please sign in again' message. Fix the underlying cache-nuke so this stops being routine.

summary: The IPC surface is unusually disciplined for an app "built by an older model with a lot of bugs": all 35 channels match 1:1 between preload and main, UUID-shaped args are validated, export paths are sandboxed, and every handler that touches a service returns the IPCResponse envelope rather than throwing. That said, there are real reliability defects that will bite 20 non-technical users. The worst is the offscreen PDF-export window: it can hang the renderer promise forever (no timeout on did-finish-load/did-fail-load), it leaks event listeners and occasionally the window itself, and its capturePage/printToPDF/writeFile error paths are fragile. settings:update and usage:record-open accept entirely unvalidated renderer payloads and persist them verbatim into electron-store, which then drive timers and UI. The window lifecycle has a macOS-activate race and a missing single-instance lock. None of these are caught by tsc, which is why they survived the type pass.

findings.title: export:current-view-pdf can hang the renderer promise forever (no timeout, listeners never removed)
findings.description: The handler awaits a Promise that only resolves on 'did-finish-load' and only rejects on 'did-fail-load'. For a data: URL, Chromium normally fires one of these, but there is no timeout guard. If neither event fires (renderer crash of the hidden window, GPU/print subsystem stall, an empty/odd data URL, the window being closed out from under it), the awaited Promise never settles, so the ipcMain.handle invocation never returns and the renderer's `await window.electronAPI.export.currentViewToPdf(...)` hangs indefinitely with no error and no UI recovery. Additionally the listeners are attached with `.on` (not `.once`) and are never removed, so they accumulate on the webContents; combined with reuse this is a listener leak.
findings.evidence: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\main\index.ts:506-510 — `await new Promise<void>((resolve, reject) => { pdfWindow!.webContents.on('did-finish-load', () => resolve()); pdfWindow!.webContents.on('did-fail-load', (_e, code, desc) => reject(...)); pdfWindow!.loadURL(...) })`. No timeout; `.on` instead of `.once`.
findings.impact: A non-technical user clicks 'Export to PDF', the spinner never stops, the app appears frozen for that flow, and there is no error toast or way to cancel. Reproducible on any transient print/render hiccup. Support burden and perceived hard hang.
findings.recommendation: Wrap the load promise in a timeout (e.g. Promise.race with a 15-30s reject), use `webContents.once` for both events, and remove the opposite listener on settle. Ensure the finally block always closes pdfWindow (it does) and that a rejected/timed-out load still resolves the IPC call into a `{ success: false, error }` envelope (the surrounding try/catch already converts thrown errors, so a timeout reject is sufficient).

verification.reasoning:
Opened C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\main\index.ts and read the export:current-view-pdf handler (lines ~414-531). The cited code at 506-510 is accurate: a Promise resolves on 'did-finish-load', rejects on 'did-fail-load', no timeout, attached via .on not .once. So the literal code description is correct.

However the claim's framing is substantially overstated and partly wrong:

1) Listener-leak claim is FALSE. pdfWindow is a fresh `new BrowserWindow` created per invocation (line 477) and unconditionally closed in the `finally` block (lines 526-530, `pdfWindow.close()`). Its webContents is destroyed with the window. There is NO reuse of the webContents across calls, so listeners cannot accumulate. The claim explicitly says "combined with reuse this is a listener leak" — there is no reuse. .on vs .once is cosmetic here since the object is single-use and disposed.

2) Hang claim has a narrow real kernel but inflated impact. The content is a static data:text/html URL with an inline base64 PNG — no network, no external resources. Chromium reliably fires did-finish-load for such content, and malformed/'odd' data URLs trigger did-fail-load -> reject (recovery exists), contradicting the claim that an "empty/odd data URL" would hang. The only genuinely unhandled path is a renderer-process crash of the hidden window (render-process-gone/crashed not listened for) plus the absence of a timeout backstop. That is a rare transient edge case, not "reproducible on any transient print/render hiccup."

3) The claim conflates printToPDF ("print subsystem stall") with the load Promise, but printToPDF is a separate awaited call at line 512, not gated by this Promise; a print stall would not be caused by this code pattern.

Net: a legitimate defense-in-depth hardening opportunity (add a timeout + handle render-process-gone, use .once), but not a high-severity production bug. The dominant failure mode (load failure) is already handled via reject; the leak premise is incorrect.
verification.notes: Reasonable low-priority hardening: add a timeout race and a webContents 'render-process-gone' reject handler around the load Promise at src/main/index.ts:506-510, and switch .on to .once. Not a hang under normal operation since data: URL load failures already reject via did-fail-load and the per-call window is disposed in finally.

findings.title: settings:update persists arbitrary unvalidated renderer payload into electron-store
findings.description: The handler passes `updates: Partial<AppSettings>` straight to settingsService.updateSettings, which does `{ ...currentSettings, ...updates }` and writes it. There is zero runtime validation: the renderer (or anything that compromises it via the embedded Power BI/MSAL web content) can set `theme` to a non-enum string, `autoRefreshInterval` to a negative/huge/NaN number, `slideshowInterval` to 0, or inject arbitrary extra keys that get persisted forever. These values then drive real behavior — autoRefreshInterval feeds a setInterval, theme feeds the titlebar overlay colors and BrowserWindow backgroundColor. A bad number can produce a 0ms/NaN interval (busy loop or no refresh) and a bad theme breaks the dark/light branch in createWindow on next launch.
findings.evidence: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\main\index.ts:393-395 (handler) and C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\main\services\settings-service.ts:30-43 (`const newSettings = { ...currentSettings, ...updates }; store.set('settings', newSettings)`). AppSettings constrains theme to a union and notes autoRefreshInterval 1-60, but nothing enforces it at runtime (types.ts:87-96).
findings.impact: A corrupted settings value persists across restarts and can wedge the auto-refresh loop or the theme-dependent window creation, leaving a user stuck with a misbehaving app that only a manual store-file delete fixes. TypeScript does not protect IPC boundaries — `Partial<AppSettings>` is a compile-time fiction over an `any` payload.
findings.recommendation: Validate and clamp in updateSettings: whitelist known keys, check `theme` against the union, clamp numeric ranges (autoRefreshInterval 1-60, slideshowInterval to a sane floor), coerce booleans, and drop unknown keys. Reject with INVALID_INPUT on type mismatch rather than persisting.

verification.reasoning: The code facts are accurate: settings-service.ts:30-34 does `const newSettings = { ...currentSettings, ...updates }; store.set('settings', newSettings)` with no runtime validation, and index.ts:393-395 passes the renderer's `updates: Partial<AppSettings>` straight through. `Partial<AppSettings>` is compile-time only, so the IPC boundary is effectively `any`. The missing-validation gap is real (legitimate defense-in-depth finding). However the claimed impact is substantially overstated on three points. (1) Theme does NOT break: createWindow (index.ts:46,56-57,70) uses `initialTheme === 'dark'` / `=== 'system'` and falls through to the light branch via ternaries for any other string — a bad theme silently renders light, it never throws or wedges window creation. (2) The auto-refresh path is not a synchronous busy loop: autoRefreshInterval feeds `setInterval(cb, minutes*60*1000)` in ReportViewer.tsx:178 / PresentationMode.tsx:367, where the callback async-reloads the embedded Power BI webview; a 0/NaN interval produces a misbehaving (frequent or absent) async refresh, not CPU spin. (3) The claim that only a manual store-file delete fixes it is false: settingsService.resetSettings (IPC `settings:reset`) restores DEFAULT_SETTINGS in-app, and the renderer sliders (SettingsPage.tsx min/max 1-60 and 30-300) re-clamp on any user interaction. The legitimate renderer UI only ever emits clamped/enum values, so benign corruption is impossible; exploitation requires the embedded Power BI/MSAL web content to first compromise the contextIsolated+sandboxed renderer and reach the IPC channel, and even then the worst case is self-inflicted DoS of the user's own local app config — no RCE, escalation, or data exposure. Genuine bug, but the busy-loop, theme-break, and unrecoverability claims that justify HIGH do not hold.
verification.notes: Recommended fix is still worth doing: clamp/validate numeric ranges and whitelist enum/keys in settingsService.updateSettings before store.set, since TypeScript provides no protection at the IPC boundary.

findings.title: usage:record-open writes unvalidated payload; electron-store read-modify-write can race and grow unbounded fields
findings.description: usage:record-open accepts an item object and passes it directly to usageTrackingService.recordItemOpened with no validation of id/name/type/workspace fields. `name` and `workspaceName` are stored verbatim with no length cap, so a hostile or buggy renderer can write arbitrarily large strings into the persisted JSON. Separately, recordItemOpened does a non-atomic get -> splice/unshift -> set on electron-store; usage:record-open and usage:clear (and the duplicated read paths in content:get-recent/usage:get-recent/get-frequent) are all async handlers that can interleave, and electron-store's set serializes the whole file — concurrent record/clear calls can lose updates or, in the worst case, write a half-formed object if a call is in flight during another set.
findings.evidence: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\main\index.ts:537-550 (handler, no field validation) and usage-tracking-service.ts:30-69 (read-modify-write via store.get/store.set with no locking; no caps on name/workspaceName).
findings.impact: Rapidly opening items (or a buggy renderer firing record-open repeatedly) can drop usage updates or bloat the usage-tracking.json store. Unbounded `name` length lets the persisted file grow without limit. Low blast radius but it is exactly the kind of intermittent 'recents are wrong' bug that erodes trust.
findings.recommendation: Validate item.type against the literal union, validate id is non-empty/UUID-shaped, and truncate name/workspaceName to a reasonable length before storing. Serialize writes (a simple in-process mutex/queue around recordItemOpened/clearUsageData) since all access is single-process.

findings.title: No single-instance lock — a second launch spawns a second window and a second electron-store writer
findings.description: There is no app.requestSingleInstanceLock(). A non-technical user double-clicking the shortcut, or Windows relaunching the unsigned exe, starts a second process. Two processes then share the same electron-store files (settings.json, usage-tracking.json) with no cross-process locking, so the read-modify-write in usage tracking and the settings writes can clobber each other or corrupt the JSON on disk. It also produces two app windows / two auth/session partitions competing.
findings.evidence: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\main\index.ts:103-126 — app.whenReady() creates the window unconditionally; there is no requestSingleInstanceLock / second-instance handling anywhere in the file.
findings.impact: Double-launch (common for non-technical users who don't see the splash) yields two windows and concurrent writers to the same store files, which can corrupt settings/usage state. With electron-store, a corrupted JSON throws on next read — though here the try/catch in settings-service degrades to defaults, usage-tracking-service has NO try/catch and would throw an unhandled error into the IPC handler.
findings.recommendation: Call app.requestSingleInstanceLock() at top of bootstrap; if not acquired, app.quit(). On 'second-instance', focus/restore the existing mainWindow. This also fixes the dual-writer corruption risk for both stores.

findings.title: usage-tracking-service has no try/catch; a corrupted store throws and rejects the renderer promise
findings.description: settings-service wraps every store access in try/catch and returns an IPCResponse error envelope. usage-tracking-service does not — recordItemOpened, getRecentItems, getFrequentItems, clearUsageData all call store.get/store.set bare. If usage-tracking.json is corrupted (see dual-writer / single-instance issue) electron-store throws on construction or access. The handlers in index.ts (content:get-recent, usage:*) do wrap calls in try/catch and convert to an error envelope, so the renderer at least sees `{success:false}` — but the store CONSTRUCTOR at module load (line 17) runs at import time outside any try/catch, so a corrupted usage store throws during main-process startup and can prevent the app from booting at all.
findings.evidence: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\main\services\usage-tracking-service.ts:17-22 — `const store = new Store<UsageStore>({...})` at module top level, no guard. Same pattern in settings-service.ts:9-14. electron-store throws synchronously on malformed JSON.
findings.impact: A single corrupted store file (from a crash mid-write, dual writers, or disk issue) makes the app fail to start with no user-facing error — the worst possible failure mode for a non-technical user with no way to recover short of manually deleting %APPDATA% files.
findings.recommendation: electron-store supports a `clearInvalidConfig` option and/or wrap store construction in try/catch that resets to defaults on parse failure. At minimum, lazily construct the store inside a guarded accessor so a corrupt file degrades to defaults instead of crashing process startup.

findings.title: activate handler can create a second window during initial-window load race
findings.description: app.on('activate') checks BrowserWindow.getAllWindows().length === 0 and creates a window. This is registered inside whenReady AFTER createWindow(). On macOS, mainWindow is created with show:false and only shown on ready-to-show; an 'activate' firing in that gap is fine because the window already exists. The real gap: createWindow sets the module-level `mainWindow` but the 'closed' handler nulls it — if a user closes the window on macOS (app stays alive due to window-all-closed guard) and then activates, a new window is created correctly. However there is no guard against mainWindow being non-null-but-destroyed, and the IPC window:* handlers use `mainWindow?.` which silently no-ops after close rather than reporting. This is primarily a macOS path; the stated target is 20 Windows users, lowering severity.
findings.evidence: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\main\index.ts:121-125 (activate) and 93-95 (closed nulls mainWindow). Window control handlers at 174-202 use optional chaining and silently succeed even when no window exists.
findings.impact: On the Windows target this is mostly latent. Minor: window-control IPC calls resolve successfully even when they did nothing, so the renderer cannot distinguish a real minimize from a no-op. Low risk for the Monday rollout.
findings.recommendation: Guard createWindow against an existing live window, and have window:* handlers return a boolean/IPCResponse so the renderer knows the action took effect. Low priority relative to the others.

findings.title: PDF export fs.writeFile has no directory/permission pre-check and surfaces raw error strings
findings.description: Both export flows do `await fs.writeFile(filePath, buffer)` (index.ts:316 for content:export-report-pdf, index.ts:518 for export:current-view-pdf). content:export-report-pdf is NOT wrapped in try/catch around the writeFile, so an EACCES/EBUSY/ENOSPC (file open in a PDF viewer, read-only folder, disk full) throws out of the handler. Because ipcMain.handle catches thrown errors and rejects the renderer promise, the renderer's await rejects with a raw Electron error rather than the app's `{success:false, error}` envelope — inconsistent with every other handler and likely unhandled in the renderer (which is coded against the envelope). isValidExportPath confirms the .pdf extension and a user-dir root but does not confirm the directory exists or is writable.
findings.evidence: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\main\index.ts:310-318 — exportReportToPdf result is checked, but `await fs.writeFile(filePath, exportResponse.data)` at line 316 has no try/catch, unlike export:current-view-pdf which wraps writeFile in try/catch (line 518/521-525).
findings.impact: Exporting over a PDF that's already open in a viewer (EBUSY on Windows — very common) throws a raw rejection instead of a clean 'could not save' message. If the renderer only handles `result.success === false`, the rejection is unhandled and the export silently fails or logs an uncaught promise rejection.
findings.recommendation: Wrap the writeFile in content:export-report-pdf in try/catch returning `{success:false, error:{code:'WRITE_FAILED', message}}`, matching export:current-view-pdf. Map EBUSY/EACCES/ENOSPC to friendly messages.

findings.title: Offscreen PDF window inherits the global CSP and default session; data: image render is CSP-dependent
findings.description: export:current-view-pdf creates a hidden BrowserWindow with no explicit `session`/`partition`, so it uses defaultSession — the same session that has the global onHeadersReceived CSP injector (index.ts:105-114) setting `default-src 'self'; img-src 'self' data:`. The data:text/html document with an inline
findings.evidence: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\main\index.ts:477-485 (pdfWindow has no session set) vs the global CSP at 105-114 applying to defaultSession; the export HTML at 488-503 depends on inline style + data: img being CSP-permitted.
findings.impact: Latent coupling. If anyone later tightens the CSP for security (a likely follow-up given the 27 npm vulns), PDF export breaks with a confusing error. Not a Monday blocker but a hidden landmine.
findings.recommendation: Give the PDF window its own isolated session/partition (or attach a session whose onHeadersReceived is a no-op for this internal data URL), so the export feature does not depend on the renderer-facing CSP. Document the dependency if left as-is.

findings.title: export:current-view-pdf trusts renderer-supplied bounds/insets for capturePage without bounding to window size
findings.description: The bounds/insets from the renderer are rounded and floored at >=0, but never clamped to the actual window content size. capturePage with a rect larger than the page, or with x/y offsets beyond the content, can return an empty or oddly-sized image; imgWidth/imgHeight then feed the @page size and printToPDF microns. A zero or absurd dimension can produce a 0-size or gigantic PDF page, or a failed printToPDF. Number.isFinite is checked on width/height but not on x/y, so a non-finite x/y survives the Math.max(0, Math.round(NaN)) -> NaN path (Math.round(NaN) is NaN, Math.max(0, NaN) is NaN), potentially producing a NaN-containing captureRect.
findings.evidence: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\main\index.ts:442-461 — Number.isFinite guards width/height only; x/y go through Math.max(0, Math.round(bounds.x)) which yields NaN if bounds.x is NaN, and nothing clamps the rect to webContents/window bounds.
findings.impact: A buggy renderer (or odd zoom/DPI state) can hand bounds that produce a malformed capture rect, yielding a blank/garbage PDF or a printToPDF failure. Self-inflicted only (renderer-controlled), so low severity, but it's an unvalidated path into a native capture call.
findings.recommendation: Validate x/y with Number.isFinite too, and clamp the final captureRect to the window's content bounds (getContentBounds). Reject with INVALID_INPUT if the resulting rect is empty rather than proceeding to capture.

dimension: React renderer correctness: effect lifecycles, timer/listener cleanup, stale closures, StrictMode double-mount, async-embed/unmount races, error-boundary coverage, re-render storms
summary: These viewer components are riddled with lifecycle defects that will manifest as real bugs for the 20 non-technical users, not theoretical edge cases. The most damaging class: the `isLoadingRef` "Strict Mode guard" pattern is broken — under StrictMode (which IS enabled in main.tsx) the mount effect's cleanup resets the guard but ALSO leaves a half-embedded report behind, and several async paths set `isLoadingRef=true` then never clear it on the unmount that races the async embed, producing permanent blank screens that require an app restart. There are also genuine uncleaned timers (the 5s fullscreen-hint setTimeout, the two export-focus setTimeouts), stale-closure bugs in the auto-refresh and slideshow effects, and a complete absence of error boundaries around the individual viewers, so any throw inside the powerbi-client embed code tears the whole app down to the root fallback. The focus-reclaim 200ms interval is correctly cleaned but thrashes focus and is recreated on every isFullscreen toggle. The shared module-level powerbiService singleton makes the StrictMode double-mount actively dangerous because reset()/embed() mutate cross-component global state. Several of these are certain to hit users; ship-blockers for a Monday rollout.

findings.title: StrictMode double-mount + isLoadingRef guard leaves viewers permanently blank (no embed, no error)
findings.description: main.tsx wraps the tree in <React.StrictMode>, so on first navigation to a viewer React mounts, immediately unmounts, then remounts the component. In ReportViewer/PresentationMode/DashboardViewer the mount effect calls loadReport()/loadDashboard(), which is async: it sets isLoadingRef.current=true synchronously, then awaits getReports()/getEmbedToken(). The StrictMode unmount runs its cleanup (powerbiService.reset(container); reportRef=null; isLoadingRef.current=false) WHILE that await is still pending. When the await resolves it calls powerbiService.embed(embedContainerRef.current, ...) — but the container DOM node may already be reset, and on the second (real) mount loadReport runs again and re-embeds. Because reportRef was nulled and isLoadingRef toggled by the racing cleanup, you get either a double-embed into the same container or, when the first promise resolves after the second embed started, the first resolution's report.on('loaded') sets isLoading=false against a report that was reset — leaving a blank container with isLoading already false (so no spinner) and no error. The isLoadingRef check at the TOP of the mount effect (lines 83-85) never actually fires in StrictMode because cleanup resets it to false between the two mounts, so the guard it was written for does nothing.
findings.evidence: ReportViewer.tsx:28,75-97,317-324 (isLoadingRef guard + async loadReport); DashboardViewer.tsx:23,102-122,137-143; PresentationMode.tsx:46,209-242,419-425; usePowerBIService.ts:4-12 (module-level singleton service shared across all viewers); main.tsx:82 (<React.StrictMode>)
findings.impact: Users open a report/dashboard and see a blank white pane with no spinner and no error message. There is no retry affordance visible because the error overlay never renders (error stays null) and the spinner is gone (isLoading=false). The only recovery is closing and reopening the app. This is intermittent (depends on async timing) which makes it look like 'the app randomly doesn't load reports' — exactly the 'a lot of bugs' symptom described.
findings.recommendation: Replace the boolean isLoadingRef hack with an AbortController/mounted flag captured per-effect-run: `let cancelled=false;` in the effect, check `if(cancelled)return;` after every await before touching refs/state/embed, and set `cancelled=true` in cleanup. Only embed if the container is still mounted and not cancelled. Track the embedded report in a local variable and reset THAT specific instance in cleanup rather than relying on container reset of the shared singleton. Verify by enabling StrictMode and navigating between two reports repeatedly.

verification.reasoning:
I read main.tsx (StrictMode at :82), usePowerBIService.ts (module-level singleton service), all three viewers (ReportViewer.tsx, DashboardViewer.tsx, PresentationMode.tsx), and the powerbi-client embed/reset internals (node_modules/powerbi-client/dist/powerbi.js).
