The claim's GUARD-DEFEAT premise is correct but incomplete: in dev StrictMode the mount-1 cleanup sets isLoadingRef.current=false before mount-2's effect, so the isLoadingRef check at ReportViewer:83-85 (and Dashboard:109, Presentation:216) does NOT stop the second loadReport. So two loadReport cycles do run. However, the claimed IMPACT (permanently blank, no spinner, no error) is NOT reachable:

1) Every loadReport begins with powerbiService.reset(container) (ReportViewer:331, Dashboard:149, Presentation:431). reset() in powerbi.js (11580-11597) removes the iframe AND calls off() on every event of the prior embed. So a stale (reset) report's 'loaded' handler is torn down and can never fire setIsLoading(false) against a dead report -- this is the exact mechanism the claim relies on, and it is defused.

2) The two loadReport calls run interleaved on one JS thread against the SAME stable DOM node. Whoever calls embed() LAST owns the DOM iframe, reportRef.current, and the only live 'loaded' handler. That handler clears the spinner against the actually-visible report. The result is consistent regardless of which promise resolves first.

3) StrictMode produces exactly ONE cleanup (mount-1's). The surviving mount-2 embed is never reset afterward, so it loads and renders normally. No duplicate iframe lingers because the surviving call's reset() removes the prior iframe before re-embedding.

4) Decisive: StrictMode double-invokes effects in DEVELOPMENT ONLY (React 18.2, confirmed in package.json). Production builds mount once, so the double token-fetch/embed never happens for end users. The 'production bug, only recovery is restart, random intermittent failures' framing does not hold.

Residual: a dev-only redundant embed + token fetch -- wasteful but self-healing and invisible in production. Not a CRITICAL/HIGH permanent-blank defect.
verification.notes: Files: src/renderer/main.tsx:82; src/renderer/hooks/usePowerBIService.ts:3-8; src/renderer/components/viewer/ReportViewer.tsx:75-97,317-331,386-425; DashboardViewer.tsx:102-122,137-181; PresentationMode.tsx:209-242,419-472; node_modules/powerbi-client/dist/powerbi.js:11398-11522 (embedInternal/embedExisting/embedNew) and 11580-11597 (reset removes iframe + off() all events). The reset-before-embed pattern plus last-embed-wins makes the claimed stale-loaded path unreachable; StrictMode is dev-only so no production impact.

findings.title: Fullscreen-hint 5s setTimeout is never cleared (fires after unmount -> setState on unmounted component)
findings.description: In ReportViewer the fullscreenchange effect schedules `setTimeout(() => setShowFullscreenHint(false), 5000)` (line 217) but the return cleanup only removes the fullscreenchange listener — it never captures or clears this timeout. If the user enters fullscreen and then navigates Back / exits within 5 seconds, the component unmounts but the timer survives and calls setShowFullscreenHint on an unmounted component. The same effect also re-subscribes whenever pages.length changes (dependency line 228), so entering fullscreen on a multi-page report and then having pages change can stack timers.
findings.evidence: ReportViewer.tsx:203-228, specifically the bare `setTimeout(() => setShowFullscreenHint(false), 5000)` at line 217 with cleanup at 225-227 that only does removeEventListener('fullscreenchange', ...)
findings.impact: Console warning 'Can't perform a React state update on an unmounted component' and a small memory leak per fullscreen-then-leave cycle. Low user-visible damage on its own but contributes to the death-by-a-thousand-leaks profile of a long-running kiosk/presentation app left open all day.
findings.recommendation: Store the timeout id in a ref or local var and clear it in the effect cleanup: `const id = setTimeout(...); return () => { document.removeEventListener(...); clearTimeout(id); }`.

verification.reasoning:
I opened src/renderer/components/viewer/ReportViewer.tsx (the file is at src/renderer/components/viewer/, not the path in the claim). The code structure is exactly as cited: lines 203-228 hold the fullscreenchange effect; line 217 schedules a bare setTimeout(() => setShowFullscreenHint(false), 5000) with no captured handle; the cleanup at 225-227 only does removeEventListener('fullscreenchange', ...). So the literal defect — an uncaptured timeout that the cleanup never clears — is real. The setter setShowFullscreenHint comes from useState at line 46.

However, the claimed IMPACT is substantially overstated, which is why this is partly-true rather than confirmed-high. package.json pins react ^18.2.0. The 'Can't perform a React state update on an unmounted component' warning was REMOVED in React 18 — calling a state setter after unmount in React 18 is a silent no-op and produces no console warning. So the claim's primary advertised symptom does not actually occur in this codebase.

The 'memory leak' characterization is also overstated: the dangling timer is a transient closure that self-clears in at most 5 seconds, after which it is garbage-collected. It is not a persistent or unbounded leak, so it does not meaningfully feed a 'death-by-a-thousand-leaks' kiosk profile. The trigger window is also narrow: the timer only exists when the user is in fullscreen on a multi-page report (pages.length > 1), and exiting fullscreen normally already sets the hint false via the else-branch at lines 219-221; the orphaned-timer path requires a full component unmount within the 5s window. The 'stacking timers on pages.length change' sub-claim is misleading — timers are created only inside the event handler on a fullscreenchange event, not on effect (re)subscription, so a pages.length change does not itself spawn timers.

Net: a genuine but trivial code-hygiene defect (clearTimeout should be added), with no real React warning and only a 5-second transient reference. Not a CRITICAL/HIGH production bug.
verification.notes: Fix is a one-liner: capture const id = setTimeout(...) and call clearTimeout(id) in the cleanup. File: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\renderer\components\viewer\ReportViewer.tsx lines 217 and 225-227.

findings.title: Two focus-reclaim setTimeouts in mousedown handler are never tracked or cleared
findings.description: The fullscreen keydown/mousedown effect's handleMouseDown schedules two setTimeouts (10ms and 100ms, lines 274-283) to re-focus the embed container after a click. These ids are never stored, so the effect cleanup (lines 308-314) cannot clear them. If the user clicks inside the embedded report and then immediately navigates away (or fullscreen exits) within 100ms, the timeout fires `embedContainerRef.current.focus()` on a possibly-detached node guarded only by `document.fullscreenElement` — benign in most cases but unbounded in count: rapid clicking spawns unbounded pending timers.
findings.evidence: ReportViewer.tsx:268-285 (handleMouseDown with two untracked setTimeout calls); cleanup at 308-314 clears only focusCheckInterval and the two document listeners
findings.impact: Minor: stray focus calls and a small leak under rapid clicking in fullscreen. The fullscreenElement guard prevents most actual misbehavior, so user-visible impact is low, but it is still uncleaned work surviving unmount.
findings.recommendation: Collect timeout ids and clear them in cleanup, or use a single ref-tracked timeout. Better: drop the multi-timeout focus hack in favor of a focusin listener that reclaims focus declaratively.

findings.title: No error boundary wraps individual viewers — any throw in powerbi-client embedding nukes the entire app
findings.description: There is exactly one ErrorBoundary, at the App root (App.tsx:55 wrapping HashRouter, and again in main.tsx:83 around ThemedApp). Individual routes/viewers are NOT wrapped in their own boundaries. powerbi-client's embed(), getPages(), bookmarksManager.capture(), and the report.on() callbacks run synchronous code that can throw (e.g. malformed embed config, SDK internal errors, accessing detached iframe). Any such synchronous throw during render or in a React-invoked callback propagates to the single root boundary, replacing the WHOLE application UI (sidebar, nav, everything) with the full-screen 'Something went wrong' fallback. The fallback's only recovery is 'Go Home' which sets window.location.hash='#/' and resets state — heavy-handed for what may be one flaky report.
findings.evidence: App.tsx:54-141 (single <ErrorBoundary> at root, each <Route> element wrapped only in <ProtectedRoute>, never in a per-view boundary); ErrorBoundary.tsx:121-123. Note most powerbi errors arrive via report.on('error') async callbacks which a boundary would NOT catch anyway — but synchronous throws in embed()/render paths are caught and escalate to full-app teardown.
findings.impact: A single problematic report can blank the entire application for a non-technical user, who then sees a scary warning emoji screen instead of an inline 'this report failed, go back' message. Recovery requires understanding the Go Home button. Worse, because the boundary is at the root, it loses all navigation context.
findings.recommendation: Wrap each viewer route element in its own <ErrorBoundary> (or a route-level boundary inside ProtectedRoute) with a lighter fallback that offers Back/Retry without tearing down the shell. Note that asynchronous powerbi errors (report.on('error')) are already handled in-component; the boundary is specifically for synchronous render/embed throws.

verification.reasoning:
The structural facts are correct but the impact narrative is refuted by the actual code. Files are at src/renderer/App.tsx and src/renderer/components/ErrorBoundary.tsx (not the cited paths, but located).

TRUE parts: There is exactly one ErrorBoundary wrapping HashRouter at App.tsx:55. Individual routes (App.tsx:69-133) are wrapped only in ProtectedRoute, never a per-view boundary. The fallback (ErrorBoundary.tsx:60-118) is full-screen with Go Home (sets window.location.hash='#/') and Try Again.

FALSE / overstated parts (the basis for HIGH severity): The claim's mechanism — that synchronous throws in embed()/render paths are caught by and escalate to the root boundary, blanking the app — does not hold against the code:
- In ReportViewer.tsx, powerbiService.embed() (lines 378-381) sits inside an explicit try/catch (329-500). A synchronous throw is converted to setError() (line 497), which renders an INLINE per-viewer error panel with a 'Try again' button (lines 742-751), local to the viewer's content area. It never reaches the React error boundary.
- DashboardViewer.tsx is identical: embed() inside try/catch (148-206) -> setError() -> inline panel (331-340). Its dashboard.on('error') handler also calls setError() (183-192) for inline display.
- getPages() (391,447,542), bookmarksManager.capture() (550), setPage(), updateSettings(), refresh() are all awaited inside try/catch or have .catch() handlers (e.g. 505, 174). Async rejections cannot reach a React error boundary regardless of nesting.
- report.on(...) callbacks (386-494) run in the SDK's async message dispatch, not React's render/event call stack. The claim itself concedes a boundary would not catch these — so per-route boundaries would not help either.
- The viewers' JSX render paths contain no powerbi-client synchronous calls; just Fluent UI markup.
- AppViewer.tsx uses an Electron <webview>, not powerbi-client embedding at all, and routes failures to inline setError() (78).

So the only way the root boundary fires is a generic React render exception in the viewer JSX (e.g. a Fluent component throwing), which is unrelated to 'powerbi-client embedding' and is not demonstrated. The codebase already isolates embed/report failures into inline, per-viewer, recoverable error UI. The 'one flaky report blanks the whole app for a non-technical user' impact is therefore not supported. Adding per-route boundaries is a reasonable defensive nicety (downgrade to low), not a HIGH production bug.
verification.notes: Cited paths App.tsx:55 / ErrorBoundary.tsx are actually src/renderer/App.tsx and src/renderer/components/ErrorBoundary.tsx. Structural claim accurate; causal/impact claim refuted by pervasive try/catch + inline setError() recovery in all three viewers.

findings.title: Auto-refresh interval recreated on every isLoading/error toggle; reads reportRef but depends on isLoading/error as stale snapshot guards
findings.description: The auto-refresh effect in ReportViewer (168-183) and PresentationMode (355-372) lists [isLoading, error, autoRefreshEnabled, autoRefreshIntervalMinutes] as deps. isLoading flips true->false on initial load and on every manual handleRefresh, and error can toggle, so the setInterval is torn down and recreated multiple times during normal use — each recreation restarts the full refresh clock, so a report that keeps toggling loading state may never actually reach its refresh interval. The interval body re-checks `!isLoading && !error` but those are the closure-captured values from when the interval was created (not live), making the dependency the only thing keeping them fresh — a fragile coupling. The interval also references reportRef.current (a ref, fine) but the isLoading/error guards inside are redundant-yet-stale.
findings.evidence: ReportViewer.tsx:168-183; PresentationMode.tsx:355-372. Deps include isLoading and error which change frequently; interval callback closes over them at creation time.
findings.impact: On reports where loading state churns, the auto-refresh timer resets repeatedly and the underlying data may refresh far less often (or more often) than the configured interval. For a wall-mounted dashboard expected to auto-refresh every N minutes, this means stale data shown to users without them realizing it.
findings.recommendation: Drop isLoading/error from the deps and instead read them via refs inside the interval body (you already use refs for pages/index elsewhere). Key the effect only on [autoRefreshEnabled, autoRefreshIntervalMinutes] so the timer is stable, and gate the refresh call on live ref values.

findings.title: PresentationMode slideshow interval is managed by both an effect and doExit, with slides.length in deps causing mid-show timer resets
findings.description: The slideshow interval is stored in slideshowIntervalRef and started/stopped by the effect at 317-334 (deps [isPlaying, intervalSeconds, slides.length]) AND separately cleared imperatively in doExit (180-183) and in the fullscreenchange handler (254-257) and the mount-cleanup (228-231). Because slides.length is a dependency, any change to the slide set (e.g. bookmarks arriving asynchronously after pages via the two separate async blocks in report.on('loaded'), or a slideshowMode change) tears down and recreates the interval mid-playback, resetting the per-slide timer. The effect's own cleanup clears the ref but the body also overwrites slideshowIntervalRef.current without first clearing a possibly-existing interval if isPlaying stays true across a deps change — the cleanup runs first so this is usually safe, but the multiple imperative clear sites mean the ref can be cleared by doExit while the effect still believes it owns a live interval.
findings.evidence: PresentationMode.tsx:317-334 (effect), 174-206 (doExit clears same ref), 246-280 (fullscreenchange handler clears same ref), 227-242 (mount cleanup clears same ref). slides built asynchronously in two stages at 471-503 (pages then bookmarks set separately), each triggering the slides useEffect at 126-164.
findings.impact: During an auto-playing presentation, when bookmarks load a beat after pages (common), the slide list changes, the interval resets, and the current slide lingers for up to a full extra interval — visible 'stutter' in an unattended slideshow. Multiple owners of one timer ref is a latent double-clear/leak hazard.
findings.recommendation: Make a single owner of slideshowIntervalRef (the effect). Have doExit/fullscreen/cleanup set isPlaying=false and let the effect's cleanup stop the timer, rather than clearing the ref directly from four places. Remove slides.length from deps and read slides.length live via a ref inside the tick callback.

findings.title: Auto-start slideshow effect omits isPlaying from deps (intentional but creates a re-trigger gap) and can re-fire on error clear
findings.description: The auto-start effect (167-171) reads isPlaying in its condition (`!isPlaying`) but deliberately omits it from the dependency array [slidesReady, autoStartSlideshow, isLoading, error]. This is the standard 'I want to read it but not react to it' pattern, but combined with error toggling: if a recoverable error sets error then clears it (e.g. token refresh path sets error then a retry succeeds), this effect re-runs and may auto-start the slideshow again even after the user manually paused it, because the only thing it checks is the stale !isPlaying captured guard plus the live state — actually it reads isPlaying live (not in deps) so a manual pause is respected, but a slidesReady/error re-fire after manual pause+resume sequencing can still force-play.
findings.evidence: PresentationMode.tsx:166-171, deps array excludes isPlaying though it is read at line 168
findings.impact: Edge-case: after an error-then-recover cycle, a slideshow the user paused may resume on its own. Minor annoyance during an attended presentation.
findings.recommendation: Track whether auto-start has already fired with a ref (hasAutoStartedRef) and only auto-start once per mount, rather than on every slidesReady/error change.

findings.title: Focus-reclaim 200ms interval is recreated on every navigateToPage change and force-steals focus 5x/sec
findings.description: The keyboard-navigation effect (232-315) depends on [navigateToPage, isFullscreen]. navigateToPage is a useCallback with [] deps so it is stable, but the effect still re-subscribes the document-level capture-phase keydown/mousedown listeners and recreates the 200ms maintainFocus interval every time isFullscreen toggles. The interval itself IS cleared in cleanup (311-313), so it is not a leak — but while active it calls embedContainerRef.current.focus() 5 times per second whenever activeElement isn't the container. If the user is trying to interact with a filter pane or a Fluent UI control that lives outside the embed container while fullscreen, this interval will yank focus away from it every 200ms, making those controls nearly unusable in fullscreen. The capture-phase keydown handler also calls stopImmediatePropagation on arrow keys, which can break arrow-key behavior inside any focused input rendered over the report in fullscreen.
findings.evidence: ReportViewer.tsx:287-302 (maintainFocus + setInterval(maintainFocus, 200)), 289-297 unconditional focus steal; 239-264 capture-phase arrow handler with stopImmediatePropagation
findings.impact: In fullscreen, any UI element other than the embed container (filter inputs, the slideshow/export buttons if surfaced, browser/native dialogs) loses focus every 200ms and becomes effectively uninteractable. Users report 'I can't type in the filter box in fullscreen.' Not a memory leak, but a real usability defect.
findings.recommendation: Only reclaim focus when focus has actually escaped to the iframe (check activeElement.tagName==='IFRAME' or activeElement===document.body), not unconditionally. Prefer a focusout/focusin listener over a polling interval. Scope stopImmediatePropagation to only when the embed container or iframe is the focus target.

findings.title: AppViewer webview listeners depend on partitionLoaded, but webview only renders after partitionLoaded — effect runs once with null ref then leaks nothing but misses early events; also no cleanup of in-flight loadAppDetails on unmount
findings.description: AppViewer's webview event-listener effect (61-91) depends on [partitionLoaded]. On first render partitionLoaded is false, so the effect runs, finds webviewRef.current === null (webview not rendered until line 184 gate), and bails early with no listeners attached and a no-op cleanup. When partitionLoaded flips true the webview renders and the effect re-runs and attaches listeners — but there is a render-commit ordering risk: the effect runs after the webview is committed, so ref should be set; however if did-start-loading fires synchronously during webview attachment before the effect binds, the initial loading state is missed and isLoading can be stuck. Separately, loadAppDetails (93-104) is an un-cancelled async that calls setAppName after await with no mounted-guard, so unmounting mid-fetch warns on unmounted setState.
findings.evidence: AppViewer.tsx:61-91 (effect keyed on partitionLoaded), 184-196 (webview render gated on partitionLoaded), 93-104 (loadAppDetails with unguarded setAppName at 99)
findings.impact: Occasional stuck spinner if the very first webview load event is missed, and an unmounted-setState warning when navigating away from an app while its name is still loading. Low frequency.
findings.recommendation: Attach webview listeners in the same effect that renders the webview using a callback ref, or guard against missed initial state by reading webview.isLoading() after binding. Add a cancelled flag to loadAppDetails.

findings.title: PresentationMode binds mousemove on BOTH window and document and recreates the hide-controls timer on every isPlaying change
findings.description: The hide-controls effect (388-417) adds the same handleMouseMove to both window and document (407-408) — document-level mousemove already bubbles from window's perspective; binding both means every mouse move fires handleMouseMove twice, each call clearing and resetting the 3s timeout. The effect depends on [isPlaying], so toggling play/pause tears down and rebuilds both listeners and the timeout. Cleanup is correct (removes both, clears timeout), so this is not a leak, but it is double work on every mouse move during a presentation and an unnecessary timer churn on every play/pause toggle. Combined with the transparent overlay's own onMouseMove (573-580), there are three independent mousemove paths.
findings.evidence: PresentationMode.tsx:388-417 (window+document both bound to handleMouseMove), 572-580 (overlay onMouseMove)
findings.impact: Negligible correctness impact but doubles mousemove handler invocations during an active presentation (a hot path). On low-end hardware driving a wall display this is wasted work; mainly a code-smell that signals the focus/control-visibility logic was patched repeatedly.
findings.recommendation: Bind mousemove once (document is sufficient). Read isPlaying via a ref inside handleMouseMove so the effect needn't depend on isPlaying and rebind on every toggle.

findings.title: Settings/details async loads in all viewers setState without a mounted guard (unmounted-setState on fast navigation)
findings.description: Every viewer kicks off an unguarded async load-on-mount that setStates after the await: ReportViewer.loadSettings (60-73, setAutoRefreshEnabled/Interval), DashboardViewer.loadDashboardDetails (65-82, setDashboardName), PresentationMode.loadSettings (107-123), AppViewer.loadPartition (36-48, setPartitionName/setPartitionLoaded). None capture a cancelled/mounted flag, so navigating away before the IPC round-trip resolves calls setState on an unmounted component. AppViewer's is the most consequential because setPartitionLoaded(true) gates the webview render — though after unmount it is harmless, it still warns.
findings.evidence: ReportViewer.tsx:60-73; DashboardViewer.tsx:65-82; PresentationMode.tsx:107-123; AppViewer.tsx:36-48
findings.impact: React 18 mostly no-ops setState on unmounted components (the warning was removed), so user-visible impact is near zero, but the pattern is the same root cause as the more serious embed race and indicates no async lifecycle discipline anywhere in the viewer layer.
findings.recommendation: Standardize a `let active = true; ...; if(!active) return; return () => { active = false; }` guard, or extract a useAbortableEffect hook and use it for every load-on-mount.

findings.title: report.on('loaded')/pageChanged callbacks reference pagesRef which may be empty when first events fire
findings.description: The pageChanged handler (442-458) guards on `pagesRef.current.length > 0` before mapping the active page to an index. But pagesRef is only populated by the separate effect at 51-53 reacting to the pages state set inside the loaded callback (397). There is a window where loaded -> setPages runs, but the pageChanged event arrives before React commits the pages state and syncs pagesRef, so the index mapping silently no-ops and currentPageIndex desyncs from the actual page. This is a self-inflicted consequence of mirroring state into refs across effect boundaries.
findings.evidence: ReportViewer.tsx:442-458 (pageChanged uses pagesRef.current), 397 (setPages in loaded), 51-53 (pagesRef synced via separate effect)
findings.impact: The fullscreen page counter (e.g. '2/5') and arrow-key starting position can be wrong immediately after load until the next pageChanged event corrects it. Cosmetic but visible in fullscreen navigation.
findings.recommendation: Compute pageInfos synchronously and set both setPages and pagesRef.current in the same loaded callback, instead of relying on a downstream effect to sync the ref.

findings.title: Module-level powerbiService singleton is reset/embedded by all viewers — StrictMode and back-to-back navigation cause cross-instance interference
findings.description: usePowerBIService returns a single module-level pbi.service.Service shared by ReportViewer, DashboardViewer, and PresentationMode. Each viewer's cleanup calls powerbiService.reset(container) and load calls powerbiService.embed(container). powerbi-client's Service tracks embeds internally keyed by element. Under StrictMode's mount/unmount/mount, and when navigating Report -> Presentation -> Report quickly (PresentationMode.doExit navigates straight to /report), two viewers can be mid-teardown/mid-embed against the same singleton in overlapping async windows. reset() on one container while another is embedding can desynchronize the service's internal embed registry, producing 'This element already has a report embedded' style failures or a silently dead iframe.
findings.evidence: usePowerBIService.ts:4-12 (single shared Service); ReportViewer.tsx:91-93,331,378; DashboardViewer.tsx:117,149,172; PresentationMode.tsx:188,234,431,464; PresentationMode.doExit navigates to /report (202) immediately after reset, racing ReportViewer's fresh embed.
findings.impact: Intermittent failures when moving between a report, its presentation mode, and back — a core user flow (the Slideshow button at ReportViewer.tsx:713 goes to presentation, Esc returns). Symptom: report fails to re-embed after exiting slideshow, blank pane, restart required.
findings.recommendation: Either ensure embed/reset are strictly serialized per container with a mounted-guard so no embed runs after its own cleanup, or fully reset the specific report instance (report not container) in cleanup. Consider whether the singleton should instead be created per-viewer-instance. Test the Report->Slideshow->Esc->Report loop repeatedly under StrictMode.

verification.reasoning:
I read the singleton (src/renderer/hooks/usePowerBIService.ts:4-12 — one module-level pbi.service.Service), all three viewers, App.tsx routing, and the actual powerbi-client implementation in node_modules/powerbi-client/dist/powerbi.js.

The claim's central mechanism — that reset() on one container can desync the shared service's embed registry and affect another viewer — is contradicted by the library implementation. Every Service operation is keyed strictly by the individual DOM element:
- embedInternal (11398-11411) branches on element.powerBiEmbed, a property stamped on that specific HTMLElement.
- embedExisting (11489-11494) and addOrOverwriteEmbed (11567-11573) match/filter by x.element === element.
- reset(element) (11580-11597) early-returns if !element.powerBiEmbed and only removes the entry where x === powerBiElement.powerBiEmbed, then deletes that element's property. It cannot touch a different element's embed.

Routing (App.tsx:110-131) makes /report, /dashboard, and /presentation mutually exclusive — Routes renders exactly one. ReportViewer, DashboardViewer, and PresentationMode each hold their own embedContainerRef pointing at a distinct DOM node, and never co-mount. So Scenario B (Report -> Presentation -> Report) involves three different container elements: PresentationMode.doExit (188,202) resets ITS element then navigates; the fresh ReportViewer mounts a brand-new container with no powerBiEmbed property, whose reset() is a no-op before its own embed(). No shared element exists, so the 'already has a report embedded' error (11493, which is intra-element and requires the embeds list to be out of sync with the DOM) cannot arise from cross-viewer interaction.

Scenario A (StrictMode mount/unmount/mount) operates on a SINGLE element that React reuses across the remount. The isLoadingRef guard (ReportViewer 83-85/321-324, PresentationMode 216-218/422-425, DashboardViewer 109-111/140-143) blocks concurrent re-entry of loadReport, and every load path does reset(element) before embed(element). addOrOverwriteEmbed keeps the per-element list consistent (last embed wins), so no registry desync is produced. At worst a redundant teardown/embed on one element, not cross-instance corruption.

The claim misreads the library as keying embeds globally/loosely when it keys per-HTMLElement, and overstates a single-element StrictMode race into cross-viewer interference that the routing model makes impossible.
verification.notes: Files: src/renderer/hooks/usePowerBIService.ts:4-12; src/renderer/App.tsx:110-131 (mutually exclusive routes); ReportViewer.tsx:89-97,317-324,331,378; PresentationMode.tsx:174-206,227-242,431,464; DashboardViewer.tsx:115-122,149,172. Library proof: node_modules/powerbi-client/dist/powerbi.js:11398-11411,11489-11494,11567-11573,11580-11597 (all per-element keying). The one defensible kernel: reset-then-embed plus isLoadingRef on a shared singleton is slightly fragile and an instance-per-viewer service would be cleaner, but it produces no production defect as written — hence low, not high.

dimension: Client-side state management (Zustand stores + consuming components): caching, invalidation, lifecycle, concurrency, error/settings drift
summary: This layer is riddled with state-correctness defects that will surface immediately for 20 non-technical users. There are TWO independent slideshow-interval implementations with non-overlapping valid ranges (30-300 vs 3-60) and a third dead constant (15/30/60/120/300), so the same feature behaves differently depending on where the user changes it, and the in-viewer change is silently discarded. The search cache has a never-called invalidateCache (pure dead code) plus a 5-minute TTL, so newly published/renamed/deleted reports are invisible or stale for up to 5 minutes with no way to force a refresh. The search store never cancels in-flight requests on dialog close or query-clear, so stale results render into a closed or emptied dialog. content-store's per-workspace Maps and the search cache never evict for the life of the process. Workspace lazy-expansion has a genuine partial-failure bug: a workspace with zero items re-fetches on every expand, and Promise.all swallows per-call partial failures into empty arrays that masquerade as "empty workspace." Error states in auth/content/settings are set and never auto-cleared, so a transient failure leaves a permanent red banner. None of this is caught by tsc; it is all logic/lifecycle.

findings.title: Slideshow interval has two incompatible ranges and a third dead constant; in-viewer changes are never persisted
findings.description: The slideshow auto-advance interval is configured in two unrelated UIs with non-overlapping valid ranges. SettingsPage.tsx (lines 171-178) uses a Slider min=30 max=300 step=10. PresentationMode.tsx (lines 618-624) uses a Slider min=3 max=60. So a value chosen in Settings (e.g. 120s) is OUT OF RANGE for the in-viewer slider, and a value chosen in the viewer (e.g. 5s) is OUT OF RANGE / unreachable in Settings. Worse, the in-viewer slider calls only setIntervalSeconds (local React state) and NEVER calls updateSettings, so the change is silently discarded the moment Presentation Mode is closed and never written back to the store/disk. On top of that, shared/constants.ts defines DEFAULT_SLIDESHOW_INTERVAL=30 and SLIDESHOW_INTERVALS=[15,30,60,120,300] which are both dead (the store default is DEFAULT_SETTINGS.slideshowInterval=60, a different value, and the arrays/constant are imported nowhere). Three sources of truth, all disagreeing.
findings.evidence: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\renderer\components\settings\SettingsPage.tsx:171-178 (Slider min=30 max=300); C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\renderer\components\viewer\PresentationMode.tsx:618-624 (Slider min=3 max=60, onChange={setIntervalSeconds} only, no persistence); C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\shared\constants.ts:13-14 (DEFAULT_SLIDESHOW_INTERVAL=30, SLIDESHOW_INTERVALS=[15,30,60,120,300]) vs constants.ts:20 (DEFAULT_SETTINGS.slideshowInterval=60)
findings.impact: A presenter who sets a comfortable 5-minute pace in Settings opens the viewer to find a slider that maxes at 60s and shows the wrong position; if they nudge it, the change vanishes on close. Non-technical users will report 'the slideshow timing doesn't save' and 'the two sliders disagree.' This is the exact 'settings not reloaded / read in two places with different ranges' drift, plus a silent data-loss bug.
findings.recommendation: Pick ONE canonical range (drive both sliders from a single shared constant, e.g. SLIDESHOW_INTERVAL_MIN/MAX/STEP). Make the in-viewer slider call useSettingsStore().updateSettings({ slideshowInterval }) (debounced) instead of local-only setState, or at minimum keep it local AND clamp on load. Delete the dead DEFAULT_SLIDESHOW_INTERVAL/SLIDESHOW_INTERVALS or wire them up as the single source. Align DEFAULT_SETTINGS.slideshowInterval with the canonical default.

verification.reasoning: Opened all three cited files and verified every claim. SettingsPage.tsx:171-174 Slider is min=30/max=300/step=10 bound to settings.slideshowInterval; PresentationMode.tsx:618-620 Slider is min=3/max=60 bound to local intervalSeconds state. Ranges overlap only in [30,60], so Settings values of 120/300 exceed the viewer max and viewer values of 3-29 are below Settings min. The viewer slider's onChange (line 622) calls ONLY setIntervalSeconds (a useState declared at line 60); grep for updateSettings/settingsApi/saveSettings and settings.set in PresentationMode.tsx returned no matches — settings are loaded on mount via electronAPI.settings.get() (line 110-112) but never written back, so an in-viewer interval change is discarded on unmount (silent data loss). constants.ts:13-14 DEFAULT_SLIDESHOW_INTERVAL=30 and SLIDESHOW_INTERVALS=[15,30,60,120,300] are referenced nowhere in src except their definition site (grep across src/ confirms), while the live default is DEFAULT_SETTINGS.slideshowInterval=60 (line 20) — a genuine third disagreeing source of truth. One small nuance vs the claim: the viewer DOES load the persisted value on mount (line 112), so it isn't 'always wrong position'; but a persisted 120/300 fed into a max=60 slider still renders a wrong/clamped position, so the impact substantially holds. All three sub-claims (incompatible ranges, no persistence, dead constants) are real.
verification.notes: No security/crash/corruption dimension, so not critical, but user-visible broken UX plus silent data loss in a core feature justifies high. Refutation attempt failed: the missing persistence call genuinely does not exist elsewhere, and the constants are genuinely dead.

findings.title: PresentationMode and ReportViewer read settings once on mount via IPC and never re-sync with the settings store
findings.description: PresentationMode.tsx (lines 107-123) and ReportViewer.tsx (lines ~60-66) each load settings exactly once on mount with their own window.electronAPI.settings.get() call and copy values into local useState (intervalSeconds, autoRefreshIntervalMinutes, etc.). They do not subscribe to useSettingsStore. So if the user changes the auto-refresh interval or slideshow settings in SettingsPage while a viewer/presentation is already open (common: settings live in a side panel / separate route the user navigates back from), the open viewer keeps the OLD value until it is fully unmounted and remounted. There is no event/store subscription to propagate the change. This is a second instance of the 'settings read in multiple places, not reloaded after change' drift.
findings.evidence: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\renderer\components\viewer\PresentationMode.tsx:107-123 (one-shot settings.get on mount, [] deps); C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\renderer\components\viewer\ReportViewer.tsx:60-66 + 33 (local autoRefreshIntervalMinutes state seeded once); settings-store.ts updateSettings (lines 35-44) updates the store but viewers never read the store
findings.impact: Auto-refresh interval changes don't take effect on already-open reports — a user sets refresh to 1 minute expecting fresher dashboards, sees no change, and concludes the feature is broken. Inconsistent behavior is confusing and erodes trust in a tool shipped to non-technical staff.
findings.recommendation: Have viewers subscribe to useSettingsStore (const { settings } = useSettingsStore()) and derive intervals from settings via useEffect, instead of a one-shot IPC read into local state. Ensure settings-store is loaded at app root so it is hydrated before viewers mount.

findings.title: Search 5-minute cache is never invalidated; invalidateCache is dead code
findings.description: search-store.ts keeps a module-level searchCache of all workspaces/apps/reports/dashboards with CACHE_TTL = 5 minutes (line 22). invalidateCache (lines 68-76) resets it, but a repo-wide grep shows invalidateCache is referenced ONLY at its own definition and in the interface — it is called from nowhere. Nothing invalidates the cache when content changes (new report published in Power BI, report renamed, workspace access granted/revoked, or even after the app's own refresh actions). For up to 5 minutes, search returns stale results: it will surface deleted reports and miss newly added ones. There is also no manual 'refresh search index' affordance. Combined with the lack of any eviction, the cache is purely time-bounded and otherwise frozen.
findings.evidence: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\renderer\stores\search-store.ts:22 (CACHE_TTL=5min), :24-30 (module cache), :68-76 (invalidateCache defined), Grep over src\renderer for invalidateCache returns only search-store.ts:47 (interface) and :68 (definition) — zero call sites
findings.impact: User publishes/renames a report in Power BI, searches for it in the viewer, and cannot find it (or clicks a stale result that 404s on navigation) for up to 5 minutes with no way to force a refresh. For 20 users relying on search to find content, this looks like missing or broken data.
findings.recommendation: Call useSearchStore.getState().invalidateCache() after content-mutating flows (WorkspacesPage refresh, successful login, any explicit Refresh button) — at minimum wire the existing dead function to the Refresh buttons. Consider lowering TTL or adding a 'Refresh results' control in SearchDialog. Long term, share one cache between content-store and search-store rather than two independent stale copies.

verification.reasoning: I opened C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\renderer\stores\search-store.ts and every cited fact checks out. CACHE_TTL = 5 * 60 * 1000 (line 22). A module-level searchCache holds all workspaces/apps/reports/dashboards (lines 24-30). invalidateCache is defined (lines 68-76) and declared in the SearchState interface (line 47). A repo-wide Grep for "invalidateCache" across src returns exactly two hits: the interface declaration (line 47) and the definition (line 68) - zero call sites. The two consumers of the store, src\renderer\components\search\SearchDialog.tsx and src\renderer\components\layout\TitleBar.tsx, destructure other actions but never invalidateCache. So the cache is purely TTL-bounded and never explicitly cleared. I also confirmed the search path has no compensating server-side freshness: main\services\powerbi-api.ts getAllItems() (lines 497-546) fetches live from the Power BI API with no main-process cache, meaning the renderer searchCache is the sole staleness source. The app DOES have multiple Refresh affordances (HomePage handleRefresh line 57, WorkspacesPage line 193, AppsPage line 99) and a settings auto-refresh, but none call invalidateCache - the auto-refresh setting governs Power BI dataset refresh, not the search index. So after a publish/rename/delete in Power BI, in-app search can return stale results (surface deleted reports, miss new ones) for up to 5 minutes with no manual force-refresh. The bug is real. However, the auditor's "high / missing or broken data" framing overstates impact: the staleness window is self-healing (bounded at 5 minutes, then auto-refetches), affects only the secondary search-dialog discovery feature, and does not touch the live content-browsing or navigation paths. A clicked stale result simply 404s rather than corrupting anything. That is a genuine but moderate UX defect, hence I downgrade to medium.
verification.notes: Easy fix: call useSearchStore.getState().invalidateCache() from the existing Refresh handlers (HomePage/WorkspacesPage/AppsPage) and/or add a TTL low enough or a manual "refresh search" affordance. The dead-code symptom strongly suggests the wiring was simply never completed.

findings.title: In-flight search resolves into a closed/emptied dialog because closeSearch and query-clear do not cancel the request
findings.description: The stale-request guard uses a module-level currentSearchId that is incremented ONLY inside search() (line 85). closeSearch() (lines 60-62) and clearResults() set results:[] but do NOT bump currentSearchId. So if a user types, then hits Escape / clicks away while a search is in flight, the resolving search passes its thisSearchId===currentSearchId check and calls set({ results, isSearching:false }) — repopulating results into a dialog the user already closed (and isOpen is false, so on next open stale results may flash). Separately, SearchDialog's debounce (SearchDialog.tsx:39-47) only calls search() when query.trim() is truthy; clicking the clear (X) button sets query to '' which does NOT trigger search(''), so an already-dispatched search for the previous text still resolves and renders results under an now-empty input. The empty-query early-return in search() (lines 79-82) only helps if search('') is actually invoked, which the debounce prevents.
findings.evidence: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\renderer\stores\search-store.ts:60-62 (closeSearch resets results but not currentSearchId), :219-221 (clearResults same), :85 (currentSearchId only incremented in search); C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\renderer\components\search\SearchDialog.tsx:39-47 (debounce guards on query.trim(), so clearing the box never dispatches search('') to flush in-flight results)
findings.impact: Users see search results appear after closing the dialog, or stale results linger after clearing the box — a flickery, 'haunted' UI. On a slow Power BI tenant (cache-miss path does 3 network calls), the window for this is seconds, easily hit in normal use.
findings.recommendation: Increment currentSearchId inside closeSearch and clearResults (and on setQuery('')) so any in-flight search aborts at its next guard. Have the debounce effect call search(query) for the empty case too (it already early-returns) or explicitly clearResults() when query is empty. Optionally gate the final set() on get().isOpen.

findings.title: Lazy workspace expansion re-fetches empty workspaces every time and treats partial Promise.all failure as an empty workspace
findings.description: toggleWorkspace (WorkspacesPage.tsx:57-112) decides whether to load content with `workspace.reports.length === 0 && workspace.dashboards.length === 0` (line 69). For a workspace that genuinely has no reports/dashboards, this condition stays true forever, so EVERY expand re-triggers a full getReports+getDashboards round-trip — there is no 'loaded' flag distinguishing 'not loaded yet' from 'loaded, found nothing.' Separately, the load uses Promise.all (lines 86-89) and then keys off `reportsRes.success`/`dashboardsRes.success` individually: if getReports succeeds but getDashboards rejects, Promise.all rejects entirely and the .catch (line 102) sets isLoading:false with NO data and NO error — the workspace renders the 'No reports or dashboards in this workspace' empty state (line 238-243) even though the reports call actually succeeded. A genuine fetch failure is indistinguishable from a truly empty workspace, and there is no error surface or retry for the per-workspace load. The double setWorkspaces pattern (lines 58-79 then 82-111) also reads `workspace.isLoading` from a state snapshot mutated by the preceding setState in the same tick, which is fragile.
findings.evidence: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\renderer\components\workspaces\WorkspacesPage.tsx:69 (empty-check doubles as loaded-check), :86-89 (Promise.all), :95-96 (per-call success fallback to []), :102-108 (.catch sets isLoading:false, no error), :238-243 (empty state shown on failure)
findings.impact: Users with empty or transiently-failing workspaces hit Power BI repeatedly on each expand (extra API load / throttling risk for 20 concurrent users), and a partial network failure silently shows 'no content' when content exists — they conclude reports are missing.
findings.recommendation: Add an explicit `contentLoaded: boolean` flag per workspace; gate fetching on `!contentLoaded` rather than array length. Replace Promise.all with Promise.allSettled so a single failing call doesn't blank both lists, and set a per-workspace error/retry state when a call fails instead of rendering the empty state.

findings.title: content-store reports/dashboards Maps and search cache grow unbounded for the app lifetime
findings.description: content-store.ts holds reports: Map<string, Report[]> and dashboards: Map<string, Dashboard[]> keyed by workspaceId (lines 12-13). loadReports/loadDashboards (lines 59-87) only ever set entries; there is no eviction, no clear-on-logout, and no size bound. Likewise the module-level searchCache in search-store holds every report+dashboard across all workspaces. For a tenant with many workspaces/reports these grow monotonically and are never freed until process exit. Critically, on logout (auth-store.logout, lines 104-122) the content-store and search cache are NOT cleared, so a second user signing in on the same machine can see the prior user's cached workspace/report names (a minor data-leak/staleness concern, not just memory).
findings.evidence: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\renderer\stores\content-store.ts:12-13 (Maps), :64-67/:79-82 (set-only, no eviction), no reset action in interface (lines 21-28); C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\renderer\stores\auth-store.ts:104-122 (logout clears auth only); search-store.ts:24-30 (module cache survives logout)
findings.impact: For a long-running kiosk/presentation session (a realistic use of this app) memory creeps; more importantly, switching accounts leaves the previous user's content list cached and searchable until app restart. Low severity for 20 users but real.
findings.recommendation: Add a clear()/reset() action to content-store and call it (plus searchStore.invalidateCache()) from auth-store.logout and on a fresh login. Consider an LRU bound or TTL on the per-workspace Maps if tenants are large.

findings.title: Error strings in auth/content/settings stores are set permanently with no auto-clear on retry success
findings.description: Across stores, error is set on failure but only cleared by an explicit clearError() (auth-store, content-store) or at the START of the next load (settings/auth set error:null in their loading branch). content-store.loadReports/loadDashboards/loadApps/loadRecent/loadFrequent (lines 59-121) only console.error on failure and never set the store error at all — so those failures are invisible to the UI (silent). Conversely loadWorkspaces sets error and clearError exists, but nothing calls clearError automatically on a subsequent successful operation other than loadWorkspaces' own start. settings-store.updateSettings/resetSettings (lines 35-55) swallow failures with console.error and never set error, so a failed settings save looks successful to the user. WorkspacesPage holds its OWN error state (line 27) that, once set, only clears when loadWorkspaces runs again — a transient blip leaves a red banner until manual retry.
findings.evidence: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\renderer\stores\content-store.ts:69-71/84-86/96-98/107-109/118-120 (console.error only, no error state, silent failures); settings-store.ts:41-43/51-53 (updateSettings/resetSettings swallow errors); auth-store.ts:124-126 (clearError exists, not auto-invoked); WorkspacesPage.tsx:27,51 (local error sticky until reload)
findings.impact: Two opposite failure modes both hurt non-technical users: (a) silent failures (settings save, report list load) give no feedback so users don't know something broke; (b) sticky errors leave a stale red banner after the problem has passed. Both generate support requests.
findings.recommendation: Standardize: every async action should set error on failure AND clear it on success. Surface settings-save and content-load failures in the UI (toast) instead of console.error-only. Auto-clear transient errors on the next successful operation.

findings.title: recordItemOpened awaits two sequential refresh round-trips, delaying navigation on every open
findings.description: content-store.recordItemOpened (lines 123-138) does: await recordOpen, then `await loadRecentItems()` then `await loadFrequentItems()` sequentially (lines 132-134) — three serial IPC/network round-trips. Callers (SearchDialog.handleNavigate line 69, WorkspacesPage.openReport/openDashboard lines 133/145, HomePage.handleOpenItem line 42) AWAIT recordItemOpened BEFORE calling navigate(). So every time a user opens a report, navigation is blocked behind three serial round-trips. On a slow link this is a visible multi-hundred-ms-to-seconds stall before the report view appears, for what is purely background bookkeeping.
findings.evidence: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\renderer\stores\content-store.ts:123-138 (await recordOpen; await loadRecentItems(); await loadFrequentItems() serial); C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\renderer\components\search\SearchDialog.tsx:69 + 78 (await recordItemOpened before navigate); WorkspacesPage.tsx:133-140 / 145-152; HomePage.tsx:42-47
findings.impact: Perceptible lag opening any report/dashboard — the most common action in the app. Users on a slow tenant connection experience a freeze between click and report load, attributed to the app being 'slow.'
findings.recommendation: Don't block navigation on usage recording: fire recordItemOpened without await (or navigate first, record after). Parallelize the refresh with Promise.all(loadRecentItems(), loadFrequentItems()) instead of serial awaits, and don't await it in the navigation handlers.

summary: The embed lifecycle is built on a fabricated token-expiry clock and brittle string-matched error classification, so the app's two main recovery paths (proactive refresh on visibility, reactive refresh on embed error) are both unreliable. Worse, ReportViewer deliberately swallows every non-token embed error, so the single most common failure mode for 20 non-technical users — a report that fails to render — produces a permanent blank screen with no message and no retry. DashboardViewer and PresentationMode have no proactive token refresh at all and depend entirely on the unreliable error classifier. Multiple getPages/setPage edge cases (zero visible pages, async embed swap races) leave the embed stuck or silently mis-navigated. This is not Monday-ready: expect blank reports, infinite spinners, and stale "1 hour" sessions that die without explanation on long-running presentation displays.

findings.title: Embed token expiration is hardcoded to 1 hour, unrelated to the real AAD token lifetime
findings.description: getEmbedToken returns the raw MSAL access token as the 'embed token' but stamps it with a fabricated expiration of `new Date(Date.now() + 3600000)` regardless of the token's actual `exp`. MSAL access tokens are frequently shorter-lived (or get rotated) and the true expiry is whatever AAD issued — not a flat 60 minutes. Every viewer copies this fictional value into `tokenExpirationRef` and `isTokenExpiringSoon()` computes 'expiring soon' against a clock that has nothing to do with reality. The proactive refresh therefore fires on the wrong schedule: it can fire too late (real token already dead) or never (visibility never changes during a kiosk presentation).
findings.evidence: src/main/services/powerbi-api.ts:368-375 (`expiration: new Date(Date.now() + 3600000).toISOString()`); consumed in ReportViewer.tsx:353 / DashboardViewer.tsx:161 / PresentationMode.tsx:445; checked in isTokenExpiringSoon() ReportViewer.tsx:117-121, DashboardViewer.tsx:34-38, PresentationMode.tsx:69-73
findings.impact: On a long-running presentation/kiosk display (the headline use case), the token silently expires and the proactive refresh never fires because the app believes it has up to an hour left when it may not. Visuals go blank or show auth errors mid-meeting with no warning. Recovery depends entirely on the brittle reactive error path below.
findings.recommendation: Have getAccessToken/getEmbedToken surface the real token expiry from MSAL's AuthenticationResult.expiresOn and return that as `expiration`. Do not invent a duration. Add a guard so that if the real lifetime is short, the viewers schedule a timer-based refresh rather than relying solely on visibilitychange.

verification.reasoning: Verified powerbi-api.ts:368-375: getEmbedToken does stamp expiration: new Date(Date.now()+3600000), a flat fabricated 60 minutes. Confirmed auth-service.ts:272-320: getAccessToken returns only result.accessToken from acquireTokenSilent and discards the real MSAL expiresOn, so the true expiry is available but not propagated. The isTokenExpiringSoon() checks in ReportViewer.tsx:117-121, DashboardViewer.tsx:34-38, PresentationMode.tsx:69-73 all compare against this fake timestamp, and the proactive refresh only fires on a visibilitychange event (ReportViewer.tsx:154-165, PresentationMode.tsx:374-385) — so for a kiosk that never changes visibility, the proactive path indeed effectively never fires. That much of the claim is accurate. HOWEVER, the claimed critical impact (visuals go blank mid-meeting, recovery depends on a brittle path) is overstated and misses the actual primary defense: every viewer registers report.on('error')/dashboard.on('error') handlers (ReportViewer.tsx:433, PresentationMode.tsx:505-511, DashboardViewer.tsx:183-192) that detect isTokenExpiredError and call refreshEmbedToken. The Power BI embed SDK raises a TokenExpired error keyed off the REAL token, independent of the fabricated clock, so when the actual token dies the app refreshes automatically. refreshEmbedToken in turn calls acquireTokenSilent, which transparently returns/mints a valid token. So the system recovers regardless of the fake expiration; the fabricated value only degrades the opportunistic proactive refresh, it does not cause unrecoverable auth failures. This is a real data-correctness/quality defect (should propagate MSAL expiresOn) but not a critical production bug.
verification.notes: Legitimate fix: thread the real expiresOn from acquireTokenSilent through getAccessToken -> getEmbedToken -> EmbedToken.expiration so isTokenExpiringSoon() is accurate. Files: src/main/auth/auth-service.ts:295-301, src/main/services/powerbi-api.ts:367-375, src/shared/types.ts:64-67. The SDK error-event fallback (report.on('error') -> isTokenExpiredError -> refreshEmbedToken) is the real safety net and is correctly wired in all three viewers.

findings.title: ReportViewer swallows every non-token embed error — failed reports show a permanent blank screen
findings.description: The report 'error' handler only acts on token-expired errors; for everything else it logs to console and intentionally returns without setting `error` state or `isLoading=false`. If the initial embed fails for any other reason (permission denied, report deleted, capacity throttling, network blip, malformed embed URL, dataset not found), the loading overlay was already dismissed only by the 'loaded' event — which never fires on a failed embed — so the user is left with the invisible embed container (`invisible` class persists because neither isLoading nor error flips) or a frozen spinner. There is no message and no retry affordance.
findings.evidence: src/renderer/components/viewer/ReportViewer.tsx:428-439 — comment 'Don\'t show error UI - let Power BI handle errors internally'; loading is only cleared in the 'loaded' handler at line 387; container visibility gated on `isLoading || error` at line 765
findings.impact: This is the most likely Monday failure for non-technical users: a report they lack permission to, or any transient embed failure, yields a blank white pane forever. They cannot tell whether the app is broken, hung, or loading. No way to recover without quitting the app.
findings.recommendation: Distinguish fatal embed failures (the report never reached 'loaded') from recoverable in-session errors. Track a 'loaded' flag; if an 'error' fires before first load, surface the error UI with the existing Try-again button. Keep silent handling only for post-load navigation/drillthrough errors.

verification.reasoning: Opened src/renderer/components/viewer/ReportViewer.tsx and traced both failure paths. There are two distinct error channels: (1) the try/catch in loadReport (lines 329-500) catches synchronous/pre-embed failures (embed-token fetch at 343-350, report-details fetch, and the synchronous powerbiService.embed() call) and DOES set error + isLoading=false (497-499), correctly rendering the error overlay with a Try again button (742-751); (2) the Power BI iframe's asynchronous 'error' event handler (428-439) handles failures reported AFTER embed() returns. The claim correctly scopes itself to channel (2). In that handler, only isTokenExpiredError (verified in src/shared/utils.ts:27-35 — matches tokenexpired/accesstokenexpired/invalidauthenticationtoken) triggers a recovery (refreshEmbedToken). Every other async embed error (permission denied, report/dataset deleted, capacity throttling, RLS failure, malformed embed) hits the final branch: console.error then return, with the explicit comment 'Don't show error UI'. It calls neither setError nor setIsLoading(false). The only other place isLoading is cleared is the 'loaded' handler (line 387), which does NOT fire when an embed fails — Power BI fires 'error' instead of 'loaded'. Since isLoading is initialized to true (line 30), the result is a permanently stuck 'Loading report...' spinner overlay (731-740) with the embed container held invisible (line 765 gates on isLoading||error), no error message, and no retry affordance. The grep for setIsLoading/setError confirms no other code path clears this state on the async-error route. The claim's only imprecision is offering 'invisible container OR frozen spinner' as alternatives; the actual deterministic outcome is the frozen spinner, which is equally bad. The defect and its user-facing impact (a permission-denied or transient embed failure yields an unrecoverable hung spinner for non-technical users) are real and present.
verification.notes: Could not refute. Real-world common triggers (no-permission report, deleted report, capacity throttle) all surface via the 'error' event, not the try/catch, so they all produce a permanent stuck spinner. Minimal fix: in the non-token-expired branch of the 'error' handler, set a user-facing error and clear isLoading so the existing error overlay + Try again button (already implemented at 742-751) renders. Severity high rather than critical: no data loss/corruption, app itself is not crashed, and the user can navigate away to other reports via the surrounding UI; impact is a confusing dead-end on the affected report only.

findings.title: Token-expiry detection relies on substring matching that misses real Power BI error shapes
findings.description: isTokenExpiredError lowercases an extracted message and checks for a fixed set of substrings ('tokenexpired', 'token expired', 'accesstokenexpired', 'invalidauthenticationtoken'). Power BI client error details are not guaranteed to contain these strings; expiry can surface as errorCode 'TokenExpired' nested differently, as HTTP 401 with no matching text, or as a generic 'PowerBIEntityNotFound'/'LoadReportFailed'. When classification misses, DashboardViewer and PresentationMode fall through to setError('Failed to load...'), and ReportViewer falls through to its silent-swallow path. Either way the automatic re-auth never triggers.
findings.evidence: src/shared/utils.ts:27-35 (isTokenExpiredError); branch sites ReportViewer.tsx:432-435, DashboardViewer.tsx:186-189, PresentationMode.tsx:508-511
findings.impact: When the fabricated 1-hour clock and the substring matcher both miss, the only safety net for an expired session is gone. Users get a dead embed (ReportViewer) or a 'Failed to load' that a re-login would fix but the app never attempts, forcing an app restart.
findings.recommendation: Match on structured fields (errorCode, level, technicalDetails) and on HTTP 401, not just free-text. As a backstop, on ANY embed error after a successful initial load, attempt one silent token refresh + setAccessToken before giving up.

verification.reasoning: Opened src/shared/utils.ts (isTokenExpiredError at lines 27-35) and the three branch sites (actual paths: src/renderer/components/viewer/ReportViewer.tsx:432, DashboardViewer.tsx:186, PresentationMode.tsx:508 -- the cited paths/line numbers were wrong). The substring matcher is real and accurately quoted; it genuinely could miss error shapes that lack the four substrings (e.g. a bare HTTP 401 or generic LoadReportFailed). getErrorMessage does inspect nested error.code/errorCode, so a nested errorCode:'TokenExpired' WOULD be caught. However the claim's impact thesis is false on two counts. (1) There is no 'fabricated 1-hour clock' -- expiry is tracked from the API-returned token expiration timestamp (tokenResponse.data.expiration, set at ReportViewer:136/353, DashboardViewer:53/161, PresentationMode:88/445). (2) The substring matcher is NOT the only safety net: each viewer has a proactive refresh path -- isTokenExpiringSoon() fires 2 minutes before the real expiry and a visibilitychange listener calls refreshEmbedToken() (ReportViewer:117-121/154-165, DashboardViewer:34-36/126, PresentationMode:69-71/376), plus an auto-refresh interval that periodically calls report.refresh(). So even if the error-event matcher misses, an expired token is refreshed proactively off its true expiration. The claimed outcome (re-auth never triggers, forced app restart) does not follow. Real but minor robustness nit, not a high-severity production bug.
verification.notes: Substring brittleness is genuine but bounded; the dominant re-auth mechanism is a proactive, real-expiration-driven visibility refresh that the claim ignores. Claim invents a non-existent 'fabricated 1-hour clock' to inflate severity.

findings.title: getDatasetRefreshInfo returns success:true on every failure, masking auth/permission errors
findings.description: The catch block logs a warning and returns `{ success: true, data: {} }`. Callers cannot distinguish 'dataset has never refreshed' from '401/403/throttled/network error'. The 'Data refreshed:' label simply never appears, which to a user looks identical to a report whose data genuinely is stale. There is no signal that the refresh-status check itself is failing.
findings.evidence: src/main/services/powerbi-api.ts:587-594 (catch returns success:true with empty data); also the no-row path at 583-586; consumed in ReportViewer.tsx:418-420
findings.impact: Operators relying on the 'Data refreshed' timestamp to trust the numbers on a wall display get no timestamp and no error — silently flying blind about data freshness. Debugging permission problems in the field is near-impossible because failures are invisible.
findings.recommendation: Return success:false with a real error code on actual API failures; reserve success:true/empty only for the genuine 'no refresh history' case. Let the UI optionally show a subtle 'refresh status unavailable' hint.

findings.title: Token refresh only fires on visibilitychange — a continuously-visible report/presentation never proactively refreshes its token
findings.description: All three viewers wire proactive refresh exclusively to the 'visibilitychange' event with isTokenExpiringSoon(). On a kiosk or presentation screen that stays foregrounded for hours, visibility never changes, so the proactive refresh path is dead code in practice. ReportViewer's auto-refresh interval calls report.refresh() (data refresh) but never setAccessToken(), so it does not renew the token. PresentationMode and DashboardViewer have no token timer at all. The session therefore degrades only when the reactive error path catches it — which, per the findings above, is unreliable.
findings.evidence: ReportViewer.tsx:154-165 + auto-refresh interval 168-183 (refresh only, no setAccessToken); DashboardViewer.tsx:124-135 (visibilitychange only, and it does a full reload not setAccessToken); PresentationMode.tsx:374-385 (visibilitychange only) and auto-refresh 355-372 (refresh only)
findings.impact: The exact unattended-display scenario this app is for is the one with no working proactive token renewal. Presentations left running past the real token lifetime go blank with no automatic recovery.
findings.recommendation: Add a setInterval that checks isTokenExpiringSoon() (against a REAL expiry) every ~30-60s regardless of visibility, and calls refreshEmbedToken() which does setAccessToken(). Apply uniformly to all three viewers.

verification.reasoning: I opened all three viewers and confirmed the mechanical core of the claim. ReportViewer.tsx: proactive refreshEmbedToken() (which calls setAccessToken at line 140) is wired ONLY to visibilitychange (lines 154-165); the auto-refresh interval (168-183) calls only report.refresh() with no setAccessToken. DashboardViewer.tsx: proactive refresh only via visibilitychange (124-135), refreshEmbedToken does a full loadDashboard() reload (54-55) rather than setAccessToken; no data-refresh interval exists. PresentationMode.tsx: proactive refresh only via visibilitychange (374-385), refreshEmbedToken does setAccessToken (92); auto-refresh interval (355-372) is data-only. I grepped for setInterval in all three and found no timer anywhere that calls isTokenExpiringSoon()/refreshEmbedToken() — so the assertion that proactive token renewal is dead code on a continuously-foregrounded kiosk is CORRECT and correctly located. However, the claimed impact ('goes blank with no automatic recovery') is overstated: every viewer wires a reactive recovery path — report.on('error') -> isTokenExpiredError(errorDetail) -> refreshEmbedToken() (ReportViewer 432-435, DashboardViewer 186-189, PresentationMode 505-514). So when the token actually expires and a visual errors, automatic recovery IS attempted. The claim's worst-case outcome only holds if that reactive path is itself broken, which it defers to a separate finding ('per the findings above, unreliable') — not provable within this finding's own scope. Real defect (missing proactive timer), but a working fallback exists, so impact is less than stated.
verification.notes: Files: src/renderer/components/viewer/ReportViewer.tsx, DashboardViewer.tsx, PresentationMode.tsx. A clean fix would be a setInterval (e.g. every 60s) that calls isTokenExpiringSoon() && refreshEmbedToken(), independent of visibilitychange, in all three viewers. The isTokenExpiringSoon helper and refreshEmbedToken already exist in each file, so this is a small, low-risk addition.

findings.title: DashboardViewer token refresh and manual refresh do a full re-embed instead of setAccessToken, causing flicker and double-embed risk
findings.description: Unlike ReportViewer (which calls setAccessToken on the live embed), DashboardViewer.refreshEmbedToken() and handleRefresh() both just set isLoadingRef=false and call loadDashboard() again. loadDashboard() calls powerbiService.reset() then embed() synchronously, but the embed-token fetch is awaited in between. If a refresh is triggered while a prior load is mid-flight (e.g. visibilitychange fires during initial load), the isLoadingRef guard may have already been cleared, and two embed() calls can target the same container. Even in the happy path the user sees a full spinner + re-render rather than a seamless token swap.
findings.evidence: DashboardViewer.tsx:40-62 (refreshEmbedToken calls loadDashboard), :209-212 (handleRefresh same), :149 reset then :172 embed with awaited token fetch between; contrast ReportViewer.tsx:139-141 which uses setAccessToken+refresh
findings.impact: Dashboards visibly reload/flicker on every token refresh, and there is a window for an orphaned/double embed if refresh and load overlap. On a wall display this looks like the app glitching.
findings.recommendation: Use the live-handle setAccessToken() pattern (as ReportViewer does) for token refresh; reserve full re-embed for genuine reload. Guard load() against concurrent invocation with a single in-flight promise, not just a boolean that callers reset.

findings.title: isLoadingRef is reset in cleanup and by callers, so it does not actually prevent overlapping embeds across param changes
findings.description: isLoadingRef is meant to prevent double-embed in StrictMode, but it is cleared in three places: the effect cleanup (set to false on unmount/param change), handleRefresh/refreshEmbedToken (set to false to 'allow reload'), and the catch block. Because loadReport() awaits multiple IPC calls (getReports, getEmbedToken) before calling embed(), a navigation that changes workspaceId/reportId triggers cleanup (reset + isLoadingRef=false) while the prior loadReport() is still awaiting. The in-flight load then resumes and calls embed() on a container that the new effect is also embedding into — classic double-embed / orphaned iframe. reportRef.current gets overwritten and the older embed leaks. powerbi.reset() in cleanup races the still-pending embed().
findings.evidence: ReportViewer.tsx:83-96 (cleanup resets ref), :143-144 and :509-511 (callers reset ref), loadReport awaits at :334 and :343 before embed at :378; same shape in PresentationMode.tsx:216-241 and DashboardViewer.tsx:109-122
findings.impact: Rapidly switching between reports (which non-technical users will do by clicking around) can leave orphaned Power BI iframes and stale event handlers in the DOM, degrading memory and occasionally rendering the wrong report or a frozen pane. Hard to reproduce, easy to hit in a 20-user fleet.
findings.recommendation: Track an in-flight load with an AbortController/generation counter captured at load start; after each await, bail if the generation no longer matches (param changed) BEFORE calling embed(). Don't rely on a boolean that multiple code paths reset.

verification.reasoning:
The core race is real. ReportViewer is mounted at route /report/:workspaceId/:reportId with no key tied to params (App.tsx:111-114), so a report A -> report B navigation reuses the same component instance and just re-runs the [workspaceId,reportId] effect. The effect cleanup (ReportViewer.tsx:89-96) sets isLoadingRef.current=false, so the guard at line 83 does NOT stop the new effect from starting a fresh loadReport() while the prior one is still in flight. loadReport (317-501) awaits getReports (334) and getEmbedToken (343) before embed() (378) and never re-checks isLoadingRef or whether params still match after the awaits. Because loadReport is a plain closure (not useCallback), the stale call carries report A's id and embeds A. So loadReport(A) and loadReport(B) can both reach embed() on the same container; reportRef.current ends up pointing at whichever ran last and the pane can show the wrong report relative to the URL. The trigger is reachable in production, not just StrictMode: SearchDialog is rendered globally above <Routes> (App.tsx:58) and navigates directly to /report/... (SearchDialog.tsx:81), and DashboardViewer tile-clicks also navigate report->report (DashboardViewer.tsx:197). The same shape is present in PresentationMode.tsx (216-241, loadReport 419-467) and DashboardViewer.tsx (109-122, loadDashboard 137-175).

However the claimed IMPACT is overstated. All embeds target a single shared container div; powerbi-client embedInternal (node_modules/powerbi-client/dist/powerbi.js:11398-11411) reuses the existing iframe via embedExisting when element.powerBiEmbed is set, and every loadReport calls powerbiService.reset() first. So iframes do not accumulate unboundedly across navigations -- the "orphaned iframes degrading memory across a 20-user fleet" claim does not hold. The realistic worst case is a transient wrong-report flash or briefly stale pane during the sub-second IPC window when A's embed happens to land after B's, and it self-corrects on the next clean navigation. No crash, no data corruption, no security impact. A generation/abort guard (capture a local request id or re-check params after the awaits) is the correct fix, but this is an occasional transient UI glitch, not a high-severity production defect.
verification.notes: Files: src/renderer/components/viewer/ReportViewer.tsx (effect 75-97, loadReport 317-501, awaits 334/343, embed 378), PresentationMode.tsx (216-242, 419-467), DashboardViewer.tsx (102-122, 137-175); App.tsx:58 (global SearchDialog), :111-114 (no key); SearchDialog.tsx:81. powerbi-client reuse logic at node_modules/powerbi-client/dist/powerbi.js:11398-11411 mitigates the memory-leak claim.

findings.title: Reports with zero visible pages (all hidden) leave navigation and presentation silently non-functional
findings.description: getPages() results are filtered with `p.visibility !== 1` to drop hidden pages. If every page is hidden (common for report-as-bookmark or tooltip-only reports), pages becomes []. In ReportViewer navigateToPage early-returns and fullscreen arrows do nothing with no feedback. In PresentationMode the slides effect falls back to pages (also empty), slidesReady stays false, autoStart never engages, and the slide counter shows '(1 / 0)'. The presentation is entered (fullscreen) but is a dead, controlless black screen aside from the chrome.
findings.evidence: ReportViewer.tsx:392 filter, navigateToPage guard :187; PresentationMode.tsx:477-483 filter, slide-build fallback :151-160 (falls back to the same empty pages), counter render :591 ('/ {slides.length}')
findings.impact: Certain valid reports turn the slideshow into an empty fullscreen with no slides and no obvious exit besides Esc; users perceive the app as frozen/broken.
findings.recommendation: If the filtered page list is empty, fall back to including hidden pages or show an explicit 'This report has no displayable pages' message with an exit/back action. Never enter fullscreen presentation with zero slides.

findings.title: getPages timing and single-page reports cause inconsistent fullscreen hint and wrap-around navigation surprises
findings.description: Fullscreen hint and indicators key off pages.length, but getPages() is fetched asynchronously in the 'loaded' handler. If the user hits Full Screen before 'loaded' resolves, pages is [] and the hint logic (pages.length > 1) is skipped; entering fullscreen first then loading does not re-evaluate the hint. For single-page reports, ArrowRight/ArrowLeft still call navigateToPage with modulo wrap that resolves to the same page (harmless) but PresentationMode's space/arrow advance on a single slide does nothing visible, which can read as unresponsive.
findings.evidence: ReportViewer.tsx:213-218 (hint gated on pages.length at fullscreen-enter time), getPages in loaded handler :391; PresentationMode wrap math :294-300
findings.impact: Minor UX confusion: keyboard navigation appears dead on single-page or not-yet-loaded reports. Low severity but contributes to the 'is it broken?' perception.
findings.recommendation: Re-show the hint when pages arrive while already in fullscreen; suppress arrow handling (or show a brief 'only one page' toast) when length<=1.

findings.title: Bookmark apply and setPage failures in presentation are logged-only — slideshow advances the counter but the screen does not change
findings.description: In PresentationMode the slide-change effect calls setPage() or bookmarksManager.apply() and only console.error on rejection. The currentSlideIndex still advances (state already changed), so the indicator/counter moves to the next slide while the embedded report stays on the previous page/bookmark. During unattended auto-advance this desyncs the label from what's shown. Bookmarks captured into slides carry no state (the build at :143-148 omits state), relying on apply-by-name; if names collide or a bookmark was deleted server-side, apply silently fails.
findings.evidence: PresentationMode.tsx:337-352 (apply/setPage .catch console only), slide build omits state :143-148 vs ReportBookmark.state captured at :492-496 but unused
findings.impact: Auto-running presentations can show a slide whose title says 'Q3 Summary' while the actual visual is still 'Q2', with zero indication anything failed. Erodes trust in an unattended display.
findings.recommendation: On apply/setPage failure, either revert currentSlideIndex or surface a non-fatal on-screen indicator; prefer applying bookmarks by captured state where available rather than by name only.

findings.title: PDF export fallback can leave the live report with panes permanently hidden if an await throws mid-fallback
findings.description: In the API-unavailable fallback branch, handleExportPdf hides filters/pageNavigation panes via updateSettings, screenshots, then restores them. The restore is NOT in a finally relative to the fallback screenshot await: if window.electronAPI.export.currentViewToPdf throws (rejects, not returns error), control jumps to the outer catch (line 628) and the `if (hidPanes)` restore block at :615-627 is skipped entirely. The embedded report is left with no filter pane and no page navigation until the next full reload. The 500ms settle timeout (:591) is also a guess that can screenshot mid-transition.
findings.evidence: ReportViewer.tsx:580-627 — restore block at 615-627 is sequential after the throwing await at 601-605, not in finally; outer catch at 628 bypasses it
