findings.impact: A failed screenshot-fallback export silently strips the report's navigation/filter UI for the rest of the session, confusing users who then can't change pages or filters and don't know why.
findings.recommendation: Wrap the pane hide/restore in try/finally so restore always runs, independent of the screenshot outcome. Consider awaiting a render/rendered event instead of a fixed 500ms.

findings.title: PDF export API-vs-fallback branch decides via fragile substring matching on the error message
findings.description: The decision to fall back from the server-side ExportTo API to the screenshot capture hinges on isExportFeatureUnavailable() matching 'featurenotavailable'/'feature not available'/'404' in the error text. ExportTo is unavailable for genuinely many reasons (capacity SKU lacking export, PaginatedReport vs PowerBIReport mismatch, throttling 429, 403 permission). A 403/429/timeout does NOT match, so instead of falling back to the screenshot the user just gets the raw API error string in the status toast and no PDF.
findings.evidence: ReportViewer.tsx:534-538 (isExportFeatureUnavailable), branch at :574-578; server export at powerbi-api.ts:384-491 surfaces 'Export request failed: <status>' and 'Export timed out'
findings.impact: On Pro/shared-capacity tenants (where ExportTo often isn't licensed and returns 401/403 rather than 404), export appears simply broken instead of falling back to the working screenshot path. Likely for several of the 20 users depending on tenant licensing.
findings.recommendation: Fall back to the screenshot capture on a broader class of API failures (any non-CANCELLED failure), or detect capacity/permission errors explicitly and route them to the fallback rather than the error toast.

findings.title: 1-minute auto data-refresh can race the token refresh and fire refresh() on a report whose token just expired
findings.description: The default auto-refresh interval is 1 minute (autoRefreshIntervalMinutes initialized to 1). report.refresh() is called on a fixed timer with errors swallowed. It does not coordinate with refreshEmbedToken: if the token has expired (real expiry, not the fake 1h), refresh() will fail with an auth error that is .catch()-swallowed, leaving no data update and no token renewal attempt. Conversely, a refresh() firing during an in-flight setAccessToken() can hit the embed mid-token-swap. Nothing serializes these two timers.
findings.evidence: ReportViewer.tsx:33 (default 1), interval 168-183 (refresh, errors swallowed); PresentationMode.tsx:66 default 1, interval 355-372; token refresh setAccessToken+refresh in refreshEmbedToken with no shared lock vs the interval
findings.impact: A very aggressive 1-minute refresh that silently no-ops once the token is stale produces a display that looks live (refreshing) but is actually frozen on old data, with auth failures hidden in the console. On a wall display nobody sees the console.
findings.recommendation: Before each auto-refresh tick, check isTokenExpiringSoon() and renew the token first; share a single in-flight lock between the data-refresh and token-refresh paths. Reconsider a 1-minute default (heavy on capacity and on the API).

findings.title: PresentationMode has two independent exit paths (doExit and fullscreenchange) that can both navigate, and Esc handling double-fires
findings.description: Esc both triggers doExit() (keydown handler) AND, because exiting fullscreen via Esc also fires fullscreenchange, the fullscreenchange handler runs its own reset+navigate. doExit guards with isExitingRef and fullscreenchange checks it, so the second is usually suppressed — but the ordering is not guaranteed across browsers/Electron, and both call powerbiService.reset() and navigate(). If hasEnteredFullscreen.current never became true (requestFullscreen rejected, common when not user-gesture-initiated on mount), the fullscreenchange exit path is disabled, so Esc-to-exit only works via the keydown path and a failed-fullscreen presentation can't be exited by the Escape-exits-fullscreen affordance users expect.
findings.evidence: PresentationMode.tsx:174-206 (doExit) vs :246-280 (fullscreenchange exit), requestFullscreen on mount :222-225 (.catch swallowed, hasEnteredFullscreen only set on success), Esc keydown :302-304
findings.impact: Inconsistent exit behavior: usually fine, but when auto-fullscreen-on-mount is blocked (frequent, since it's not always a user gesture in HashRouter navigation) the presentation lacks the fullscreen-escape exit, and reset()/navigate() can double-run in edge orderings.
findings.recommendation: Funnel all exits through a single idempotent doExit(); drive the fullscreenchange handler to call doExit() rather than duplicating its body. Ensure an always-available Exit button/Esc path regardless of whether fullscreen was entered.

findings.title: Loading spinner is cleared only by the 'loaded' event, so an embed that errors before load shows an infinite spinner
findings.description: In all three viewers setIsLoading(false) happens inside the 'loaded' event handler. If the embed never reaches 'loaded' (network failure to app.powerbi.com, blocked iframe, bad embed URL, immediate auth rejection), 'loaded' never fires. In DashboardViewer/PresentationMode the 'error' handler at least sets error for unclassified errors, but in ReportViewer the error handler deliberately does nothing for non-token errors — so the spinner spins forever. There is also no timeout that converts a never-loading embed into an error state.
findings.evidence: ReportViewer.tsx:386-387 (loaded clears loading) with error swallow :437-438 and no timeout; DashboardViewer.tsx:179-181; PresentationMode.tsx:471-472
findings.impact: Any environment where app.powerbi.com is slow/blocked (corporate proxy, offline, captive portal) gives non-technical users an eternal 'Loading report...' spinner with no error and no retry. Indistinguishable from a hang.
findings.recommendation: Add a load watchdog timeout (e.g. 30-45s) that, if neither 'loaded' nor a fatal 'error' has fired, flips to the error UI with a retry. Always clear the spinner on fatal error in ReportViewer.

verification.reasoning:
I opened all three viewers and confirmed the core code claims. ReportViewer.tsx: setIsLoading(false) lives only in the report.on('loaded') handler (line 387), and the report.on('error') handler (lines 428-439) explicitly returns without setError/setIsLoading for any non-token-expired error ("Don't show error UI" comment at 437-438). I grepped the whole file for setTimeout/timeout: the only timeouts are for PDF export (112), fullscreen hint (217), focus maintenance (273-279), and an export delay (591) — there is NO watchdog timeout that converts a never-loading embed into an error. So if the iframe embeds successfully but never reaches 'loaded' (dead network to app.powerbi.com, blocked iframe, captive portal), or emits a non-token error, the ReportViewer spinner (lines 731-740, "Loading report...") spins forever with no error and no retry. The "Try again" button (746) only renders when error is set, which never happens in that path. That part of the claim is fully confirmed and is a real production defect for offline/proxy/captive-portal users.

Why partly-true rather than confirmed: the claim overstates two things. (1) Its cross-viewer framing ("in all three viewers") is wrong about impact — DashboardViewer.tsx:183-192 and PresentationMode.tsx:505-514 DO call setError(...) + setIsLoading(false) in the unclassified-error branch, so those two recover whenever the SDK emits an error event; only ReportViewer swallows it. (2) Several failure examples it lists (immediate auth rejection, bad embed URL, failure to get embed token) are actually caught by the try/catch wrapping loadReport (ReportViewer 329-500, catch at 496-500 sets error + clears spinner), because those fail during the awaited token fetch BEFORE powerbiService.embed() — they are NOT subject to the infinite spinner. The genuinely-uncovered case is a post-embed stall where the iframe never reaches 'loaded' and no error event fires (or fires non-token in ReportViewer). That residual case is real and unhandled in all three (no timeout anywhere), and is fully unhandled-plus-swallowed in ReportViewer.

Net: the central defect (infinite spinner on a stalled/non-token-errored embed, no timeout, no retry) is genuine — most acutely in ReportViewer — so severity high stands, but the claim's "all three viewers" impact and some of its cited trigger examples are inaccurate.
verification.notes: Fix suggestion: add a watchdog timeout (e.g. 30-60s after embed) that, if 'loaded'/'rendered' has not fired, sets an error + clears the spinner; and in ReportViewer's error handler, set an error state for non-token errors that occur before first load instead of silently swallowing. Files: src/renderer/components/viewer/ReportViewer.tsx (loadReport ~317-501, error handler 428-439, loading render 731-740), DashboardViewer.tsx:179-192, PresentationMode.tsx:471-514.

dimension: Resilience & error handling (transient failures, timeouts, retries, rate limits, pagination, offline, stuck spinners, unhandled rejections)
summary: This app has essentially zero resilience engineering. Across the entire data layer there is not a single retry, not a single fetch() timeout, and not a single 429/rate-limit handler — yet getAllItems and WorkspacesPage deliberately fan out concurrent requests against Power BI's heavily-throttled REST API. The renderer's most important surface, the report/dashboard embed, gates its spinner exclusively on a Power BI 'loaded' event that may never fire, so the single most common failure mode for 20 non-technical users will be a permanent spinner with no error and no recovery short of force-quitting. Offline/disconnect is completely unmodeled: there is no navigator.onLine check, no detection of network loss, and console.error is the terminal destination for a large class of failures the user can never see. The few error paths that do exist surface raw, unactionable strings ("TypeError: Failed to fetch", "Power BI API error: 429 - ..."). For a Monday rollout to non-technical users this is the highest-risk dimension in the codebase: the app will frequently appear frozen or silently broken with no path to recovery and no diagnostics.

findings.title: Report/Dashboard spinner hangs forever when the Power BI embed never fires 'loaded'
findings.description: In ReportViewer.loadReport (and identically in DashboardViewer and PresentationMode), isLoading is only set false inside the powerbi-client report.on('loaded', ...) callback. The try/catch around loadReport only catches synchronous throws from embed() and the token fetch — it does NOT catch the case where embed() succeeds but the embedded iframe never reaches 'loaded' (slow tenant, throttled visuals, blocked frame, a transient network drop during the embed handshake, or a Power BI-side error that fires 'error' but isTokenExpiredError() returns false). The 'error' handler in ReportViewer explicitly comments 'Don't show error UI' and returns without ever calling setIsLoading(false). There is no watchdog timeout on the embed.
findings.evidence: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\renderer\components\viewer\ReportViewer.tsx lines 386-387 (setIsLoading(false) only in 'loaded'), lines 428-439 ('error' handler returns without clearing spinner), no timeout in loadReport (317-501). Same pattern: DashboardViewer.tsx 179-192; PresentationMode.tsx 471-514.
findings.impact: This is the single most likely thing 20 non-technical users hit. Open a report on a busy tenant or a flaky network and the spinner 'Loading report...' spins indefinitely. There is no error, no Try again button (it is gated behind error state which never sets), and the embed div stays invisible. The user's only escape is to force-kill the app. They will call this 'the app is frozen / broken.'
findings.recommendation: Add a watchdog: after embed(), start a setTimeout (e.g. 30-45s) that, if 'loaded' has not fired, sets an actionable error and clears isLoading; clear the timeout in the 'loaded' handler. Also, in the 'error' handler, when the error is not a token error, set a visible error state + clear the spinner instead of swallowing it.

verification.reasoning: I opened all three cited files. ReportViewer.tsx: confirmed exactly as claimed. setIsLoading(false) appears only inside report.on('loaded') (line 387). The try/catch (329-500) only wraps synchronous throws from the token fetch and embed(); once embed() returns, the catch is out of scope. The error handler (428-439) explicitly comments "Don't show error UI" and returns without setIsLoading(false) for any non-token-expired error (isTokenExpiredError in shared/utils.ts only matches token-expired strings). There is NO watchdog timeout in loadReport (the only setTimeout uses are export-status line 112 and fullscreen-hint line 217). The spinner overlay (731) and the embed div's invisible class (765) are gated on isLoading, and Try-again (746) is gated on error which never sets — so for ReportViewer the spinner genuinely hangs forever if loaded never fires (or a non-token error fires). That part of the claim is correct and is a real defect with no user recovery short of killing the app. HOWEVER, the claim's "same pattern" assertion for DashboardViewer (179-192) and PresentationMode (505-514) is WRONG: both of those error handlers DO call setError(...) + setIsLoading(false) for non-token errors, so a Power BI error event there clears the spinner and shows Try-again. Only ReportViewer suppresses error events. All three still share the narrower silent-stall gap (loaded never fires AND no error event is emitted) because none has a load watchdog. Because the most common embed failures (auth/token/config) actually surface as 'error' events — which Dashboard/Presentation recover from, and which even in ReportViewer represent the common case the claim mislabels as unrecoverable — the "single most likely thing 20 users hit / app is frozen" framing overstates impact. The genuine unrecoverable case is the tail where embed() resolves but 'loaded' silently never arrives. Real defect, missing watchdog, worst in ReportViewer; downgraded from critical to high and from "identical across all three" to "ReportViewer fully, Dashboard/Presentation only the silent-stall tail."
verification.notes: Fix: add a load watchdog timeout in each loadReport/loadDashboard that, if neither 'loaded' nor a handled error has fired within N seconds, sets an error state with a Try-again path. Additionally, ReportViewer's error handler should clear the spinner for non-recoverable errors the way Dashboard/Presentation already do. Cited files: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\renderer\components\viewer\ReportViewer.tsx (386-387, 428-439, 731-767), DashboardViewer.tsx (179-192), PresentationMode.tsx (471-514).

findings.title: No fetch() in the app has a timeout — any hung connection hangs the whole operation indefinitely
findings.description: Every network call in powerbi-api.ts uses bare fetch() with no AbortController/signal and no timeout. makeRequest, makeRequestWithUrl, exportReportToPdf (export POST, the poll loop, and the file download) all rely on the OS/Chromium default, which for a half-open TCP connection (VPN drop, captive portal, server accepting but never responding) can be minutes or effectively never. The export poll loop bounds itself to 30 attempts x 2s of *status* calls but each individual fetch within it is unbounded.
findings.evidence: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\main\services\powerbi-api.ts: fetch at lines 27, 52, 416, 444, 472 — none pass a signal/timeout. Poll loop 443-466 bounds attempts but not per-request duration.
findings.impact: If a user's network goes half-open (common on corporate VPNs and hotel/cafe wifi), opening any list or report leaves the upstream IPC promise pending. The renderer spinner (workspaces/apps/report) never resolves, never errors. Same indefinite-hang user experience as the embed case, but originating in main. For 20 mixed-network users this will recur.
findings.recommendation: Wrap all fetch calls with AbortController + a timeout (e.g. 20-30s), and translate AbortError into a clear 'The request timed out — check your connection and try again' error. Apply to makeRequest, makeRequestWithUrl, and all three export fetches.

verification.reasoning: I read C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\main\services\powerbi-api.ts in full. All five cited fetch() calls are bare: makeRequest (line 27), makeRequestWithUrl (line 52), exportReportToPdf's ExportTo POST (line 416), the poll-loop status GET (line 444), and the file download (line 472) each pass only headers/method/body and no signal. A project-wide grep for AbortController/AbortSignal/signal:/timeout across src returned zero abort-signal usages anywhere; the only setTimeout in powerbi-api.ts is the 2s poll delay at line 465, which the claim already acknowledges. The poll loop (443-466) bounds attempts (30 x 2s) but each individual fetch inside it is unbounded, exactly as claimed. I also checked the renderer (ReportViewer.tsx): the exportTimeoutRef is only a 4s status-banner dismissal (lines 107-115), not a bound on the IPC promise, so a hung main-process fetch leaves the renderer spinner pending with no error path. So the core technical claim is correct. I downgrade severity because: (a) this is a reliability/UX defect, not correctness/data/security — no crash, corruption, or leak, and the user can restart; (b) Electron main uses undici fetch, which has default headersTimeout/bodyTimeout that fire on stalled header/body delivery, providing a partial backstop in the common stalled-stream case, so 'effectively never' is overstated — the genuine unbounded windows are connection establishment and post-headers idle hangs; (c) for the stated 20-user deployment it is a recurring annoyance rather than a catastrophic failure.
verification.notes: Real defect: add an AbortController with a per-request timeout (and an overall export budget) to all fetch calls in powerbi-api.ts. Note undici's headersTimeout/bodyTimeout defaults provide a partial backstop, which is why this is medium not high; connect-phase and post-headers idle hangs remain unbounded.

findings.title: Zero retry on transient failures (5xx, network blips, throttling) anywhere in the stack
findings.description: There is no retry/backoff logic in powerbi-api.ts, auth-service, or any store. A single transient failure — a 503 from the Power BI gateway, a momentary DNS/socket error, a token-refresh hiccup — turns the entire operation into a hard, user-visible failure. acquireTokenSilent in auth-service likewise has no retry; one transient failure there propagates as TOKEN_FAILED and every downstream API call fails.
findings.evidence: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\main\services\powerbi-api.ts makeRequest 20-40 throws immediately on !response.ok with no retry; auth-service.ts getAccessToken 272-320 single attempt; no backoff utility imported anywhere in src.
findings.impact: Transient cloud errors are routine, not exceptional, especially first thing Monday morning when 20 users authenticate and enumerate workspaces simultaneously. Without retry, normal cloud flakiness presents as 'it failed, try again' (best case) or a stuck spinner (worst case). Users will perceive the app as unreliable even when Power BI itself is fine 2 seconds later.
findings.recommendation: Add a small retry-with-exponential-backoff+jitter wrapper for idempotent GETs (workspaces/reports/dashboards/apps/refresh-info) on 5xx, 429, and network errors (e.g. 3 attempts). Retry the export status-poll GETs too. Do NOT blindly retry the export POST (non-idempotent) without care.

verification.reasoning: I read powerbi-api.ts and auth-service.ts and grepped src for retry/backoff/exponential. The code matches the claim. makeRequest (lines 20-40) does a single fetch and throws immediately on !response.ok with no retry and no special handling for 429/5xx; its twin makeRequestWithUrl (45-65) is identical and used by all the paginated list calls (getWorkspaces/getReports/getDashboards/getApps via fetchAllPages). exportReportToPdf likewise throws hard on the first non-ok status response (448-451). auth-service getAccessToken (272-320) makes a single acquireTokenSilent call (295); the catch only special-cases InteractionRequiredAuthError and re-throws everything else (312), which becomes TOKEN_FAILED (317) and fails every downstream API call since makeRequest calls getAccessToken first. The grep confirms there is no retry/backoff utility anywhere in src; the only setTimeout/maxAttempts hits are the export status-polling loop (which is not a retry of failed requests) and unrelated UI timers. So the factual core of the claim is correct: zero transient-failure retry across the API layer, the token layer, and any store. Where I temper it: this is a desktop Electron user-owns-data viewer, not a high-throughput backend; the failure mode is a user-visible error / re-click, not data loss or corruption, and errors are returned as structured IPCResponse rather than crashing the process. The 'stuck spinner' worst case depends on renderer handling the claim does not substantiate. Notably Power BI 429 responses carry a Retry-After header that this code ignores entirely, so under the Monday-morning concurrent-auth scenario throttling will surface as raw failures. Genuine resilience gap, worth fixing, but UX/robustness rather than a critical correctness bug.
verification.notes: Cited files: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\main\services\powerbi-api.ts (makeRequest 20-40, makeRequestWithUrl 45-65, exportReportToPdf status loop 443-466) and C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\main\auth\auth-service.ts (getAccessToken 272-320). A reasonable fix is a shared retryWithBackoff that retries on 429 (honoring Retry-After) and 5xx/network errors, applied in makeRequest/makeRequestWithUrl and around acquireTokenSilent for non-InteractionRequired errors.

findings.title: No 429/Retry-After handling while getAllItems and WorkspacesPage fan out concurrent requests
findings.description: getAllItems iterates workspaces in batches of 5, issuing getReports + getDashboards per workspace — i.e. up to 10 concurrent Power BI REST calls per batch, with batches back-to-back and fetchAllPages potentially multiplying that further. Power BI's REST API enforces aggressive per-user/per-tenant throttling and returns HTTP 429 with a Retry-After header. The code never reads Retry-After, never pauses, and never distinguishes 429 from any other error — a 429 is just thrown as 'Power BI API error: 429 - ...'. The batch comment claims it avoids rate limits but does nothing on actually hitting one. WorkspacesPage also fires getReports+getDashboards concurrently on each expand.
findings.evidence: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\main\services\powerbi-api.ts getAllItems 514-538 (BATCH_SIZE=5, Promise.all of 2 calls each), makeRequest 34-37 treats 429 like any error with no Retry-After read; WorkspacesPage.tsx 86-89 concurrent fetch on expand.
findings.impact: On a large tenant (the explicit 'thousands of items' scenario), the burst of concurrent calls will trip 429 throttling. getReports/getDashboards for the throttled workspaces silently return empty (getAllItems swallows failed batches at 531-535), so users see PARTIAL or empty content with no indication anything was throttled — reports simply 'go missing.' Worse, throttling can cascade: once throttled, subsequent calls keep 429ing because nothing backs off. Non-technical users will report 'some of my reports disappeared.'
findings.recommendation: Detect 429 specifically, honor the Retry-After header (sleep then retry), and reduce concurrency / add inter-batch delay. Track and surface partial failures from getAllItems rather than silently dropping throttled workspaces.

verification.reasoning: Opened C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\main\services\powerbi-api.ts and the cited WorkspacesPage.tsx. Every factual claim holds. makeRequest (lines 34-37) and makeRequestWithUrl (59-62) throw any non-ok response, including 429, as a generic `Power BI API error: ${response.status} - ${errorText}` with no read of the Retry-After header, no pause, and no retry/backoff. getAllItems (518-538) iterates workspaces in batches of 5, each running getReports+getDashboards via Promise.all = up to 10 concurrent REST calls per batch, batches issued back-to-back with no delay, and fetchAllPages (70-84) loops @odata.nextLink so a single workspace can multiply calls further. The `// Process 5 workspaces at a time to avoid rate limits` comment (516) only caps batch width; it does nothing when a 429 actually occurs. Critically, failed batches are silently swallowed at 530-537: only reportsResponse.success/dashboardsResponse.success results are pushed, so a throttled workspace's items are simply omitted and getAllItems still returns success:true — the user sees partial/empty content with no error surfaced ("reports go missing"). WorkspacesPage.tsx 86-89 also fires getReports+getDashboards concurrently on each expand and falls back to empty arrays on catch (102-108). A repo-wide grep for 429/Retry-After/retry/backoff/rate-limit found only the comment — confirming no handling exists anywhere. The defect is genuine. I downgrade from high to medium because the impact is gated by scale: with BATCH_SIZE=5 the burst only realistically trips Power BI throttling on large tenants (hundreds of workspaces), getAllItems runs once per load rather than in a tight loop, and the "cascade" worsening is plausible but not guaranteed. For typical tenants it never trips. The silent-swallow making items vanish without any error indication is the real, confirmable harm.

Fix should both honor Retry-After with bounded retry in makeRequest/makeRequestWithUrl and surface partial-failure to the caller instead of returning success:true with dropped data.

findings.title: No offline/disconnect detection anywhere — failures present as cryptic errors or hangs, never as 'you're offline'
findings.description: There is no navigator.onLine check, no 'online'/'offline' event listener, and no network-state handling in any store or component. When the machine is offline or loses connectivity mid-session, fetch() rejects with 'TypeError: Failed to fetch' (surfaced raw in WorkspacesPage/AppsPage via String(err)) or the embed simply never loads (stuck spinner). Token refresh on visibilitychange (used by all three viewers when returning from sleep/lock) will also fail with no offline-aware messaging.
