findings.impact: A laptop that resumes from sleep without wifi, or a user on a dropped VPN, gets either a frozen spinner or a raw 'TypeError: Failed to fetch' message — meaningless and alarming to non-technical users. They cannot tell it's just their connection. Expect support calls framed as 'the app is broken' when the real fix is 'reconnect to wifi.'
findings.recommendation: Detect offline state (navigator.onLine + offline/online events) and render a clear 'You appear to be offline — reconnect and retry' banner. Map 'Failed to fetch'/AbortError to a friendly connectivity message rather than dumping the raw error string.

verification.reasoning:
Every factual assertion in the claim verifies against the code. grep across src/ finds zero occurrences of navigator.onLine and zero online/offline event listeners (the only "offline" hit is the OAuth scope 'offline_access' in msal-config.ts, unrelated). WorkspacesPage.tsx:50-51 and AppsPage.tsx:35-36 both do `catch (err) { setError(String(err)); }`, and the error UI renders that string raw — AppsPage.tsx:74-84 shows `<Text className="text-status-error">{error}</Text>`, so a fetch failure surfaces literally as "Error: TypeError: Failed to fetch". ReportViewer.tsx:146-147 surfaces `err.message` raw in the token-refresh catch. I confirmed all three viewers (ReportViewer, DashboardViewer, PresentationMode) register a visibilitychange handler that calls token refresh, which will fail with the same non-offline-aware message when resuming offline. So the description is accurate: there is genuinely no offline/disconnect detection.

However, the HIGH severity is overstated. This is the absence of a UX nicety, not a broken or dangerous code path. Failures are caught, not crashes: the list pages (Workspaces/Apps) render a retry path with a "Try again" button (AppsPage.tsx:79) that works correctly once connectivity returns, and the visibilitychange refresh sets an error state rather than hanging. There is no data loss, no corruption, no security impact, and no permanent hang for the list pages. The single genuinely "stuck" case is the embed spinner if Power BI assets never load, but even that is recoverable by user action. The real-world harm is limited to a confusing/alarming error string for non-technical users — a messaging-quality defect. That maps to low severity (a missing-feature / poor-error-messaging gap), not high. Downgrading accordingly.
verification.notes: Relevant files: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\renderer\components\workspaces\WorkspacesPage.tsx (lines 50-51), src\renderer\components\apps\AppsPage.tsx (lines 35-36, 74-84), src\renderer\components\viewer\ReportViewer.tsx (lines 146-147, 154-165). A reasonable fix is a small offline-aware wrapper that maps "Failed to fetch"/network errors to a friendly "You appear to be offline" message plus a navigator.onLine check before token refresh — low effort, UX-only payoff.

findings.title: Many failures are swallowed into console.error/console.warn with no user feedback
findings.description: Numerous failure paths log to the console and leave the UI in a silently-degraded state with no signal to the user. In a packaged Electron build the user never opens DevTools, so these are effectively black holes. Examples: content-store loadReports/loadDashboards/loadApps/loadRecent/loadFrequent all catch and console.error only, so the Home page and search just show stale/empty data on failure. The ReportViewer 'error' handler explicitly suppresses non-token errors. Auto-refresh failures are swallowed. AppViewer details failure is swallowed.
findings.evidence: content-store.ts 69-71, 84-86, 96-98, 107-109, 118-120, 135-137 (console.error only); ReportViewer.tsx 437-438 (comment 'Don't show error UI'); ReportViewer.tsx 174-176 auto-refresh .catch swallow; AppViewer.tsx 101-103.
findings.impact: The Home page is the first thing all 20 users see. If usage:getRecent/getFrequent fail (DB locked, IPC error), the store silently keeps empty arrays and HomePage shows the 'Welcome / no usage yet' empty state — indistinguishable from a genuine first run. Users think their history vanished. Across the app, intermittent failures look like 'nothing happened,' which is the most confusing failure class for non-technical users and impossible to diagnose remotely since logs go only to an unopened console.
findings.recommendation: Surface user-facing, dismissible error/toast on these catches (at least for the Home and list loads), and write logs to a file (not just console) so support can diagnose. Stop suppressing non-token report 'error' events without at least a subtle indicator.

findings.title: Raw error strings (HTTP status + API body, stringified exceptions) shown directly to non-technical users
findings.description: Error messages are constructed as raw technical strings and passed straight to the UI. makeRequest throws `Power BI API error: ${status} - ${errorText}` where errorText is the raw OData error JSON; WorkspacesPage/AppsPage render String(err); viewers render err.message. None of these are translated to plain language or mapped by status code.
findings.evidence: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\main\services\powerbi-api.ts 36, 61; WorkspacesPage.tsx 51; AppsPage.tsx 36; ReportViewer.tsx 497, 147.
findings.impact: Users see things like 'Power BI API error: 403 - {"error":{"code":"..."}}' or 'TypeError: Failed to fetch'. For a 403 (no workspace access) the right message is 'You don't have access to this workspace'; instead they get a JSON blob. This both confuses users and generates avoidable support tickets, and may leak internal detail.
findings.recommendation: Add a central error mapper keyed on HTTP status / known codes (401/403 -> access/sign-in guidance, 404 -> not found, 429 -> 'busy, retrying', network -> connectivity) producing friendly messages; keep the raw detail in file logs only.

findings.title: Renderer has no global unhandledrejection/error handler; main process handlers only console.error
findings.description: The main process installs process.on('unhandledRejection')/('uncaughtException') but both only console.error — an uncaught exception in main leaves the app in an undefined state rather than logging-and-recovering or showing a dialog. The renderer (main.tsx/App.tsx) installs NO window.onerror or window.addEventListener('unhandledrejection'). React's ErrorBoundary only catches errors thrown during render/lifecycle — it does NOT catch async rejections (e.g. an await in an event handler, a rejected promise in a useEffect callback), which is exactly where this app's async IPC calls live.
findings.evidence: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\main\index.ts 99-100 (console.error only); ErrorBoundary.tsx 25-36 (render-time only); no 'unhandledrejection' listener anywhere in src/renderer.
findings.impact: An unhandled promise rejection in a handler (e.g. recordItemOpened chain, an export path) will not be caught by ErrorBoundary and produces no UI — it just silently fails, often leaving a button in a stuck 'Exporting...' or no-op state. In the worst case an uncaughtException in main destabilizes the process while only writing to an unread console. Hard to diagnose post-rollout.
findings.recommendation: Add window.addEventListener('unhandledrejection') and window.onerror in the renderer to log to file and show a non-fatal toast. In main, on uncaughtException consider showing an error dialog and logging to file before deciding whether to continue or relaunch.

findings.title: PDF export status poll throws on first transient status error and has no overall deadline beyond 30 polls
findings.description: exportReportToPdf polls export status up to 30 times every 2s, but a single non-ok status response throws immediately and aborts the whole export (no retry of the poll). Each fetch is unbounded (see no-fetch-timeout). The button shows 'Exporting...' and is disabled while this runs; if the underlying fetch hangs, the button stays stuck on 'Exporting...' indefinitely because isExporting is only cleared in finally after the awaited chain resolves.
findings.evidence: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\main\services\powerbi-api.ts 443-466 (throw on !ok inside poll, no retry); ReportViewer.tsx 514-633 isExporting cleared only in finally (630-632); DashboardViewer.tsx 214-247 same.
findings.impact: A user clicks Export PDF; a transient hiccup during polling aborts with a raw error, or a hung connection leaves the Export button stuck on 'Exporting...' forever, blocking re-export until app restart. Non-technical users interpret a permanently-disabled button as the feature being broken.
findings.recommendation: Make the poll loop tolerant of transient status-fetch failures (retry a few times before giving up), add per-request timeouts, and ensure the UI 'Exporting...' state cannot get stuck (timeout the whole export operation).

findings.title: Large-tenant scale: full enumeration + non-virtualized rendering risks UI freeze; no pagination/limit on getAllItems
findings.description: getAllItems enumerates every workspace's full report and dashboard list with no cap, and the consuming UI lists (WorkspacesPage expansion, search) render plain mapped arrays with no virtualization. The dataset-refresh and pages logic are fine, but on a tenant with thousands of items the combination of many sequential paginated batches (slow, throttling-prone) plus rendering thousands of DOM nodes can lock the renderer.
findings.evidence: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\main\services\powerbi-api.ts getAllItems 497-553 (no limit); WorkspacesPage.tsx 247-282 (.map over all reports/dashboards, no virtualization).
findings.impact: On the explicit 'huge tenant with thousands of items' scenario, the catalog fetch is slow and throttling-prone (see 429 finding) and the eventual render can jank or briefly freeze the window, which non-technical users read as a crash. Note the app already moved 'recent' off full enumeration for this reason (index.ts 360-362 comment), but getAllItems still does it.
findings.recommendation: Confirm where getAllItems is still invoked and cap/paginate it; consider list virtualization for large workspaces. At minimum, bound total items fetched and indicate truncation.

findings.title: Startup auth check failures dump the user to the login screen with no explanation
findings.description: auth-store.checkAuth sets isAuthenticated=false on any failure (including transient token-validation failures), and validateToken in auth-service proactively calls logout() when it can't get a token — even if the cause was transient (network blip during silent acquisition). On a transient failure at startup the user is silently signed out and bounced to the login screen.
findings.evidence: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\renderer\stores\auth-store.ts checkAuth 51-65, 66-73; auth-service.ts validateToken 79-96 (logout on INTERACTION_REQUIRED/NO_ACCOUNT) and getAccessToken 302-311 (logout inside on InteractionRequired).
findings.impact: A momentary network problem at launch (very plausible Monday morning) can silently log a user out, forcing a full re-authentication. Mildly annoying individually but, at 20 users hitting it the same morning, generates a cluster of 'it logged me out / made me sign in again' reports that look like a systemic bug.
findings.recommendation: Distinguish transient token failures from genuine interaction-required: do not logout()/clear cache on transient/network errors; retry silent acquisition, and only force re-login on a true InteractionRequiredAuthError. Surface a 'couldn't verify session, retrying' state rather than a silent bounce.

summary: This rollout is shippable-once but not operable. The single largest defect is that there is NO update mechanism whatsoever (publish:null, no electron-updater, no autoUpdater code): once 20 non-technical users install Monday's build, every subsequent bug fix requires you to manually re-distribute an installer and walk each user through uninstall/reinstall — for an app you already describe as having "a lot of bugs," that is an operational dead end. The second-largest is that the installer is unsigned, so Windows SmartScreen and possibly Defender will block or scare non-technical users on first install (and on every future re-install, since there's no other delivery path). Compounding both: there is zero crash reporting or error telemetry, so you will be blind to the bugs your users hit. Secrets handling is actually OK (only .env.example is tracked; the baked Azure IDs are non-secret public-client identifiers), and the CI/asar wiring is broadly correct, but the CI has a release-blocking flaw (npm audit --audit-level=high will fail the build given 18 high vulns) and several reproducibility/version-management gaps.

findings.title: No update mechanism at all — 20 users cannot receive any bug fix without manual reinstall
findings.description: electron-builder.yml sets `publish: null`, electron-updater is not a dependency (package.json deps list has no electron-updater), and there is no autoUpdater code anywhere in src (grep for electron-updater/autoUpdater returns nothing). The app therefore has no channel to deliver a fixed build. The CI release job publishes installers as GitHub Release assets, but the app never checks that release. For an app explicitly described as buggy, every future fix means re-sending an .exe to 20 non-technical users and talking each one through uninstall + reinstall.
findings.evidence: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\electron-builder.yml:6 (`publish: null`); package.json:21-32 (no electron-updater in dependencies); grep across src for `electron-updater|autoUpdater` -> no matches; src\main\index.ts has no updater wiring.
findings.impact: After Monday, you cannot ship a bug fix in any automated way. With 20 non-technical users, each patch becomes 20 manual support touches (uninstall, re-download, re-run unsigned installer). In practice many users will stay on the broken first build indefinitely.
findings.recommendation: Before Monday: add `electron-updater`, configure `publish:` in electron-builder.yml to the GitHub releases provider (provider: github, owner/repo), and in main process call autoUpdater.checkForUpdatesAndNotify() on app ready (guarded to production only). This makes the existing CI GitHub Release the update feed. NOTE: NSIS auto-update requires a signed build to be reliable on Windows — pair this with code signing (see unsigned-installer finding). If signing is impossible by Monday, at minimum ship an in-app 'check for updates' that opens the GitHub releases page and an explicit version banner, so users have a deterministic upgrade path.

verification.reasoning: All cited facts verified on disk. electron-builder.yml:6 has `publish: null` with comment (line 5) confirming releases are handled manually via GitHub Actions. package.json dependencies (21-32) and devDependencies (33-47) contain no electron-updater. A repo-wide case-insensitive grep for electron-updater|autoUpdater|checkForUpdate returns zero matches. src/main/index.ts exposes only `app:get-version` (line 611) returning app.getVersion() — no updater wiring, no checkForUpdates call. The CI release job (.github/workflows/build.yml:83-106) does publish installer .exe/.dmg assets to a GitHub Release via softprops/action-gh-release, but the app never queries that release. NSIS config uses oneClick:true with no differential/auto-update support. So the app genuinely has no in-app update channel; every fix requires manually redistributing and reinstalling the full installer. The technical claim is accurate. However, this is an absent feature / operational gap, not a runtime code defect — the app functions correctly as built. There is no crash, data corruption, security hole, or wrong behavior. For a 20-user internally-distributed Electron app, shipping without auto-update is a common and here apparently intentional tradeoff (the yml comment shows manual release was a deliberate design choice). The 'critical production bug' label overstates it; the real cost is maintainability/operability (manual reinstalls per patch). Confirmed as a true gap but downgraded from critical to medium.
verification.notes: Factually correct finding; severity reduced because it is a missing distribution feature / operability concern rather than a runtime defect. Mitigation: add electron-updater + a GitHub provider publish block (or a lightweight in-app "new version available" check against the GitHub Releases API) so the existing CI-published release assets can be consumed. Note unsigned installers (no CSC_LINK set) would still trigger SmartScreen even with auto-update.

findings.title: Installer is unsigned — SmartScreen/Defender will block or scare every non-technical user
findings.description: electron-builder.yml only documents the signing env vars (CSC_LINK / CSC_KEY_PASSWORD) in comments; they are never set, and the CI 'Build and package Windows' step passes only GH_TOKEN/AZURE_* — no CSC_LINK secret. So the produced .exe is unsigned. Windows SmartScreen shows a full-screen 'Windows protected your PC / Unknown publisher' warning for unsigned installers from a new publisher, and the user must click 'More info -> Run anyway'. Non-technical users routinely abandon at this screen.
findings.evidence: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\electron-builder.yml:25-32 (signing vars only in comments); .github\workflows\build.yml:34-39 (package step env has no CSC_LINK/CSC_KEY_PASSWORD).
findings.impact: On Monday, a meaningful fraction of 20 non-technical users will see a scary 'Unknown publisher' warning and either refuse to install or flood you with support requests. SmartScreen reputation also takes time to build even after you start signing, so EV/OV certs help most.
findings.recommendation: Obtain an OV (or ideally EV, which bypasses SmartScreen reputation immediately) Windows code-signing certificate, store it as a CI secret (CSC_LINK base64 + CSC_KEY_PASSWORD), and add them to the package:win step env. If a cert cannot be acquired by Monday, prepare a written click-through guide with screenshots for the 'More info -> Run anyway' flow and warn users in advance — but treat this as a stopgap, not a fix.

verification.reasoning: I opened both cited files and grepped the whole repo (excluding node_modules) for any signing config. The claim holds up factually. electron-builder.yml lines 25-29 mention CSC_LINK / CSC_KEY_PASSWORD / certificateSubjectName only inside comments; the only active Windows-signing-adjacent keys are signingHashAlgorithms: sha256 and signDlls: false, which do nothing without an actual certificate. No certificateFile, certificateSubjectName, azureSignOptions, .pfx, afterSign hook, or any signing script exists anywhere in the repo. In .github/workflows/build.yml the 'Build and package Windows' step (lines 34-39) passes only GH_TOKEN, AZURE_CLIENT_ID, AZURE_TENANT_ID — and I verified via scripts/generate-config.js and src/main/auth/msal-config.ts that those AZURE_* vars are MSAL/Entra app-auth credentials for in-app Microsoft sign-in, NOT Azure Trusted Signing credentials (no AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET, no signing endpoint/profile, no CSC_LINK secret). So the produced NSIS .exe is genuinely unsigned. The described SmartScreen / 'Unknown publisher' warning for an unsigned installer from a publisher with no established reputation is the correct real-world consequence. The guard the auditor says is missing is in fact missing; I could not refute it.
verification.notes: Downgraded critical -> high: this is a distribution/rollout and UX blocker, not a code-correctness or security defect in the running app. It won't crash, corrupt data, or introduce a vulnerability — but the abandonment/support-load impact on non-technical users is real and accurately described. Fix requires obtaining a code-signing cert (EV/OV for faster SmartScreen reputation) and wiring CSC_LINK/CSC_KEY_PASSWORD (or Azure Trusted Signing with a real client secret) into the CI package step.

findings.title: No crash reporting or error telemetry — you will be blind to users' bugs
findings.description: There is no crashReporter, no Sentry, no remote logging anywhere. The only error handling is `process.on('uncaughtException', err => console.error(...))` in the main process — which writes to a console no user will ever see and does not even prevent the default crash. Renderer errors, MSAL auth failures, and Power BI embed failures vanish silently. For a known-buggy app shipped to 20 remote non-technical users, you have no way to learn what is breaking.
findings.evidence: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\main\index.ts:100 (`process.on('uncaughtException', (error) => console.error(...))` — logs to invisible console, no reporter); grep for crashReporter|Sentry across src -> no matches.
findings.impact: When users hit bugs Monday, your only signal is them describing the problem over the phone. You cannot triage, reproduce, or prioritize fixes — which also undermines the whole point of being able to 'push bug-fix updates afterward' since you won't know what to fix.
findings.recommendation: Add at minimum electron's built-in crashReporter and a persisted local log file (e.g. electron-log) written to userData, so you can ask a user to send a log. Ideally add a lightweight error reporter (Sentry electron SDK or a self-hosted endpoint) gated behind user consent given the auth/PHI-adjacent nature. Replace the swallow-and-continue uncaughtException handler with one that logs to disk and surfaces a friendly error dialog.

verification.reasoning: I opened src\main\index.ts and confirmed lines 99-100: process.on('unhandledRejection') and process.on('uncaughtException') both only call console.error, with no reporter, and uncaughtException does not re-throw/quit so it does not alter default crash behavior. Grep across src and a package.json check confirm there is NO crashReporter, Sentry, bugsnag, rollbar, datadog, winston, pino, or electron-log anywhere — no remote telemetry and no persistent local log file a user could even attach to a bug report. So the central factual claim (zero crash reporting / error telemetry; operator is blind to user-side failures) is TRUE. However the claim's wording "the only error handling is console.error in main" is an overstatement: the renderer also has a React ErrorBoundary (src\renderer\components\ErrorBoundary.tsx) that catches render errors and shows a graceful fallback UI with Go Home / Try Again, plus a window 'unhandledrejection' handler in src\renderer\main.tsx:18. These improve UX but, like the main-process handlers, only console.error — they send nothing anywhere an operator can observe, so the observability conclusion holds. On severity: this is the absence of an observability feature, not a code defect that breaks functionality, loses data, or creates a security hole. The app runs correctly without it. It is a legitimate and meaningful operational gap for a known-buggy app shipped to 20 remote non-technical users (it does undercut the 'push fixes later' plan), but 'high production bug' overstates it — it is a medium-severity observability/operability gap rather than a production-breaking bug.
verification.notes: Cited code is accurate. Mitigating context the auditor missed: renderer ErrorBoundary and renderer unhandledrejection handler exist (graceful UI fallback), though neither reports remotely. No local log file exists either, so users cannot self-export diagnostics. Reasonable fix: add electron crashReporter + a lightweight remote/error sink or at minimum electron-log to disk so users can attach logs.

findings.title: CI `npm audit --audit-level=high` will fail the build and block the release entirely
findings.description: Both build jobs run `npm audit --audit-level=high` as a hard step. Ground truth: 27 vulns including 18 high. `npm audit` exits non-zero when vulns at/above the threshold exist, so this step fails, the job fails, build-windows/build-mac never produce artifacts, and the `release` job (needs: both) never runs. The tag-triggered release pipeline is therefore currently non-functional. (Separately, these high vulns — e.g. `tmp` path-traversal, msal-node's vulnerable uuid — ride into the shipped asar, but the immediate operability issue is that CI can't even cut a release.)
findings.evidence: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\.github\workflows\build.yml:28-29 and 63-64 (`npm audit --audit-level=high`); release job at :83-86 gated on `needs: [build-windows, build-mac]`.
findings.impact: If you push a `v*` tag Monday expecting CI to build and publish the installer, the pipeline fails at the audit step and produces nothing. You either get no release or you scramble to disable the gate under pressure.
findings.recommendation: Decide the policy explicitly: either (a) triage and remediate the high vulns (npm audit fix / dependency bumps; many are transitive via msal-node and may need overrides), then keep the gate; or (b) downgrade to a non-blocking `npm audit || true` / report-only step for Monday and track remediation as follow-up. Do NOT discover this failure for the first time while cutting the release tag — run the workflow via workflow_dispatch first.

verification.reasoning: Opened C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\.github\workflows\build.yml. Both build jobs run `npm audit --audit-level=high` as plain `run:` steps at lines 28-29 (build-windows) and 63-64 (build-mac), with no `continue-on-error:` to soften them. The `release` job at lines 83-86 declares `needs: [build-windows, build-mac]`, so it is skipped if either build job fails. I confirmed ground truth by running `npm audit --audit-level=high` against the present node_modules/lockfile: it printed "27 vulnerabilities (1 low, 8 moderate, 18 high)" and exited with code 1. With 18 high advisories and threshold=high, npm exits non-zero, which fails the step and the job under default fail-fast. No .npmrc exists to disable audit or redirect behavior, and package-lock.json is present so `npm ci` (the prior step) succeeds, meaning the audit step is genuinely reached and is the first failure point (before type-check and packaging). The cited examples check out: `tmp <0.2.6` path-traversal (high) and `@azure/msal-node`'s vulnerable `uuid` both appear. Result: a `v*` tag push fails both build jobs at the audit step, no installer artifacts are uploaded, and the release job is skipped. Claim is accurate on every point.
verification.notes: Build-blocking but CI/release-tooling only (not a runtime exploit path), so high rather than critical is appropriate. Fix options: gate the audit with `continue-on-error: true`, lower it to a reporting step, run `npm audit fix` to clear the non-breaking high vulns, or pin/override the vulnerable transitive deps. Note many fixes are non-breaking (`npm audit fix`) but electron/uuid(msal-node)/tar(electron-builder)/vite require `--force` breaking upgrades.

findings.title: Version management is manual and decoupled from the git tag — easy to ship mislabeled builds
findings.description: The release is triggered by pushing a `v*` tag, but package.json version (1.2.1) is what electron-builder stamps into the installer/app and what app.getVersion() returns in the UI. Nothing verifies the tag matches package.json. You can tag `v1.3.0` while package.json still says 1.2.1, producing an installer named 1.2.1 from a v1.3.0 tag. With no auto-update, the in-app version (shown on LoginScreen and SettingsPage) is the only way users and you can tell what build they're on, so a mismatch directly defeats your ability to know who has the fix.
findings.evidence: build.yml:3-6 (trigger on tag `v*`); package.json:3 (`"version": "1.2.1"`); src\renderer\components\auth\LoginScreen.tsx:20 and SettingsPage.tsx:32 (display app.getVersion()); no step in CI asserts tag == package.json version.
findings.impact: Confusing/incorrect version labels across 20 installs make it impossible to confirm who upgraded — particularly damaging once you start pushing fixes, since version is your only rollout-tracking signal.
findings.recommendation: Add a CI step that fails if the pushed tag does not equal `v$(node -p require('./package.json').version)`, or derive the build version from the tag. Adopt `npm version <bump>` (which bumps package.json and creates the matching tag atomically) as the release ritual.

findings.title: Reproducibility gaps: no Node version pin file and caret-ranged deps across two machines
findings.description: CI pins Node to '20' and uses `npm ci` against the committed package-lock.json (good, deterministic in CI). But there is no .nvmrc/.node-version, and all dependencies use caret ranges. The working tree is a 'source-of-truth folder with uncommitted WIP refactor', and a sibling 'powerbi-viewer - Copy' exists. If a release is ever built locally (package:win is a documented npm script) instead of via CI, `npm install` on a different Node/npm could resolve different transitive versions than the locked CI build, yielding a non-reproducible installer — exactly the situation where a 'works on my machine' bug ships to all 20 users.
findings.evidence: build.yml:18-19 (node-version '20', no file pin); package.json:21-46 (all `^` ranges); presence of package-lock.json (good) but no .nvmrc/.node-version (ls returned none); additional working dir 'powerbi-viewer - Copy' noted in env.
findings.impact: Local/ad-hoc builds may diverge from CI builds, and a future maintainer on a different Node version can't reproduce Monday's exact artifact — complicating any post-ship debugging.
findings.recommendation: Add an `.nvmrc` (or `engines.node`) pinning Node 20 to match CI, mandate that all release artifacts come from CI (`npm ci`) not local `package:win`, and commit/clean the WIP so the shipped commit is identifiable. Consider deleting or clearly quarantining the '- Copy' directory to avoid building from the wrong tree.

findings.title: Azure config + readable source are packed into the (default-unencrypted) asar — acceptable but worth confirming no real secret is added later
findings.description: scripts/generate-config.js writes src/main/auth/azure-config.generated.ts at build time; tsc compiles it into dist/main, which electron-builder packs into app.asar (default asar:true, no asarUnpack/extraResources overrides). asar is a trivially-extractable archive, so clientId and tenantId are recoverable from any user's install. This is acceptable today because Azure public-client clientId/tenantId are not secrets (they appear in redirect flows anyway), and the repo correctly tracks only .env.example (real .env is gitignored and not committed; the generated file is gitignored). The risk is forward-looking: this same pattern (env -> generated.ts -> asar) must never be used to bake a client secret, since asar provides no confidentiality.
findings.evidence: scripts/generate-config.js:49-51 (writes src/main/auth/azure-config.generated.ts); src\main\auth\msal-config.ts:2-7 (imports baked config); electron-builder.yml has no asarUnpack/extraResources (default asar packs dist/**, source readable); .gitignore:10-15 (.env and generated file ignored); git ls-files shows only .env.example tracked.
findings.impact: No leak today (IDs are non-confidential and nothing secret is committed). But the architecture invites a future maintainer to bake a genuine secret into the asar, which would expose it to all 20 users' machines in cleartext.
findings.recommendation: Document that azure-config.generated.ts must only ever contain non-secret public-client identifiers. If a confidential value (client secret, API key) is ever needed, do not bake it into the asar — use the auth-code-with-PKCE public-client flow (no secret) or a backend. Keep the current .env.example-only tracking discipline.

findings.title: CI builds an unsigned, un-notarized macOS DMG that wastes a job and would not install for users anyway
findings.description: The rollout target is 20 Windows users (NSIS), but CI also runs a full build-mac job producing a DMG with notarize:false and no signing identity. An unsigned, un-notarized DMG is blocked by Gatekeeper on modern macOS ('app is damaged / cannot be opened'), so it is undeliverable to non-technical mac users regardless. The job also makes the `release` step depend on build-mac succeeding, so a mac-side failure (e.g. the audit gate, or notarization config) can block the Windows release you actually need.
findings.evidence: build.yml:48-81 (build-mac job, runs on every tag); electron-builder.yml:61 (`notarize: false`), :44-55 (mac signing not configured); release job :84 (`needs: [build-windows, build-mac]`).
findings.impact: Extra CI time and a coupling where a non-target macOS failure can block the Windows release. If anyone hands the DMG to a mac user, it won't open.
findings.recommendation: For Monday, either drop the build-mac job (and the release `needs` on it) to decouple the Windows release, or keep it but make the release job not hard-depend on mac. Only invest in mac signing/notarization if mac is a real target.

summary: This codebase is shippable in the narrow sense that tsc passes, but it is structurally primed to make post-launch bug-fixing slow and dangerous. The three Power BI viewers (ReportViewer, DashboardViewer, PresentationMode) are 60-80% copy-paste of the same embed/token-refresh/fullscreen/error-overlay/auto-refresh logic, with subtle divergences — so any bug found by one of the 20 users must be diagnosed and patched 2-4 times in 2-4 slightly-different places, and fixes WILL drift. There is genuinely inconsistent behavior the user will hit immediately (the slideshow interval range is 30-300s in the settings page but 3-60s in the in-presentation panel, over the SAME setting, so a value saved in one place is impossible to represent in the other). There is heavy DEBUG console logging left in ReportViewer that ships to production. Dead code (ContentTabs, AppsList, getErrorMessage, several constants, unused type imports) is confirmed and clutters the surface. The complete absence of ESLint and any test framework means none of this is mechanically caught, and there is no regression net for the fixes that are about to be made. The highest-leverage problems for accelerating other bug fixes are: (1) extract the shared viewer logic into a hook/component, (2) add ESLint with no-unused/no-console to stop the rot, and (3) fix the slideshow range contradiction.

findings.title: Three viewers duplicate embed/token-refresh/fullscreen/error logic — fixes must be applied 2-4 times
findings.description: ReportViewer.tsx, DashboardViewer.tsx, and PresentationMode.tsx independently re-implement the same lifecycle: isLoadingRef double-load guard, isTokenExpiringSoon() (identical 2-minute window), refreshEmbedToken(), the visibilitychange token-refresh effect, the loadX() embed sequence (reset -> getEmbedToken -> build embedConfig -> powerbiService.embed -> on('loaded'/'error')), the loading/error overlay JSX, showExportStatus()/exportTimeoutRef, the auto-refresh interval effect, and handleFullScreen(). isTokenExpiringSoon, refreshEmbedToken, and the visibility effect are near-byte-identical across all three; the auto-refresh effect is identical between ReportViewer and PresentationMode. None of this is shared. The owner explicitly reports 'a lot of bugs' — every embed/token/refresh bug found by the 20 users must be located and fixed in up to 3 divergent copies, and the copies have ALREADY drifted (see token-refresh divergence finding), proving fixes are not being propagated. This is the single biggest tax on post-launch fixing.
findings.evidence: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\renderer\components\viewer\ReportViewer.tsx:117-152 (isTokenExpiringSoon/refreshEmbedToken), :154-183 (visibility + auto-refresh effects), :317-501 (loadReport), :731-751 (overlays); DashboardViewer.tsx:34-62 (same), :102-207 (loadDashboard), :320-340 (overlays); PresentationMode.tsx:69-104 (same), :355-385 (auto-refresh+visibility), :419-522 (loadReport), :543-564 (overlays). Compare isTokenExpiringSoon at ReportViewer:117, DashboardViewer:34, PresentationMode:69 — identical.
findings.impact: Each user-reported embed/token/refresh/overlay bug costs 2-4x the diagnosis+fix effort and carries high risk of fixing it in one viewer while leaving the others broken. Guarantees inconsistent behavior across the app's three main screens for 20 non-technical users who cannot work around it.
findings.recommendation: Extract a usePowerBIEmbed hook (or a shared EmbedViewer wrapper) owning loading/error/token-expiry/refresh/auto-refresh/cleanup state and a shared <EmbedOverlay> component for the loading/error JSX. Have all three viewers consume it. Do this BEFORE fixing the reported bugs so each fix lands once.

verification.reasoning: I read all three files in full. The duplication is real and accurately described. isTokenExpiringSoon is byte-identical across ReportViewer:117-121, DashboardViewer:34-38, and PresentationMode:69-73 (same 2-min window, same Number.isFinite guard). The visibilitychange token-refresh effect is near-identical (RV:154-165, DV:124-135, PM:374-385). The loadX() embed sequence (reset -> getEmbedToken -> build embedConfig -> powerbiService.embed -> on('loaded'/'error') -> isTokenExpiredError->refreshEmbedToken) is structurally duplicated in all three (RV:317-501, DV:137-207, PM:419-522). The isLoadingRef double-load guard and the loading/error overlay JSX are duplicated. The auto-refresh interval effect is functionally identical between RV:168-183 and PM:355-372 (PM only adds a console.warn). handleFullScreen exists in RV:635 and DV:249. There is no shared hook/component: the only shared code is usePowerBIService.ts (the raw service) and isTokenExpiredError from shared/utils. The claim's 'already drifted' point is genuinely supported: DashboardViewer.refreshEmbedToken (40-62) lacks the setAccessToken+refresh in-place path that RV (139-141) and PM (91-93) share — it always full-reloads via loadDashboard(); and handleFullScreen diverges (RV toggles exit/enter, DV only enters). So fixes do require 2-3 edits and the copies have measurably diverged. However, this is a maintainability/duplication liability, not a demonstrated production-breaking defect — the app works; no concrete user-facing bug is proven by the duplication itself, and some cited divergences are intentional (dashboard SDK token handling legitimately differs from reports). The 'single biggest tax' and 'inconsistent behavior for 20 users' framing is editorializing beyond what the code proves. Scope note: there is a 4th viewer (AppViewer.tsx) but it does not embed or duplicate this logic, so the 'three viewers' scope is correct.
verification.notes: Recommend extracting a shared usePowerBIEmbed hook covering: isLoadingRef guard, isTokenExpiringSoon, refreshEmbedToken, visibilitychange effect, the embed sequence, and the loading/error overlay. This is a legitimate refactor target but not an urgent production fix. Downgraded from critical to medium: it is a duplication/maintainability tax, not a runtime bug, and the divergence-driven inconsistency impact is partly intentional and partly speculative.

findings.title: Slideshow interval range is 30-300s in Settings but 3-60s in Presentation panel for the same setting
findings.description: SettingsPage's slideshow interval Slider is min={30} max={300} step={10} (SettingsPage.tsx:171-178). PresentationMode's in-presentation Settings panel Slider for the SAME slideshowInterval value is min={3} max={60} (PresentationMode.tsx:618-624). These edit the identical persisted field settings.slideshowInterval. A value saved as 300 in Settings cannot even be represented by the presentation slider (clamps/renders out of range); a value of 3 chosen in the presentation panel is below the Settings slider minimum. Compounding it: DEFAULT_SETTINGS.slideshowInterval=60 (constants.ts:21) but DEFAULT_SLIDESHOW_INTERVAL=30 (constants.ts:13) and PresentationMode's local useState default is 30 (PresentationMode.tsx:60) — three different 'defaults' for one concept. SLIDESHOW_INTERVALS=[15,30,60,120,300] (constants.ts:14) is a fourth, unused, contradictory set.
findings.evidence: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\renderer\components\settings\SettingsPage.tsx:171-178 (min 30 max 300); C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\renderer\components\viewer\PresentationMode.tsx:618-624 (min 3 max 60); src\shared\constants.ts:13,14,21 (three conflicting defaults).
findings.impact: Non-technical users will set an interval in one place and see it behave differently or appear corrupted in the other — a visible, confusing, unfixable-by-user inconsistency on a core feature. Generates support tickets and erodes trust on day one.
findings.recommendation: Pick one canonical min/max/step and one default. Define them once in constants.ts (e.g. SLIDESHOW_INTERVAL_MIN/MAX/STEP/DEFAULT) and import in both sliders and DEFAULT_SETTINGS. Delete the unused SLIDESHOW_INTERVALS and DEFAULT_SLIDESHOW_INTERVAL or reconcile them.

verification.reasoning:
I opened all three cited files and confirmed the factual claims, then checked the data flow the claim glosses over.

VERIFIED FACTS:
- SettingsPage.tsx:171-178 — Slider min={30} max={300} step={10}, bound to settings.slideshowInterval, persisted via handleSlideshowIntervalChange -> updateSettings({slideshowInterval: value}) (lines 41-43). Confirmed.
- PresentationMode.tsx:618-624 — Slider min={3} max={60}, bound to local intervalSeconds. Confirmed.
- Both edit the SAME concept: PresentationMode loads settings.slideshowInterval into intervalSeconds on mount (line 112: setIntervalSeconds(response.data.slideshowInterval)). So the ranges genuinely contradict for one persisted field.
- constants.ts: DEFAULT_SLIDESHOW_INTERVAL=30 (line 13), SLIDESHOW_INTERVALS=[15,30,60,120,300] (line 14), DEFAULT_SETTINGS.slideshowInterval=60 (line 20), and PresentationMode useState(30) (line 60). Confirmed all four numbers exist.

WHERE THE CLAIM OVERSTATES:
1. "appears corrupted / unfixable-by-user" is wrong. The slideshow timer uses intervalSeconds directly (line 321: setInterval(..., intervalSeconds * 1000)). A loaded value of 300 is NOT clamped on read — the timer fires correctly at 300s. The Fluent v9 Slider with value=300, min=3, max=60 simply pins the thumb visually at the far right while the label still reads "300s" (line 625). Misleading visual, not data corruption.
2. The presentation panel NEVER persists its changes: I grepped the whole file — there is no settings.update/settings.set call, only settings.get (line 110). So setIntervalSeconds is local-only. This both limits blast radius (nothing gets written as an out-of-range corrupt value) AND is arguably a separate bug (panel changes are lost on relaunch), but it means there is no cross-contamination of the persisted store.
3. "Three different defaults" is mostly code smell: useState(30) is overwritten on mount by the persisted value (line 112), and DEFAULT_SLIDESHOW_INTERVAL + SLIDESHOW_INTERVALS are DEAD CODE — grep across src found zero imports/usages anywhere. The only default that actually governs runtime behavior is DEFAULT_SETTINGS.slideshowInterval=60.

REAL RESIDUAL BUG: The range mismatch is genuine and user-facing. If a user sets 300 in Settings then opens the in-presentation panel, the slider sits pinned at max; if they nudge it, it silently snaps to <=60, shrinking their interval for that session. And a value below 30 chosen in the panel can never be shown by the Settings slider. This is a real UX inconsistency worth fixing (unify the ranges), but it causes no crash, no persisted corruption, and the timer always honors the true value. That is a medium-severity polish/consistency defect, not a high-severity production bug.
verification.notes: Fix is straightforward: make both sliders share one range (and ideally a single MIN/MAX/STEP constant), and remove the dead DEFAULT_SLIDESHOW_INTERVAL/SLIDESHOW_INTERVALS. Note separately that the presentation panel never persists its interval change (no settings.update) — a related but distinct issue.

findings.title: ReportViewer ships 10 DEBUG console.log handlers wired to every Power BI event
findings.description: ReportViewer registers chatty console.log('[ReportViewer] DEBUG - ...') handlers on pageChanged, dataSelected, rendered, commandTriggered, swipeStart, swipeEnd, buttonClicked, filtersApplied, visualClicked, visualRendered. dataSelected/visualRendered/visualClicked fire on essentially every interaction and render, flooding the console continuously while a report is open. These are unconditional (not gated on isDev/NODE_ENV) and ship to all 20 users. Beyond noise, dataSelected and visualClicked detail payloads can contain report data values — logging them is a minor data-exposure smell in addition to the performance/noise cost. ReportViewer alone accounts for 20 of the 63 console.* calls in src and all 10 DEBUG markers in the codebase.
findings.evidence: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\renderer\components\viewer\ReportViewer.tsx:442-494 (ten DEBUG handlers); grep: 'DEBUG' = 10 occurrences, all in ReportViewer; console.* = 20 in ReportViewer.
findings.impact: Continuous console spam degrades DevTools usefulness when diagnosing the real reported bugs (the very thing the team will be doing post-launch), adds per-event handler overhead during interaction, and leaks selection/visual data into logs.
findings.recommendation: Delete the DEBUG event handlers entirely (they serve no product function), or gate all of them behind a single isDev guard. Add ESLint no-console (allow warn/error) to prevent reintroduction.

verification.reasoning: Opened ReportViewer.tsx and confirmed all 10 DEBUG handlers exist verbatim at lines 442-494 (pageChanged, dataSelected, rendered, commandTriggered, swipeStart, swipeEnd, buttonClicked, filtersApplied, visualClicked, visualRendered), each unconditionally calling console.log('[ReportViewer] DEBUG - ...'). Grep confirms the cited counts exactly: 10 DEBUG occurrences (all in this file), 20 console.* in this file. Grep for isDev/NODE_ENV/import.meta.env/process.env in the file returns zero matches, so there is no dev gating. Checked vite.config.ts: it has no esbuild.drop, terser drop_console, or pure_funcs, and Vite's default esbuild minify does NOT strip console.* — so these logs genuinely ship and run in the production renderer bundle. dataSelected and visualClicked log event?.detail, which for Power BI carries selected data-point values/identities, so the minor data-in-logs smell is real. So the factual core of the claim is accurate. However the severity is overstated: this is console noise plus a minor logging-hygiene smell, not a production bug — nothing breaks, crashes, corrupts data, or misbehaves functionally. The handler bodies are single console.log calls (negligible per-event CPU), and on an Electron desktop app the DevTools console is not surfaced to the 20 end users, so the 'leaks data' and 'performance' angles are low-impact. The 'continuous flooding' framing is also imprecise: these are event-driven (per render / per interaction), not a steady idle stream. Genuine defect, wrong altitude — downgrade from high to low.
verification.notes: Cited file: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\renderer\components\viewer\ReportViewer.tsx (handlers 442-494). Build config: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\vite.config.ts (no console stripping). Cheap real fix: delete the 10 DEBUG handlers or gate them behind import.meta.env.DEV, and/or add esbuild.drop:['console'] for production builds.

findings.title: No ESLint and no test framework — no mechanical net under the bug-fixing about to happen
findings.description: There is no ESLint config, no lint script, and no test framework/files/CI gate (confirmed ground truth). tsconfig also omits noUnusedLocals/noUnusedParameters (verified: grep for noUnused returns nothing), which is precisely why tsc passes despite multiple dead imports. The result: dead code, leftover console.logs, unused vars, and accidental behavior divergences are all invisible to tooling, and there is zero regression protection for fixes. For an app already described as buggy and about to receive a round of fixes, the absence of any automated check means each fix can silently break another path (exactly the cross-viewer drift risk above).
findings.evidence: Ground truth: no ESLint/lint script/test framework. Verified: grep -i 'noUnused' tsconfig*.json -> no matches, so unused imports like getErrorMessage (ReportViewer.tsx:13, DashboardViewer.tsx:13, PresentationMode.tsx:14) and unused type imports (EmbedToken, Report, App, AppSettings, Dashboard) pass tsc.
findings.impact: Every post-launch fix is unguarded. Quality issues that linters catch in seconds instead surface as user-reported bugs from the 20 users. Reviewers cannot rely on a green gate, slowing every change.
findings.recommendation: Add ESLint (typescript-eslint + react-hooks) with no-unused-vars, no-console (warn/error allowed), and react-hooks/exhaustive-deps; add noUnusedLocals/noUnusedParameters to tsconfig; add at least a smoke test for the shared embed hook and utils. Wire a lint+typecheck script as a pre-distribution gate.

verification.reasoning:
Verified against disk. CONFIRMED parts: (1) No ESLint anywhere - no .eslintrc*, no eslint.config.*, no eslint dependency, no lint script in package.json. (2) No test framework/files - grep for jest|vitest|mocha|playwright|@testing-library returns NONE, and no *.test.*/*.spec.* files exist. (3) All three tsconfigs (tsconfig.json, tsconfig.main.json, tsconfig.renderer.json) lack noUnusedLocals/noUnusedParameters - grep for noUnused returns nothing. (4) The getErrorMessage dead-import example is correct: it appears exactly once (the import line on line 13) in ReportViewer.tsx, DashboardViewer.tsx, and PresentationMode.tsx with zero usages, and tsc passes it precisely because noUnusedLocals is off.

INACCURATE parts that warrant downgrade: (a) The claim asserts 'no CI gate' but C:/Users/Brendan Cameron/Desktop/powerbi-viewer/.github/workflows/build.yml DOES exist and runs `npm audit --audit-level=high` plus `tsc --noEmit` type-checking on both renderer and main configs. The real weakness is narrower than stated: that workflow triggers only on `v*` tag push and workflow_dispatch (not on PRs/branch pushes), and runs no lint/test - so there is no per-change gate, but the flat 'no CI' is wrong. (b) The claim's list of 'unused type imports (EmbedToken, Report, App, AppSettings, Dashboard)' overreaches: EmbedToken appears 6 times in ReportViewer.tsx and is clearly used. Only the getErrorMessage examples are genuinely dead.

Most importantly, this is a tooling/process-hygiene gap, not a production runtime defect - no user-facing code path is broken by the absence of a linter, and a type-check CI gate does exist. That does not meet the bar for a CRITICAL/HIGH production bug.
verification.notes: Relevant files: C:/Users/Brendan Cameron/Desktop/powerbi-viewer/tsconfig.json, tsconfig.main.json, tsconfig.renderer.json (no noUnused flags); package.json (no lint/test scripts or deps); .github/workflows/build.yml (CI exists: npm audit + tsc type-check, tag-triggered only, no lint/test); src/renderer/components/viewer/{ReportViewer,DashboardViewer,PresentationMode}.tsx line 13 (genuinely dead getErrorMessage import). Recommended fix is cheap and worthwhile: enable noUnusedLocals/noUnusedParameters and add an eslint config + lint step to CI - but this is hygiene, not a runtime bug.

findings.title: Confirmed dead code: ContentTabs, AppsList, getErrorMessage, and several constants
findings.description: Confirmed unused via full-tree grep: (1) ContentTabs.tsx — exported but never imported anywhere (only self-references). (2) AppsList.tsx — never imported; the real apps UI lives in AppsPage.tsx, so AppsList is an orphaned earlier implementation. (3) getErrorMessage (utils.ts:5) is imported into all three viewers but never called — only isTokenExpiredError is used; the import is dead in every viewer. (4) Constants DEFAULT_SLIDESHOW_INTERVAL, SLIDESHOW_INTERVALS, and POWERBI_EMBED_BASE are never referenced. (5) Several type-only imports in the viewers (EmbedToken, Report, App, AppSettings, Dashboard) are unused. This dead surface invites confusion ('which apps list is real?') during debugging and is the kind of thing that misleads a fixer into editing the wrong file.
findings.evidence: grep ContentTabs|AppsList across src -> only definitions/self-exports, no import sites. utils.ts:5 getErrorMessage imported at ReportViewer.tsx:13 / DashboardViewer.tsx:13 / PresentationMode.tsx:14 but no call sites. constants.ts:13,14,10 unreferenced (DEFAULT_SLIDESHOW_INTERVAL/SLIDESHOW_INTERVALS/POWERBI_EMBED_BASE).
findings.impact: Wastes reader time and creates 'which is the live code path' ambiguity during the upcoming bug-fixing — a real slowdown. AppsList in particular looks like a plausible-but-wrong place to fix an apps bug.
findings.recommendation: Delete ContentTabs.tsx, AppsList.tsx, getErrorMessage (and its imports), and the unused constants/type imports. Once ESLint no-unused is on, the rest surface automatically.

findings.title: Token-refresh recovery diverges between viewers — same concept, three behaviors (drift evidence)
findings.description: The copy-pasted refreshEmbedToken has already drifted, proving duplication causes inconsistency. ReportViewer and PresentationMode call reportRef.current.setAccessToken(token) then refresh() in place (ReportViewer.tsx:139-145, PresentationMode.tsx:91-97), preserving the user's current page/state. DashboardViewer instead throws away the embed and fully reloads via loadDashboard() (DashboardViewer.tsx:53-55) — heavier and visually disruptive. Separately, ReportViewer's report.on('error') deliberately does NOT surface non-token errors to the UI (comment: 'let Power BI handle errors internally'), while DashboardViewer and PresentationMode DO set a blocking error overlay on the same error event (DashboardViewer.tsx:190, PresentationMode.tsx:512). So an identical underlying failure shows nothing in one viewer and a full-screen 'Try again' in another.
findings.evidence: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\renderer\components\viewer\ReportViewer.tsx:139-145 & :428-439; DashboardViewer.tsx:53-55 & :183-192; PresentationMode.tsx:91-97 & :505-514.
findings.impact: Users experience the same error/refresh event differently depending on which screen they're on, and a fix to the recovery behavior in one viewer won't help the others. Makes 'why does it behave differently on dashboards' tickets hard to reason about.
findings.recommendation: Centralize token-refresh and error-handling policy in the shared embed hook so all three viewers recover identically and surface errors with one agreed policy.

findings.title: Presentation/search dialogs lack focus management; viewers lack keyboard access for non-power-users
findings.description: PresentationMode is a full-screen fixed overlay (z-50) with no focus trap — focus is never moved into the overlay and the embedded iframe can hold focus, so keyboard-only users may not be able to reach the Play/Exit/nav controls; control affordances rely on global window keydown rather than focusable buttons receiving focus. The slide indicator buttons have aria-labels (good) but the play/prev/next icon buttons rely on title only. ReportViewer's fullscreen page navigation is arrow-key only with an aggressive 200ms setInterval that force-refocuses the container (ReportViewer.tsx:289-302) — a brittle focus-stealing hack that fights the iframe and will be a maintenance hazard. SearchDialog focuses the input via a 100ms setTimeout (a magic-timeout smell) and implements its own roving selection but has no explicit focus return to the trigger on close. None of these block launch for sighted mouse users, but they reflect missing a11y basics that also make the focus logic fragile and hard to fix.
findings.evidence: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\renderer\components\viewer\PresentationMode.tsx:540-685 (no focus trap, controls via window keydown :283-314); ReportViewer.tsx:289-315 (200ms focus-reclaim interval); SearchDialog.tsx:50-57 (100ms focus setTimeout).
findings.impact: Keyboard-only or assistive-tech users among the 20 may be unable to operate presentation mode; more pressingly for maintenance, the timer-based focus-stealing in ReportViewer is exactly the kind of fragile code that breaks under future changes and is hard to debug.
findings.recommendation: Move focus into the presentation overlay on mount and trap it; give icon-only controls aria-labels; replace ReportViewer's 200ms refocus interval with a focus/blur-driven approach. Run the accessibility-audit skill on the viewer components.

findings.title: Magic numbers and hardcoded timeouts scattered across viewers
findings.description: Hardcoded literals are duplicated inline with no named constants: 2*60*1000 token-expiry window (repeated in all three isTokenExpiringSoon), 4000ms export-status auto-dismiss (ReportViewer:114, DashboardViewer:99), 5000ms fullscreen hint (ReportViewer:217), 3000ms controls-hide (PresentationMode:400), 200ms focus-reclaim (ReportViewer:301), 100ms input focus (SearchDialog:53), 200ms search debounce (SearchDialog:43), 500ms export pane-hide wait (ReportViewer:591), and export insets {right:40,bottom:40} (ReportViewer:603). The PDF-export microns math uses a hardcoded 96 DPI assumption (index.ts:469-470) that will mis-size exports on HiDPI displays. None are individually severe but collectively they make timing/behavior tuning a scavenger hunt.
findings.evidence: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\renderer\components\viewer\ReportViewer.tsx:114,120,217,301,591,603; DashboardViewer.tsx:37,99; PresentationMode.tsx:72,400; SearchDialog.tsx:43,53; main\index.ts:469-470 (hardcoded 96 DPI).
findings.impact: Tuning any timing-related bug (e.g. 'hint disappears too fast', 'export wrong size on my 4K monitor') means hunting magic numbers across files instead of editing one named constant.
findings.recommendation: Hoist these into named constants (TOKEN_EXPIRY_WINDOW_MS, EXPORT_STATUS_TIMEOUT_MS, etc.), ideally in constants.ts, and derive export DPI from window.devicePixelRatio / screen scale rather than a fixed 96.

findings.title: 40+ IPC channels are duplicated magic strings across preload and main with untyped handler returns
findings.description: Every IPC channel name (e.g. 'content:get-embed-token') is a hand-written string literal duplicated between preload/index.ts (ipcRenderer.invoke) and main/index.ts (ipcMain.handle) — ~40 pairs with no shared channel constant/enum, so a typo on one side produces a silently-hanging invoke that tsc cannot catch. The renderer-facing surface IS well-typed via the ElectronAPI interface (good), but the ipcMain.handle implementations have no shared return-type contract with ElectronAPI: handlers return ad-hoc {success:false,error:{...}} objects and raw service results, and the INVALID_INPUT/CANCELLED/NO_PATH error codes are stringly-typed and duplicated. The renderer trusts the types but nothing verifies the main side actually conforms.
findings.evidence: C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\preload\index.ts:8-91 vs C:\Users\Brendan Cameron\Desktop\powerbi-viewer\src\main\index.ts:174-614 — paired literal channel strings, no shared enum; ipcMain.handle return shapes (e.g. index.ts:242, :303, :341) untyped against IPCResponse.
findings.impact: A channel-name typo or a handler returning the wrong shape is invisible to the compiler and surfaces only as a runtime hang or a renderer crash for users — slow to diagnose because the type system gives false confidence on the renderer side.
findings.recommendation: Define channel names as a shared const map and reference it in both preload and main; type ipcMain.handle returns against IPCResponse<T> (a typed registerHandler<K> helper keyed off ElectronAPI). Define error codes as a union/enum.
